topics.name: Measurement of Delta U and H: Calorimetry
Enthalpy Change, Delta_r H of A Reaction – Reaction Enthalpy
Enthalpies for Different Types of Reactions

questions.question: Thermodynamics is the branch of physical science concerned with
questions.question_raw: Thermodynamics is the branch of physical science concerned with

options.text: kinetic energy and its transformations to and from other forms of energy.
potential and its transformations to and from other forms of energy.
heat and its transformations to and from other forms of energy.
mass and its transformations to and from other forms of energy.

questions.explanation: Thermodynamics deals with heat and its transformation from one form to another. The branch of physical science that deals with the relations between heat and other forms of energy (such as mechanical, electrical, or chemical energy) and by extension of the relationships between all forms of energy.

questions.question: In a closed system, which of the following take place?
questions.question_raw: In a closed system, which of the following take place?

options.text: The boundaries prevent the flow of matter into or out of it.
The boundaries prevent the flow of matter out of it but not vice versa.
The boundaries permit the flow of matter into it but not vice versa.
The boundaries permit the flow of matter into or out of it.

questions.explanation: In a closed system, there is no flow of matter from system to surrounding or vice versa. For example, a certain quantity of fluid bounded within a closed cylinder constitutes a closed system.

questions.question: In an open system, which of the following take place?
questions.question_raw: In an open system, which of the following take place?

options.text: The boundaries prevent the flow of energy into it.
The boundaries prevent the flow of matter into or out of it.
The boundaries prevent the flow of matter but not of energy.
The boundaries permit the flow of matter into or out of it.

questions.explanation: In an open system, there is a flow of matter and energy in and out of the system or vice versa. For example, an air compressor in which the air enters at low pressure and exit at high pressure and there is energy interaction with the surrounding.

questions.question: First law of thermodynamics is a restatement of:
questions.question_raw: First law of thermodynamics is a restatement of:
questions.explanation:
It is a restatement of the law of conservation of energy.
It states that Thermodynamics is the branch of physical science that deals with the relations between heat and other forms of energy (such as mechanical, electrical, or chemical energy) and by extension, of the relationships between all forms of energy.

questions.question: According to the first law of thermodynamics $\Delta$U = q + w, here what is a sign of q and w?
questions.question_raw: According to the first law of thermodynamics {tex}\Delta{/tex}U = q + w, here what is a sign of q and w?

options.text: q is negative if heat is transferred from the system and w is negative if work is done by the system.
q is negative if heat is transferred into the system and w is negative if work is done on the system.
q is positive if heat is transferred into the system and w is positive if work is done on the system.
q is positive if heat is transferred into the system and w is positive if work is done by the system.

questions.explanation: The first law is simply the conservation of energy equation. q is positive if heat is added to the system, and negative if heat is removed; w is positive if work is done on the system, and negative if work is done by the system.

questions.question: If Vf is the final volume and Vi is the initial volume and ${{\text{p}}_{{\text{ex}}}}$ external pressure then the work is done can be calculated by?
questions.question_raw: If Vf is the final volume and Vi is the initial volume and {tex}{{\text{p}}_{{\text{ex}}}}{/tex} external pressure then the work is done can be calculated by?
questions.explanation:
{tex} - \mathop \smallint \limits_{{V_i}}^{{V_f}} {p_{ex}}dV{/tex} = w
It is equal to the integration of change in volume with respect to the initial and final volume.

questions.question: For the isothermal reversible free expansion of an ideal gas from Vi, the initial volume to Vf, the final volume of the work done on the gas is:
questions.question_raw: For the isothermal reversible free expansion of an ideal gas from Vi, the initial volume to Vf, the final volume of the work done on the gas is:
questions.explanation:
w = {tex}- nRTln\left( {\frac{{{V_f}}}{{{V_i}}}} \right){/tex}
This is work done by the system in an isothermal process where
w is the work done
n is the number of moles
R is the universal gas constant 
T is the temperature in Kelvin scale

questions.question: The correct equation for the change in enthalpy is:
questions.question_raw: The correct equation for the change in enthalpy is:
questions.explanation: Enthalpy is a thermodynamic quantity equivalent to the total heat content of a system. It is equal to the internal energy of the system plus the product of pressure and volume.

questions.question: The molar specific heat capacity of a substance is the quantity of heat needed to raise the temperature of one
questions.question_raw: The molar specific heat capacity of a substance is the quantity of heat needed to raise the temperature of one

options.text: mole by one degree celsius or one kelvin.

questions.explanation: Molar specific heat capacity can be defined as the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of 1 mole of a substance by 1o C.

questions.question: Which one among the following is the correct option for right relationship between Cp and Cv for one mole of ideal gas?
questions.question_raw: Which one among the following is the correct option for right relationship between Cp and Cv for one mole of ideal gas?
questions.explanation:
For a mole of an ideal gas, The difference between Cp and Cv is always constant i.e. equals to the gas constant.
Therefore, for one mole of an ideal gas, Cp - CV = R.

questions.question: Calorimetry is an experimental technique or process of:
questions.question_raw: Calorimetry is an experimental technique or process of:

options.text: measuring the amount of chemicals consumed.
measuring the energy changes associated with chemical or physical processes.
measuring the maximum pressure developed in a reaction.
measuring the amount of work done in a reaction.

questions.explanation: Calorimetry is an experimental technique or process of measuring the heat of chemical reactions or physical changes as well as heat capacity.

questions.question: An exothermic process:
questions.question_raw: An exothermic process:

options.text: results in a decrease in the enthalpy of the system.
results in a increase in the enthalpy of the system.
results in a increase in the internal energy of the system.
results in a decrease in the temperature of the system.

questions.explanation: Exothermic process results in a decrease in enthalpy of the system hence for exothermic reaction {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -ve.

questions.question: The enthalpy change of a chemical reaction ${\Delta _{\text{r}}}{\text{H}}$ equals (ai and bi are stoichiometric coefficients)?
questions.question_raw: The enthalpy change of a chemical reaction {tex}{\Delta _{\text{r}}}{\text{H}}{/tex} equals (ai and bi are stoichiometric coefficients)?
questions.explanation: The enthalpy of reaction ({tex}{\Delta _r}H{/tex}) = (Sum of enthalpies of products)- (sum of enthalpies of reactants) =  {tex}\sum {{a_i}{H_{(product)}}} - \sum {{b_i}{H_{(reactant)}}} {/tex}

questions.question: Standard enthalpy of fusion or molar enthalpy of fusion is:
questions.question_raw: Standard enthalpy of fusion or molar enthalpy of fusion is:

options.text: the enthalpy change that accompanies vaporization of one kg of a solid substance in the standard state.
the enthalpy change that accompanies vaporization of one mole of a solid substance in the standard state.
the enthalpy change that accompanies melting of one mole of a solid substance in the standard state.
the enthalpy change that accompanies melting of one kg of a solid substance in the standard state.

questions.explanation: Molar enthalpy of fusion (always increases i.e. {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = positive) is the amount of energy needed to change completely one mole of a substance from the solid phase to the liquid phase in the standard state.

questions.question: Standard Molar Enthalpy of formation is:
questions.question_raw: Standard Molar Enthalpy of formation is:

options.text: the standard enthalpy change for the formation of one kg of a compound from its elements in their most stable states of aggregation.
the standard enthalpy change for the formation of one mole of a compound from its elements in at a pressure of 2 bar and 25o C.
the standard enthalpy change for the formation of one mole of a compound from its elements in their most stable states of aggregation.
the standard enthalpy change for the formation of one mole of a compound from its elements in at a pressure of 10 bar and 30°C.

questions.explanation: The standard enthalpy change for the formation of one mole of a compound from its elements in their most stable states of aggregation (reference states) is called standard molar enthalpy of formation.

questions.question: According to Hess’s Law, if a reaction takes place in several steps then its standard reaction enthalpy is:
questions.question_raw: According to Hess’s Law, if a reaction takes place in several steps then its standard reaction enthalpy is:

options.text: the sum of the standard enthalpies of the intermediate reactions into which the overall reaction may be divided at 2 bar.
the sum of the standard enthalpies of the intermediate reactions into which the overall reaction may be divided at 35°C.
the sum of the standard enthalpies of the intermediate reactions into which the overall reaction may be divided at the same temperature.
the difference of the standard enthalpies of the intermediate reactions into which the overall reaction may be divided at the same temperature.

questions.explanation: Hess’s Law states that if a reaction takes place in several steps then its standard reaction enthalpy is the sum of the standard enthalpies of the intermediate reactions into which the overall reaction may be divided at the same temperature.

questions.question: Enthalpy of atomization is enthalpy change on breaking:
questions.question_raw: Enthalpy of atomization is enthalpy change on breaking:

options.text: one kg of bonds completely to obtain atoms in the liquid phase.
one mole of bonds completely to obtain atoms in the gas phase.
one kg of bonds completely to obtain atoms in the gas phase.
one mole of bonds completely to obtain atoms in the liquid phase.

questions.explanation: Enthalpy of atomization is the enthalpy change that takes place on breaking one mole of bonds completely of a compound or an element, to obtain atoms in the gas phase.

questions.question: In the case of compounds such as CH3CH2Cl, CH3NO2, etc. which of the following term is used as bond enthalpy?
questions.question_raw: In the case of compounds such as CH3CH2Cl, CH3NO2, etc. which of the following term is used as bond enthalpy?
questions.explanation: For polyatomic molecules, the term means bond enthalpy is used for the enthalpy changes associated with chemical bonds.

questions.question: Enthalpy of the solution of a substance is the enthalpy change:
questions.question_raw: Enthalpy of the solution of a substance is the enthalpy change:

options.text: when one mole of it dissolves in a specified amount of solvent.
when one mole of it reacts with a specified amount of solvent.
when one kg of it dissolves in a specified amount of solvent.
when one kg of it reacts with a specified amount of solvent.

questions.explanation: Enthalpy of the solution of a substance is the enthalpy change when one mole of it dissolves in a specified amount of solvent.

questions.question: The lattice enthalpy of an ionic compound is the enthalpy change which occurs when
questions.question_raw: The lattice enthalpy of an ionic compound is the enthalpy change which occurs when

options.text: one kg of an ionic compound dissociates into its ions in a liquid state.
one mole of an ionic compound dissociates into its ions in a gaseous state.
one mole of an ionic compound dissociates into its ions in a liquid state.
one gm of an ionic compound dissociates into its ions in a gaseous state.

questions.explanation: The lattice enthalpy of an ionic compound is the enthalpy change which occurs when one mole of an ionic compound dissociates into its ions in a gaseous state.

questions.question: Spontaneity in the context of chemical thermodynamics means
questions.question_raw: Spontaneity in the context of chemical thermodynamics means

options.text: having the potential to proceed fast.
having the potential to proceed instantaneously.
having the potential to proceed with the assistance of an external agency.
having the potential to proceed without the assistance of an external agency.

questions.explanation: Spontaneity means a reaction occurring on its own without any help of an external agency.

questions.question: Entropy is a state function and measures
questions.question_raw: Entropy is a state function and measures

options.text: the internal energy of the system.
the degree of randomness or disorder in the system.
the degree of regularity or order in the system.

questions.explanation: Entropy is a state function and it measures the degree of randomness or disorder of a system. It is denoted by 'S'.

questions.question: Change in entropy of a reversible process can be calculated by:
questions.question_raw: Change in entropy of a reversible process can be calculated by:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta {S_{sys}} = \frac{{{q_{sys,rev}}}}{T}{/tex}. The equation can be used to calculate the change in entropy of a system of a reversible process as entropy is a state property.
Where, qsys, rev = Heat supplied to a system at temperature T.

questions.question: If change in Gibbs energy $\Delta {\text{G}}$ is negative (< 0) at constant pressure and temperature?
questions.question_raw: If change in Gibbs energy {tex}\Delta {\text{G}}{/tex} is negative (< 0) at constant pressure and temperature?

options.text: The process will never take place
The process will rarely take place

questions.explanation: {tex}\Delta {\text{G}}{/tex} gives criteria for the spontaneous and non-spontaneous process at constant temperature and pressure. {tex}\Delta {\text{G}}{/tex} < 0 (negative) means reaction is spontaneous and {tex}\Delta {\text{G}}{/tex} > 0 (positive) means reaction is non spontaneous.

questions.question: Standard Gibbs energy change ${\Delta _{\text{r}}}{{\text{G}}^0}$ is related to the equilibrium constant of the reaction as follows:
questions.question_raw: Standard Gibbs energy change {tex}{\Delta _{\text{r}}}{{\text{G}}^0}{/tex} is related to the equilibrium constant of the reaction as follows:
questions.explanation:
{tex}{\Delta _{\text{r}}}{{\text{G}}^0}{/tex} = -RTln K  
where {tex}{\Delta _{\text{r}}}{{\text{G}}^0}{/tex} is standard Gibbs energy change, K is the equilibrium constant, R is the universal gas constant and T is the temperature in kelvin scale.

questions.question: A thermodynamic state function is a physical quantity whose value:
questions.question_raw: A thermodynamic state function is a physical quantity whose value:
questions.explanation: State function is one which is only dependent on the initial and final state of the system and is independent of the path by which that change has occurred.

questions.question: For an adiabatic change:
questions.question_raw: For an adiabatic change:
questions.explanation: An adiabatic process would not allow the exchange of heat between the system and surroundings, hence q = 0.

questions.question: By convention, standard enthalpy for the formation of an element in reference state is:
questions.question_raw: By convention, standard enthalpy for the formation of an element in reference state is:
questions.explanation: By convention, enthalpies of formation of all elements in their most stable state of aggregation (reference state) at 25oC and 1atm are taken as zero.

questions.question: $\Delta {{\text{U}}^\ominus} $of combustion of methane is -X kJ ${\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}$. The value of $\Delta {{\text{H}}^\ominus}$ is
questions.question_raw: {tex}\Delta {{\text{U}}^\ominus} {/tex}of combustion of methane is -X kJ {tex}{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}{/tex}. The value of {tex}\Delta {{\text{H}}^\ominus}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
CH4 (g) + 2O2 (g)  → CO2 (g) + 2H2O (l)
 We know, {tex}\Delta {H^\ominus} = {\text{ }}\Delta {U^\ominus} + {\text{ }}\Delta {n_g}{/tex} RT
Δng is negative as product water in combustion reaction is in liquid state.
{tex}\Delta {H^\ominus} = {\text{ }}-X-{\text{ }}\Delta {n_g}RT {/tex}
This implies {tex}\Delta H \ominus{/tex} will be more negative than {tex}\Delta U \ominus{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\Delta H \ominus{/tex} < {tex}\Delta U \ominus{/tex}

questions.question: The enthalpy of combustion of methane, graphite and dihydrogen at 298 K are, -890.3 kJ mol-1, -393.5 kJ mol-1 and -285.8 kJ mol-1 respectively. Enthalpy of formation of CH4 (g) will be
questions.question_raw: The enthalpy of combustion of methane, graphite and dihydrogen at 298 K are, -890.3 kJ mol-1, -393.5 kJ mol-1 and -285.8 kJ mol-1 respectively. Enthalpy of formation of CH4 (g) will be
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{CH}_{4}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}{/tex} {tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}=-890.3 \mathrm{KJ} / \mathrm{mol}{/tex} ...(1)
{tex}\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}{/tex} {tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}_{2}=-393.5 \mathrm{KJ} / \mathrm{mol}{/tex} ...(2)
{tex}\mathrm{H}_{2}+0.5 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}{/tex} {tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}_{3}=-285.8 \mathrm{KJ} / \mathrm{mol}{/tex} ...(3)
{tex}\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{4}{/tex} {tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}=\Delta \mathrm{H}_{2}+2\left(\Delta \mathrm{H}_{3}\right)-\Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}{/tex}
{tex}\triangle{/tex}H = -393.5 +2(-285.8) - (-890.3)
= -74.8 kJ/mol

questions.question: A reaction, A + B $\to$ C + D + q is found to have a positive entropy change. The reaction will be:
questions.question_raw: A reaction, A + B {tex}\to{/tex} C + D + q  is found to have a positive entropy change. The reaction will be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H -T {tex}\Delta{/tex}S
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S is positive and {tex}\Delta{/tex}H is negative as heat is liberated in the reaction.
So {tex}\Delta{/tex}G is negative hence reaction will be spontaneous at all temperatures.

questions.question: In a process, 701 J of heat is absorbed by a system and 394 J of work is done by the system. What is the change in internal energy for the process?
questions.question_raw: In a process, 701 J of heat is absorbed by a system and 394 J of work is done by the system. What is the change in internal energy for the process?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta {\text{U}}{/tex} = q + w (1st law of thermodynamics)
q = +701J (heat is absorbed by system)
w = -394 J (work is done by system)
{tex}\Delta {\text{U}}{/tex} = 701- 394
= 307J

questions.question:
The reaction of cyanamide, NH2CN (s), with dioxygen was carried out in a bomb calorimeter, and $\Delta$U was found to be 742.7 kJ mol-1 at 298 K. Calculate enthalpy change for the reaction at 298 K.
NH2CN(s) + $\frac{3}{2}$O2(g) + N2(g) +CO2(g) + H2O(l)?
questions.question_raw:
The reaction of cyanamide, NH2CN (s), with dioxygen was carried out in a bomb calorimeter, and {tex}\Delta{/tex}U was found to be 742.7 kJ mol-1 at 298 K. Calculate enthalpy change for the reaction at 298 K.
NH2CN(s) + {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}O2(g) + N2(g) +CO2(g) + H2O(l)?
questions.explanation:
Here, {tex}\Delta{/tex}ng = 2 - ({tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H° = {tex}\Delta{/tex}U° + {tex}\Delta{/tex}ngRT
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H° = - 742.7 kJ/mol + (0.5 mol {tex}\times{/tex} 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3kJ/mol {tex}\times{/tex} 298K)
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H° = ( - 742.7 kJ + 1238.786 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3kJ) mol-1
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H° =  - 741.46 kJ mol-1

questions.question: Calculate the number of kJ of heat necessary to raise the temperature of 60.0 g of aluminium from 35o C to 55o C. [Molar heat capacity of Al is 24 J mol-1K-1 ]
questions.question_raw: Calculate the number of kJ of heat necessary to raise the temperature of 60.0 g of aluminium from 35o C to 55o C. [Molar heat capacity of Al is 24 J mol-1K-1 ]
questions.explanation:
Given, Mass of Al = 60.0g 
Molar mass = 27 g/mol
Molar heat capacity, C = 24 J mol-1 K-1. {tex}\Delta{/tex}T = 55oC - 35oC = 20oC or 20 K.
Heat, q = nC {tex}\Delta{/tex}T = {tex}{\frac{{60}}{{27}}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 1066.66 J = 1.067 kJ, where n = 60.0/27 mole

questions.question:
Calculate the enthalpy change on freezing of 1.0 mol of water at 10.0°C to the ice at -10.0o C. ${\Delta _{{\text{fus}}}}$H = 6.03 kJ ${\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}$at 0°C.
 CP[H2O(l)] = 75.3 Jmol-1K-1
 CP[H2O(s)] = 36.8 Jmol-1K-1
questions.question_raw:
Calculate the enthalpy change on freezing of 1.0 mol of water at 10.0°C to the ice at -10.0o C. {tex}{\Delta _{{\text{fus}}}}{/tex}H = 6.03 kJ {tex}{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}{/tex}at 0°C.

  1. CP[H2O(l)] = 75.3 Jmol-1K-1
  2. CP[H2O(s)] = 36.8 Jmol-1K-1
questions.explanation:
Water (10.0oC) {tex} \to {/tex} ice (-10.0oC) {tex}\Delta H = ?{/tex}
The enthalpy change for the conversion of 1 mole liquid water at 10.0oC into 1 mole liquid water 0oC, water (10.0o C) {tex} \rightleftharpoons{/tex} water(0o C).
{tex}\triangle H_1=C_p\times H_2O(l)\times\triangle T{/tex} = -75.3 Jmol-1 K-1 = -753 J mol-1 
Enthalpy of fusion, water (0o C) {tex} \rightleftharpoons{/tex} ice(0o C) , {tex}\Delta {H_2} = {\Delta _{fus}}H{/tex}
{tex}{l}\triangle H_2\;=\;\triangle H_{freezing}=-\triangle H_{fusion\;}{/tex} = -6.06 kJ mol-1 
Enthalpy change for conversion of 1 mole of ice at 0oC to 1 mole of ice at 10oC, Ice (0o C) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} ice(10.0o C);  {tex}\Delta {H_3} = {C_p}[{H_2}O(s)]\Delta T{/tex} = -36.8 Jmol-1K-1 x 10K =-368 J mol-1
{tex}\Delta H = {\text{ }}\Delta {H_1} + {\text{ }}\Delta {H_2} + {\text{ }}\Delta {H_3}{/tex}= - (0.753 + 6.03 + 0.368)kJ mol-1 = -7.151 kJmol-1

questions.question: Enthalpy of combustion of carbon to CO2 is 393.5 kJ mol-1. Calculate the heat released upon formation of 35.2 g of CO2 from carbon and dioxygen gas.
questions.question_raw: Enthalpy of combustion of carbon to CO2 is 393.5 kJ mol-1. Calculate the heat released upon formation of 35.2 g of CO2 from carbon and dioxygen gas.
questions.explanation:
When 1 mole of CO2 is produced energy released is –393.5 kJ mol-1. Moles of  CO2 given = {tex}\frac{35.2}{44}{/tex} = 0.8 moles
So energy released  = 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} -393.5 kJ/mol = -315 kJ/mol

questions.question: Enthalpies of formation of CO(g), CO2(g), N2O(g), and N2O4(g) are -110, –393, 81, and 9.7 kJ mol-1 respectively. Find the value of ${\Delta _{\text{r}}}{\text{H}}$ for the reaction:${{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 3CO}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} \to {\text{ }}{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 3C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)$
questions.question_raw: Enthalpies of formation of CO(g), CO2(g), N2O(g), and N2O4(g) are -110, –393, 81, and 9.7 kJ mol-1 respectively. Find the value of {tex}{\Delta _{\text{r}}}{\text{H}}{/tex} for the reaction:{tex}{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 3CO}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} \to {\text{ }}{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 3C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){/tex}
questions.explanation:
Heat of reaction, {tex}\Delta_r{/tex}H = {tex}{\textstyle\sum_{}}\triangle_rH_{products}-{\textstyle\sum_{}}{\textstyle{\scriptstyle\triangle}_r}{\textstyle{\scriptstyle H}_{reactants}}{\textstyle\;}{\textstyle\;}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\Delta_{r} H{/tex} = [{tex}\Delta_{f} H{/tex}(N2O) + 3{tex}\Delta_{f} H{/tex}(CO2)] - [{tex}\Delta_{f} H{/tex}(N2O4) + 3{tex}\Delta_{f} H{/tex}(CO)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\Delta_{r} H{/tex} = [81 + {3 {tex}\times{/tex} (-393)}] - 99.7 + {3 {tex}\times{/tex} (-110)}] kJ
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\Delta_{r} H{/tex} = -777.7 kJ {tex}\approx{/tex} -778 kJ

questions.question: Given N2(g) + 3H2(g) $\to$ NH3(g); $\Delta_r$H° = -92.4 kJ mol-1. What is the standard enthalpy of formation of NH3 gas?
questions.question_raw: Given N2(g) + 3H2(g) {tex}\to{/tex} NH3(g); {tex}\Delta_r{/tex}H° = -92.4 kJ mol-1. What is the standard enthalpy of formation of NH3 gas?
questions.explanation:
Given, N2(g) + 3H2(g) {tex}\to{/tex} 2NH3(g) ; {tex}\Delta_r{/tex}H° = –92.4 kJ mol-1.
Chemical reaction for the enthalpy of formation of NH3 (g) is as follows:
{tex}{\frac{1}{2}{N_{2\;}}(g)\; + \frac{3}{2}{H_2}(g)\; \to N{H_3}(g)}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}{{\Delta _f}{H^\circ } = \frac{{ - 92.4}}{2} = }{/tex} - 46.2 kJ/mol

questions.question:
Calculate the standard enthalpy of formation of CH3OH(l) from the following data:
CH3OH (1) + O2(g) $\rightarrow$ CO2(g) + 2H2O (1); $\Delta_rH^o$ = -726 kJ mol-1 
C(graphite) + O2(g) $\rightarrow$ CO2(g); $\Delta_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{H}^{o}$ = -393 kJ mol-1
H2(g + O2(g) $\rightarrow$ H2O (1); $\Delta_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{H}^{o}$ = -286 kJ mol-1
questions.question_raw:
Calculate the standard enthalpy of formation of CH3OH(l) from the following data:
CH3OH (1) + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CO2(g) + 2H2O (1); {tex}\Delta_rH^o{/tex} = -726 kJ mol-1 
C(graphite) + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CO2(g); {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{H}^{o}{/tex} = -393 kJ mol-1
H2(g + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2O (1); {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{H}^{o}{/tex} = -286 kJ mol-1
questions.explanation:
Required reaction for the formation of methanol is:
C(s) + 2H2(g) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{O_2}{/tex} (g) {tex}\to{/tex} CH3OH(l) {tex}\Delta_f{/tex}Ho = ?
Enthalpy for the combustion of methanol;

  1. CH3OH (l) + {tex}\frac{3}{2}{O_2}{/tex}(g) {tex}\to{/tex} CO2(g) + 2H2O (l) {tex}\Delta_f{/tex}Ho = - 726 kJ mol-1 ...(1)
    Enthalpy for the formation of 1 mole of CO2(g)
  2. C(s) + O2 (g) {tex}\to{/tex} CO2(g) {tex}\Delta_f{/tex}Ho = - 393 kJ mol-1 ...(2)
    Enthalpy for the formation of 2 moles of H2O(l)
  3. H2(g) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{O_2}{/tex} (g) {tex}\to{/tex} H2O(l) {tex}\Delta_f{/tex}Ho = - 286 kJ mol-1 ...(3)

Now, [eq...(3) {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + eq ...(2) + [ by reversing equation(1)] we get]
C(s) + 2H2(g) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{O_2}{/tex} (g) {tex}\to{/tex} CH3OH (l) {tex}\Delta_f{/tex}Ho = - 239 kJ mol-1

questions.question:
Calculate the enthalpy change for the process ${\text{CC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} \to {\text{ C }}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 4 Cl }}\left( {\text{g}} \right)$and calculate the bond enthalpy of C--Cl in ${\text{CC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}$ (g).
${\Delta _{{\text{vap}}}}{{\text{H}}^0}\left( {{\text{CC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}} \right){\text{ }} = {\text{ 3}}0.{\text{5 kJ mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}$.
${\Delta _{\text{f}}}{{\text{H}}^0}\left( {{\text{CC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}} \right){\text{ }} = {\text{ }} - {\text{135}}.{\text{5 kJ mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}$.
${\Delta _{\text{a}}}{{\text{H}}^0}\left( {\text{C}} \right){\text{ }} = {\text{ 715}}.0{\text{ kJ mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}$, where ${\Delta _{\text{a}}}{{\text{H}}^0}$ is enthalpy of atomisation
${\Delta _{\text{a}}}{{\text{H}}^0}\left( {{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}} \right)$= 242 kJ ${\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}$
questions.question_raw:
Calculate the enthalpy change for the process {tex}{\text{CC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} \to {\text{ C }}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 4 Cl }}\left( {\text{g}} \right){/tex}and calculate the bond enthalpy of C--Cl in {tex}{\text{CC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}{/tex} (g).
{tex}{\Delta _{{\text{vap}}}}{{\text{H}}^0}\left( {{\text{CC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}} \right){\text{ }} = {\text{ 3}}0.{\text{5 kJ mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}{/tex}.
{tex}{\Delta _{\text{f}}}{{\text{H}}^0}\left( {{\text{CC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}} \right){\text{ }} = {\text{ }} - {\text{135}}.{\text{5 kJ mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}{/tex}.
{tex}{\Delta _{\text{a}}}{{\text{H}}^0}\left( {\text{C}} \right){\text{ }} = {\text{ 715}}.0{\text{ kJ mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}{/tex}, where {tex}{\Delta _{\text{a}}}{{\text{H}}^0}{/tex} is enthalpy of atomisation
{tex}{\Delta _{\text{a}}}{{\text{H}}^0}\left( {{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}} \right){/tex}= 242 kJ {tex}{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}{/tex}
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CCl}_{4}(\mathrm{l}){/tex} {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{0}\left(\mathrm{CCl}_{4}\right)=-135.5 \mathrm{KJ} / \mathrm{mol}{/tex} ....(1)
{tex}\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s}) \rightarrow \mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g}){/tex} {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{H}^{0}(\mathrm{C})=715.0 {/tex}KJ mol-1 ......(2)
{tex}\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cl}(\mathrm{g}){/tex} {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{H}^{0}\left(\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right)=242 \ {{kJ}} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} .....(3)
{tex}\mathrm{CCl}_{4}(1) \rightarrow \mathrm{CCl}_{4}(\mathrm{g}){/tex} {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{vap}} \mathrm{H}^{0}\left(\mathrm{CCl}_{4}\right)=30.5 \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} ....(4)
{tex}\mathrm{CCl}_{4}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CCl}_{4}(\mathrm{l}){/tex}  {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{vap}} \mathrm{H}^{0}\left(\mathrm{CCl}_{4}\right)=-30.5 \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} ....(5)
Equation (1) - (2) - 2(3) + (4)
So For CCl4 (g) → C (g) + 4 Cl (g) reaction ΔH0 = -( -135.5 - 715 - 2(242) + 30.5) = 1304 KJ/mol
Bond enthalpy of C—Cl bond will be 1304/4 KJ/mol = 326KJ/mol

questions.question: For an isolated system, $\Delta {\text{U}}$ = 0, what will be $\Delta {\text{S}}$?
questions.question_raw: For an isolated system, {tex}\Delta {\text{U}}{/tex} = 0, what will be {tex}\Delta {\text{S}}{/tex}?

options.text: $\Delta {\text{S}}$ will increase for some time and then reduce

questions.explanation:
For an isolated system, {tex}\begin{array}{l}\triangle U=\;0\\\\\\\end{array}{/tex}and for a spontaneous process, Total entropy change must be positive.

Moreover, {tex}\begin{array}{l}\triangle S=\;\frac{q_{rev}}T=\frac{\triangle H}T=\;\frac{\triangle U\;+p\triangle V}T=\;\frac{0+p\triangle V}T\\i.e.\;T\triangle S>0\;\\Or\;\;\;\triangle S>0\\\\\\\end{array}{/tex}

questions.question: For the reaction at 298 K, 2A + B $\to$ C, $\Delta$H = 400 kJ mol-1 and $\Delta {\text{S}}$ = 0.2 kJ K-1mol-1. At what temperature will the reaction become spontaneous considering $\Delta {\text{H}}$ and $\Delta {\text{S}}$ to be constant over the temperature range.
questions.question_raw: For the reaction at 298 K, 2A + B {tex}\to{/tex} C, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 400 kJ mol-1 and {tex}\Delta {\text{S}}{/tex} = 0.2 kJ K-1mol-1. At what temperature will the reaction become spontaneous considering {tex}\Delta {\text{H}}{/tex} and {tex}\Delta {\text{S}}{/tex} to be constant over the temperature range.
questions.explanation:
Gibbs free energy, {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
At equilibrium {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = 0;  then T= {tex}\frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S}{/tex} = 2000K
Therefore, above 2000K, the reaction will be spontaneous.

questions.question: For the reaction, 2Cl(g) $\rightarrow$ Cl2(g), what are the signs of $\Delta {\text{H}}$ and $\Delta {\text{S}}$?
questions.question_raw: For the reaction, 2Cl(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cl2(g), what are the signs of {tex}\Delta {\text{H}}{/tex} and {tex}\Delta {\text{S}}{/tex}?
questions.explanation: As in this case bond formation between two Cl atom is taking place so energy will be released so {tex}\Delta{/tex}H < 0 and since 2 atoms are combining to form 1 molecule randomness decreases so {tex}\Delta{/tex}S < 0.

questions.question: For the reaction 2A(g) + B(g) $\to$ 2D(g) $\Delta {U^0}$ = - 10.5kJ and $\Delta {S^0}$ = - 44.1J K-1. Calculate $\Delta {{\text{G}}^0}$ for the reaction, and predict whether the reaction may occur spontaneously?
questions.question_raw: For the reaction 2A(g) + B(g) {tex}\to{/tex} 2D(g)  {tex}\Delta {U^0}{/tex} = - 10.5kJ and {tex}\Delta {S^0}{/tex} = - 44.1J K-1. Calculate {tex}\Delta {{\text{G}}^0}{/tex} for the reaction, and predict whether the reaction may occur spontaneously?

options.text: 0.225 kJ, the reaction is not spontaneous
0.165 kJ, the reaction is not spontaneous

questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta {G^0} = \Delta {H^0} - T\Delta {S^0}{/tex}
and {tex}\Delta {H^0} = \Delta {U^0} + \Delta {n_g}RT{/tex}
Here {tex}\Delta {n_g} = - 1{/tex},
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H° = - 10.5 kJ + (-1 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 kJ K-1mol-1 {tex}\times{/tex} 298K = - 10.5 (-2.477) kJ mol-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H° = - 12.977 kJ mol-1
Now, {tex}\Delta{/tex}G° = - 12.977 - (298 {tex}\times{/tex} 44.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3) = - 12.977 + 13.14 = + 0.165 kJ mol-1
The reaction will not occur spontaneously because {tex}\Delta{/tex}G°is positive.

questions.question: The equilibrium constant for a reaction is 10. What will be the value of $\Delta G^0$? R = 8.314 JK-1, T= 300 K, T = 300 K?
questions.question_raw: The equilibrium constant for a reaction is 10. What will be the value of {tex}\Delta G^0{/tex}? R = 8.314 JK-1, T= 300 K, T = 300 K?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}rG0 = -RTln Kc.
{tex}\Delta G^0{/tex} = -2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 {tex}\times{/tex} log10 = -5744.14 Jmol-1 = -5.74414 kJmol-1

questions.question:
Comment on the thermodynamic stability of NO(g), Given:
$\frac{1}{2}$N2 (g) +$\frac{{1}}{2}$ O2(g) $ \to $ NO (g); ${\Delta _{\text{r}}}{{\text{H}}^0}$ = 90 kJ mol-1
NO(g) +$\frac{1}{2}$ O2(g) $ \to $ NO2 (g); ${\Delta _{\text{r}}}{{\text{H}}^0}$ = -74 kJ mol-1
questions.question_raw:
Comment on the thermodynamic stability of NO(g), Given:
{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}N2 (g) +{tex}\frac{{1}}{2}{/tex} O2(g) {tex} \to {/tex} NO (g); {tex}{\Delta _{\text{r}}}{{\text{H}}^0}{/tex} = 90 kJ mol-1
NO(g) +{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} O2(g) {tex} \to {/tex} NO2 (g); {tex}{\Delta _{\text{r}}}{{\text{H}}^0}{/tex} = -74 kJ mol-1

options.text: NO2 (g) is unstable, but NO(g) is formed
NO(g) is unstable, but NO2 (g) is formed
NO(g) is stable, but NO2 (g) is formed
NO2 (g) is stable, but NO(g) is formed

questions.explanation: NO(g) is unstable because the formation of NO is endothermic, but NO2(g) is formed because its formation is exothermic. Therefore, unstable NO(g) converts into stable NO2(g).

questions.question: Calculate the heat and the work associated with a process in which 5 mol of gas expands reversibly at constant temperature T = 298 K from a pressure of 10.00 to 1.00 atm
questions.question_raw: Calculate the heat and the work associated with a process in which 5 mol of gas expands reversibly at constant temperature T = 298 K from a pressure of 10.00 to 1.00 atm
questions.explanation: {tex}\begin{array}{l}Work\;done\;for\;an\;isothermal\;process,\;w=-2.303nRT\;\log\frac{P_1}{P_2}=-2.303\times5\times8.314\times298\times\log\frac{10}1=-2.303\times5\times8.314\times298\times1=-28529.24J=-28.5kJ\\Acoording\;to\;equation\;of\;first\;law\;of\;thermodynamics,\;\triangle U=\;q+w\\For\;isothermal\;process,\triangle U=0;\;\\\Rightarrow Heat,\;q=-w=-(-28.5\;kJ)=28.5\;kJ\\\end{array}{/tex}

questions.question: Thermodynamics is not concerned about
questions.question_raw: Thermodynamics is not concerned about

options.text: the rate at which a reaction proceeds.
the feasibility of a chemical reaction.
energy changes involved in a chemical reaction.
the extent to which a chemical reaction proceeds.

questions.explanation: Thermodynamics tells whether a reaction will take place or not. It doesn't tell about the rate (speed) of reaction.

questions.question: The state of a gas constant can be described by quoting the relationship between
questions.question_raw: The state of a gas constant can be described by quoting the relationship between

State of gas constant is determined by stating the value of P,V,n,T.

i.e. {tex}R=\frac{PV}{nT}{/tex}

questions.question: The volume of gas is reduced to half from its original volume. The specific heat will be
questions.question_raw: The volume of gas is reduced to half from its original volume. The specific heat will be
questions.explanation:
Specfic heat (c) of a substance is defined as the amount of heat required to raise the temperature of 1 gram of substance through 10C.

 {tex}\begin{array}{l}C=\frac q{\triangle T}\;\\\\\\\end{array}{/tex},Specific heat is independent of volume, so it will remain constant.

questions.question: During complete combustion of one mole of butane, 2658 kJ of heat is released. The thermochemical reaction for above change is
questions.question_raw: During complete combustion of one mole of butane, 2658 kJ of heat is released. The thermochemical reaction for above change is
questions.explanation:
Enthapy of combustion is the energy released when 1mole of a hydrocarbon (butane ) reacts completely in presence of excess of oxygen. The chemical equation for exothermic reaction for combustion of one mole of butane is represented as;

{tex}\begin{gathered} 2{C_4}{H_{10}}\left( g \right) + 13{O_2}\left( g \right) \to 8C{O_2}\left( g \right) + 10{H_2}O\left( l \right){\Delta _c}H \hfill= -2658.0{\text{ }}kJ{\text{ }}mo{l^{-1}} \hfill \\ \end{gathered}{/tex}

questions.question: ${\Delta _{\text{f}}}{{\text{U}}^ \ominus }$of formation of CH4 (g) at certain temperature is –393 kJmol-1. The value of ${\Delta _{\text{f}}}{{\text{H}}^\ominus}$ is
questions.question_raw: {tex}{\Delta _{\text{f}}}{{\text{U}}^ \ominus }{/tex}of formation of CH4 (g) at certain temperature is –393 kJmol-1. The value of {tex}{\Delta _{\text{f}}}{{\text{H}}^\ominus}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
The combustion of metahne is given as follows: CH4(g) + 2O2(g) {tex}\to{/tex} CO2(g) + 2H2O(l)
Since {tex}\Delta_{f} H^{\circ}=\Delta_{f} U^{\circ}+\Delta n_{g} R T{/tex} and {tex}\triangle_{f} U^{\circ}{/tex} = - 393 kJmol-1, {tex}\triangle_{f} H^{\circ}{/tex} = (-393) + {tex}\triangle n_{g} R T{/tex}
(Here, {tex}\triangle n_{g}<0{/tex}) {tex}\Rightarrow \Delta_{f} H^{\circ}<\Delta_{f} U^{\circ}{/tex}

questions.question: In an adiabatic process, no transfer of heat takes place between system and surroundings. Choose the correct option for free expansion of an ideal gas under adiabatic condition from the following.
questions.question_raw: In an adiabatic process, no transfer of heat takes place between system and surroundings. Choose the correct option for free expansion of an ideal gas under adiabatic condition from the following.
questions.explanation:
For free expansion {tex}P_{ext} =0{/tex}. So w=0 and for adiabatic process q = 0 ; Now from from 1st law of thermodynamics,

{tex}\Delta{/tex}U = q + w =0 + 0 = 0  therefore {tex}\Delta{/tex}U = 0

Since, there is no change of internal energy, and temperature will also remain constant, making {tex}\Delta{/tex}T=0

questions.question: The entropy change can be calculated by using the expression $\Delta {\text{S }} = $$\frac{{{q_{rev}}}}{T}$ When water freezes in a glass beaker, choose the correct statement amongst the following :
questions.question_raw: The entropy change can be calculated by using the expression {tex}\Delta {\text{S }} = {/tex}{tex}\frac{{{q_{rev}}}}{T}{/tex} When water freezes in a glass beaker, choose the correct statement amongst the following :
questions.explanation: For freezing of process since process is spontaneous therefore if ΔS (system) decreases but ΔS (surroundings) increases.Also, Freezing is exothermic process. The heat released increases the entropy of surrounding.

questions.question:
On the basis of thermochemical equations (A), (B) and (C), find out which of the algebraic relationships given in options (i) to (iv) is correct:
 C(graphite) + O2(g) $\rightarrow$ CO2(g); $ \triangle_{r} H$ = x KJ mol-1
 C(graphite) + $\frac{1}{2} O_{2}(g) \rightarrow C O(g) ; \Delta_{r} H$ = y KJ mol-1
 $C O(g)+\frac{1}{2} O_{2}(g) \rightarrow C O_{2}(g) ; \Delta_{r} H=z k J m o l^{-1}$
questions.question_raw:
On the basis of thermochemical equations (A), (B) and (C), find out which of the algebraic relationships given in options (i) to (iv) is correct:

  1. C(graphite) + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CO2(g); {tex} \triangle_{r} H{/tex} = x KJ mol-1
  2. C(graphite) + {tex}\frac{1}{2} O_{2}(g) \rightarrow C O(g) ; \Delta_{r} H{/tex} = y KJ mol-1
  3. {tex}C O(g)+\frac{1}{2} O_{2}(g) \rightarrow C O_{2}(g) ; \Delta_{r} H=z k J m o l^{-1}{/tex}
questions.explanation:
We have, C(graphite) + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CO2(g); {tex}\triangle_{r} H{/tex} = x KJ mol -1 ..... (1)
C(graphite) {tex}+\frac{1}{2} O_{2}(g) \rightarrow C O(g) ; \Delta_{r} H=y k J m o l^{-1}{/tex} .... (2)
Subtacting (1) and (2), we get;
{tex}C O(g)+\frac{1}{2} O_{2}(g) \rightarrow C O_{2}(g){/tex}; {tex}\triangle_{r} H{/tex} = (x-y) kJmol-1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} z = x - y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y + z

 C (g) + 4 H (g) $ \to {\text{ C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right);{\text{ }}{\Delta _{\text{r}}}{\text{H }} = {\text{ x kJ mo}}{{\text{l}}^{-{\text{1}}}}$
 C (graphite,s) + ${\text{2}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} \to {\text{ C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right);{\text{ }}{\Delta _{\text{r}}}{\text{H }} = {\text{ y kJ mo}}{{\text{l}}^{-{\text{1}}}}$

Consider the reactions given below. On the basis of these reactions find out which of the algebraic relations given in options (i) to (iv) is correct?

  1. C (g) + 4 H (g) {tex} \to {\text{ C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right);{\text{ }}{\Delta _{\text{r}}}{\text{H }} = {\text{ x kJ mo}}{{\text{l}}^{-{\text{1}}}}{/tex}

questions.explanation: Here, x > y. Because in equation (B) all the reactants are in their most stable states of aggregation hence have zero standard molar enthalpies of formation.

questions.question: The enthalpies of elements in their standard states are taken as zero. The enthalpy of formation of a compound is:
questions.question_raw: The enthalpies of elements in their standard states are taken as zero. The enthalpy of formation of a compound is:
questions.explanation:
Standard molar enthalpy of formation of a compound from its elements can be +ve or –ve.
For example: {tex}C+O_{2}(g) \rightarrow C O_{2}(g) ; \Delta_{r} H{/tex} = 393.5 kJ mol-1
{tex}N_{2}(g)+\frac{1}{2} O_{2}(g) \rightarrow N_{2} O(g);{/tex} {tex}\Delta_{r} H{/tex} = +92 k J mol-1

questions.question: Enthalpy of sublimation of a substance is equal to
questions.question_raw: Enthalpy of sublimation of a substance is equal to

options.text: enthalpy of fusion + enthalpy of vapourisation

questions.explanation: The process of sublimation involves the change of solid into vapour. Though in sublimation a solid does not pass through the liquid phase on its way to the gas phase, the enthalpy change is equal to the sum of enthalpy of fusion and enthalpy of vaporization because enthalpy is a state function.

questions.question: Standard Molar Enthalpy of Formation is the standard enthalpy change for the formation of:
questions.question_raw: Standard Molar Enthalpy of Formation is the standard enthalpy change for the formation of:

options.text: one mole of a compound from its elements in at a pressure of 10 bar and 30° C.
one mole of a compound from its elements in their most stable states of aggregation.
one mole of a compound from its elements in at a pressure of 2 bar and 25° C.
one kg of a compound from its elements in their most stable states of aggregation.

questions.explanation: The standard enthalpy change for the formation of one mole of a compound from its elements in their most stable states of aggregation (reference states) is the standard molar enthalpy of formation.

questions.question: Suppose that 1.00 kJ of heat is transferred to 2.00 mol argon (at 298 K, 1 atm). What will the final temperature Tf, if the heat is transferred at constant volume?
questions.question_raw: Suppose that 1.00 kJ of heat is transferred to 2.00 mol argon (at 298 K, 1 atm). What will the final temperature Tf, if the heat is transferred at constant volume?
questions.explanation:
Argon is monoatomic, Here, {tex}{{C_v} = \frac{3}{2}R = \;\frac{3}{2} \times 8.314J{K^{ - 1}}mo{l^{ - 1}}}{/tex}  = 12.47J K-1 mol-1
As volume is kept constan t, qv = nCv {tex}\Delta{/tex}T
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1000J = (2.00mol) {tex}\times{/tex} (12.47JK-1 mol-1) {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}T
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T = 40.09K
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} Tf = 298 + 40.1 = 338K

questions.question: Suppose that 1.00 kJ of heat is transferred to 2.00 mol argon (at 298 K, 1 atm). What will the final temperature Tf be if the heat is transferred at constant pressure?
questions.question_raw: Suppose that 1.00 kJ of heat is transferred to 2.00 mol argon (at 298 K, 1 atm). What will the final temperature Tf be if the heat is transferred at constant pressure?
questions.explanation:
Argon is monoatomic, Here, {tex}C _ { p } = \frac { 5 } { 2 } R = \frac { 5 } { 2 } \times 8.314 J K ^ { - 1 } m o l ^ { - 1 } = 20.79 J K ^ { - 1 } m o l ^ { - 1 }{/tex}
As pressure is kept constant, {tex}q _ { p } = n C _ { p } \Delta T{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 1000 J = ( 2.00 m o l ) \times \left( 20.79 J K ^ { - 1 } m o l ^ { - 1 } \right) \times \Delta T{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \Delta T = 24.05 K{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow T _ { f } = 298 + 24.05 = 322.05 K{/tex}

questions.question: Red phosphorus reacts with liquid bromine in an exothermic reaction $2P(s)\;+3Br_2(l)\rightarrow2PBr_3(g)\:;\;\triangle H=-243kJ$ Calculate the enthalpy change when 2.63 g phosphorus reacts with an excess of bromine in this way.
questions.question_raw: Red phosphorus reacts with liquid bromine in an exothermic reaction {tex}2P(s)\;+3Br_2(l)\rightarrow2PBr_3(g)\:;\;\triangle H=-243kJ{/tex} Calculate the enthalpy change when 2.63 g phosphorus reacts with an excess of bromine in this way.
questions.explanation:
The given reaction is, {tex}2 P ( s ) + 3 B r _ { 2 } ( l ) \rightarrow 2 P B r _ { 3 } ( g ) ; \Delta H = - 243 k J{/tex}
moles of {tex}P ( s ) = \frac { 2.63 } { 31 } = 0.084 \text { moles. }{/tex}
Heat liberated, when 2 mol of P(s) reacted = -243 kJ
Heat liberated, when 0.084 mol of P(s) reacted {tex}= \frac { 243 } { 2 } \times 0.084 = -10.206 k J{/tex}

questions.question: To vaporize 100.0 g carbon tetrachloride at its normal boiling point, 349.9 K, and P =1 atm, 19.5 kJ of heat is required. Calculate $\Delta {{\text{H}}_{{\text{vap}}}}$ for CCl4?
questions.question_raw: To vaporize 100.0 g carbon tetrachloride at its normal boiling point, 349.9 K, and P =1 atm, 19.5 kJ of heat is required. Calculate {tex}\Delta {{\text{H}}_{{\text{vap}}}}{/tex} for CCl4?
questions.explanation:
Number of moles of CCl4 = {tex}{\frac{{100}}{{154}}}{/tex} = 0.6493 moles.
Heat required for 0.6493 moles = 19.5kJ
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Heat required for 1.00 moles = {tex}{ \frac{{19.5}}{{0.6493\;}}}{/tex} = 30.032kJ

questions.question: Calculate the heat and the work associated with a process in which 5.00 mol of gas expands reversibly at constant temperature $T = 298k$ from a pressure of 10.00 to 1.00 atm
questions.question_raw: Calculate the heat and the work associated with a process in which 5.00 mol of gas expands reversibly at constant temperature {tex}T = 298k{/tex} from a pressure of 10.00 to 1.00 atm
questions.explanation:
At constant {tex}T , \frac { P _ { 1 } } { P _ { 2 } } = \frac { 10 a t m } { 1 a t m } = 10{/tex}; n = 5 mol, T = 298K
Thus, work ​​​​​​​{tex}w = - 2.303nRT\log \frac{{{P_1}}}{{{P_2}}} = - 2.303 \times 5 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times \log 10{/tex}{tex} = - 2.85 \times {10^4}J = - 28.5kJ{/tex}
Here, heat {tex}\Delta U = 0 ; \Rightarrow q = - w = + 28.5 k J{/tex}

questions.question: Choose the correct answer. A thermodynamic state function is a quantity:
questions.question_raw: Choose the correct answer. A thermodynamic state function is a quantity:

options.text: whose value depends on temperature only.
whose value is independent of path.
used to determine pressure volume work.

questions.explanation: A state function value depends only on the state of the system and is independent of path.

questions.question: For the process to occur under adiabatic conditions, the correct condition is:
questions.question_raw: For the process to occur under adiabatic conditions, the correct condition is:
questions.explanation: Adiabatic condition would not allow exchange of heat between system and surroundings. Hence q = 0

questions.question: By convention, the standard enthalpies of formation of all elements in their most stable states are:
questions.question_raw: By convention, the standard enthalpies of formation of all elements in their most stable states are:
questions.explanation: By convention, the standard enthalpies of formation of all elements in their most stable states (reference states) are zero.

questions.question: A reaction, A + B $\rightarrow$ C + D + q is found to have a positive entropy change reaction will be:
questions.question_raw: A reaction, A + B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C + D + q is found to have a positive entropy change reaction will be:
questions.explanation:
We know,  {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = ΔH - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -ve (as reaction is exothermic)
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = +ve
So, {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -{tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G will be negative at all temperature hence reaction will be spontaneous at all temperature.

questions.question: Thermodynamics is applicable to:
questions.question_raw: Thermodynamics is applicable to:
questions.explanation: Thermodynamics does not deal with the properties of the individual atoms and molecules but deals with the matter in bulk.

questions.question: An isochoric process takes place at constant:
questions.question_raw: An isochoric process takes place at constant:
questions.explanation: An isochoric process is a thermodynamic process in which the volume remains constant.

questions.question: For a cyclic process, the change in internal energy of the system is________.
questions.question_raw: For a cyclic process, the change in internal energy of the system is________.
questions.explanation: For a cyclic process, the initial state is the same as that of the final state. As internal energy is a state function, its value at the initial point is the same as that at the final point and enthalpy change is zero.

questions.question: Which of the following property is not a state function?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following property is not a state function?
questions.explanation: Work is not a state function because it is proportional to the distance an object is moved, which depends on the path used to go from the initial to the final state.

questions.question: Which of the following relationship is true?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following relationship is true?
questions.explanation:
We know, Cp - Cv = R
Hence, Cp > Cv

questions.question: Which of the following always has a negative value?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following always has a negative value?
questions.explanation: Combustion is an exothermic process. Hence heat of combustion has a negative value.

questions.question: The bond enthalpy depends on?
questions.question_raw: The bond enthalpy depends on?
questions.explanation: The bond enthalpy depends on many factors sizes of atoms involved in the bond, differences in their electronegativity, bond length, electron affinities, etc.

questions.question: For an endothermic reaction:
questions.question_raw: For an endothermic reaction:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H is positive for endothermic reactions that absorb heat from the surroundings.
As, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = Hp - Hr = positive (since Hp > Hr)

questions.question:
For the process depicted by the equation:
H2O (s) $\longrightarrow$ H2O (l)

questions.question_raw:
For the process depicted by the equation:
H2O (s) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H2O (l)

questions.question: Which one is the correct unit for entropy?
questions.question_raw: Which one is the correct unit for entropy?
questions.explanation:
As {tex}\vartriangle S=\frac{{{q}_{rev}}}{T}{/tex}
It is an extensive entropy, therefore, the SI unit of entropy change is Joule K-1 mol-1.

questions.question: An ideal gas is allowed to expand from 1 L to 10 L against a constant external pressure of 1 bar. The work done in kJ is
questions.question_raw: An ideal gas is allowed to expand from 1 L to 10 L against a constant external pressure of 1 bar. The work done in kJ is
questions.explanation:
According to the given conditions, the expansion is against constant external pressure. So, the work done is given by following formula;
W = {tex}-p_{\text {ext }}\left(V_{2}-V_{1}\right){/tex}
= -1bar (10 L - 1L) = -9L bar ({tex}\because{/tex} 1 L bar = 100 J)
= -9 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 J = -0.9 kJ

questions.question: A process will be spontaneous at all temperature if
questions.question_raw: A process will be spontaneous at all temperature if
questions.explanation:
A process will be spontaneous when its free energy (Gibb’s energy) change will be negative, i.e. {tex}\Delta G{/tex} < 0.
The spontaneity of a process is decided by the value of {tex}\Delta G{/tex}, which can be predicted from Gibb’s equation, {tex}\Delta G{/tex} = {tex}\Delta T{/tex} - {tex}T\Delta S{/tex} for positive/negative signs of {tex}\Delta H{/tex} and {tex}\Delta S{/tex} at any/higher/lower temperature as:

{tex}\Delta H{/tex} {tex}\Delta S{/tex} Comment on temperature
(T)		 {tex}\Delta S{/tex} Comment on the process
<0 > 0 at any temp. <0 spontaneous
>0 <0 at any temp. > 0 non-spontaneous
<0 <0 at lower temp. <0 spontaneous
> 0 >0 at higher temp. <0 spontaneous

questions.question: An ideal gas expands from an initial volume Pinto vacuum under isothermal conditions. For this process,
questions.question_raw: An ideal gas expands from an initial volume Pinto vacuum under isothermal conditions. For this process,
questions.explanation:
For an isothermal expansion of an ideal gas 
{tex}\Delta{/tex}U = 0
Since Pext is zero in vacuum. 
W = -Pext{tex}\Delta{/tex}V = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Q = 0

questions.question: During compression of a spring the work done is 10 kJ and 2 kJ escaped to the surroundings as heat. The change in internal energy, $\Delta$U (in kJ) is
questions.question_raw: During compression of a spring the work done is 10 kJ and 2 kJ escaped to the surroundings as heat. The change in internal energy, {tex}\Delta{/tex}U (in kJ) is
questions.explanation:
In the given system, during the compression of spring the work done is 10 kJ and 2 kJ of heat is escaped to the surroundings. So, q = - 2 kJ and W = 10 kJ According to the first law of thermodynamics,
{tex}\Delta U{/tex} = q + W = -2 kJ + 10 kJ
{tex}\Delta U{/tex} = 8 kJ
The change in internal energy, {tex}\Delta U{/tex} (in kj) is 8 kj.

questions.question:
Among the following the set of parameters that represents path functions, is
 q + W
 q
 W
 H - TS
questions.question_raw:
Among the following the set of parameters that represents path functions, is

  1. q + W
  2. q
  3. W
  4. H - TS
questions.explanation:
q(heat) and W (work) represent path functions. These variables are path-dependent and their values depend upon the path followed by the system in attaining that state. They are inexact differentials whose integration gives a total quantity depending upon the path.
In other given options q+W and H-TS are state functions. The value of state functions is independent to the way in which the state is attained. All the state functions are exact differentials and cyclic integration involving a state function is zero.

questions.question: 5 moles of an ideal gas at 100 K is allowed to undergo reversible compression till its temperature becomes 200 K. If Cv = 28 JK-1 mol-1, calculate $\Delta$U and $\Delta$pV for this process. (R = 8.0 JK-1 mol-1)
questions.question_raw: 5 moles of an ideal gas at 100 K is allowed to undergo reversible compression till its temperature becomes 200 K. If Cv = 28 JK-1 mol-1, calculate {tex}\Delta{/tex}U and {tex}\Delta{/tex}pV for this process. (R = 8.0 JK-1 mol-1)
questions.explanation:
Given,
n = 5 mol, T2 = 200 K, T1 = 100 K
CV = 28 JK-1mol-1
{tex}\triangle{/tex}U = nCV {tex}\triangle{/tex}T
= nCV (T2 - T1)
= 5 mol {tex}\times{/tex} 28 JK-1mol-1 {tex}\times{/tex} (200 - 100) K
= 14,000 J = 14 kJ
{tex}\triangle{/tex}pV = nR {tex}\triangle{/tex}T
= nR (T2 - T1)
= 5 mol {tex}\times{/tex} 8JK-1 mol-1 {tex}\times{/tex} (200 - 100) K
= 4000 J = 4 kJ

questions.question: Which one of the following equations does not correctly represent the first law of thermodynamics for the given processes involving an ideal gas? (Assume non-expansion work is zero)
questions.question_raw: Which one of the following equations does not correctly represent the first law of thermodynamics for the given processes involving an ideal gas? (Assume non-expansion work is zero)
questions.explanation:
From the 1st law of thermodynamics,
{tex}\Delta U{/tex} = q + W
where, {tex}\Delta U{/tex} = change in internal energy
q = heat 
W = work done
The above equation can be represented for the given processes involving ideal gas as follows:
Adiabatic process for an adiabatic process, 
q = 0 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta U{/tex} = W
Thus the given option is incorrect.

questions.question: For silver, Cp(JK-1 mol-1) = 23 + 0.01 T. If the temperature (T) of 3 moles of silver is raised from 300 K to 1000 K at 1 atm pressure, the value of $\Delta$H will be close to
questions.question_raw: For silver, Cp(JK-1 mol-1) = 23 + 0.01 T. If the temperature (T)  of 3 moles of silver is raised from 300 K to 1000 K at 1 atm pressure, the value of {tex}\Delta{/tex}H will be close to
questions.explanation:
According to Kirchoff s relation,
{tex}\Delta H=n \int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} C_{p} d T`{/tex}
where, {tex}\Delta H{/tex} = Change in enthalpy.
Cp = Heat capacity at constant pressure.
Given, n = 3 moles, T1 = 300K, T2 = 1000 K, Cp = 23 + 0.01 T
On susbstituting the given values in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\Delta H=3 \int_{300}^{1000}(23+0.01 T) d T=3 \int_{300}^{100} 23 d T+0.01 T d T{/tex}
{tex}=3\left[23 T+\frac{0.01 T^{2}}{2}\right]_{300}^{1000}{/tex}
{tex}=3\left[23(1000-300)+\frac{0.01}{2}\left(1000^{2}-300^{2}\right)\right]{/tex}
= 3 [16100 + 4550] = 61950 J {tex}\approx{/tex} 62 kJ

questions.question: For a diatomic ideal gas in a closed system, which of the following plots does not correctly describe the relation between various thermodynamic quantities?
questions.question_raw: For a diatomic ideal gas in a closed system, which of the following plots does not correctly describe the relation between various thermodynamic quantities?
questions.explanation:
For diatomic ideal gases,
CV = {tex}\frac{f}{2}{/tex} R and CP = {tex}\left(\frac{f}{2}+1\right){/tex}R
where, f = degree of freedom
f = translational degree of freedom + rotational degree of freedom
= 3 + 2 = 5 [at normal temperature]
The explanation of various plots are as follows.
We know that Cp is heat capacity at constant pressure. Thus, it does not vary with the variation in pressure.

questions.question:
The standard electrode potential $E^{\Theta}$ and its temperature coefficient $\left(\frac{d E^{\Theta}}{d T}\right)$ for a cell are 2V and -5 $\times$ 10-4 VK-1 at 300 K respectively. The cell reaction is
Zn(s) + Cu2+(aq) $\rightarrow$ Zn2+(aq) + Cu(s)
The standard reaction enthalphy ($\Delta_{r} H^{\Theta}$) at 300 K in kJ mol-1 is, [Use, R = 8 JK-1 mol-1 and F = 96,000 C mol-1]
questions.question_raw:
The standard electrode potential {tex}E^{\Theta}{/tex} and its temperature coefficient {tex}\left(\frac{d E^{\Theta}}{d T}\right){/tex} for a cell are 2V and -5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 VK-1 at 300 K respectively. The cell reaction is
Zn(s) + Cu2+(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn2+(aq) + Cu(s)
The standard reaction enthalphy ({tex}\Delta_{r} H^{\Theta}{/tex}) at 300 K in kJ mol-1 is, [Use, R = 8 JK-1 mol-1 and F = 96,000 C mol-1]
questions.explanation:
Given,
Eo = 2V, {tex}\left(\frac{d E^{\circ}}{d T}\right){/tex} = - 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 VK-1
T = 300K, R = 8JK-1 mol-1
F = 96000 C mol-1
According to Gibbs-Helmholtz equation,
{tex}\triangle{/tex}G = {tex}\triangle{/tex}H - T{tex}\triangle{/tex}S ....(i)
Also, {tex}\triangle{/tex}G = -nFEo cell ....(ii)
On substituting the given values in equation (ii), we get
{tex}\triangle{/tex}G = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96000 C mol-1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 V [{tex}\because{/tex} n = 2 for the given reaction]
= -4 {tex}\times{/tex} 96000 J mol-1
= -384000 J mol-1
Now {tex}\triangle{/tex}S = nF{tex}\left(\frac{d E^{\circ}}{d T}\right){/tex}
or {tex}\triangle{/tex}S =2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96000C mol-1 {tex}\times{/tex} (-5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4VK-1)
= - 96 JK-1 mol-1
Thus, on substituting the values of {tex}\triangle{/tex}G and {tex}\triangle{/tex}S in Eq. (i), we get -384000 J mol-1
= {tex}\triangle{/tex}H - 300 K {tex}\times{/tex} (-96 JK-1 mol-1)
{tex}\triangle{/tex}H = -984000 - 28800 J mol-1
= -412800 J mol-1
= -412800 kJ mol-1

questions.question:
The reaction, MgO(s) + C(s) $\rightarrow$ Mg(s) + CO(g),
for which $\Delta_{r} H^{\circ}$ = +491.1 kJ mol-1 and $\Delta_{r} S^{\circ}$ = 198.0 JK-1 mol-1, is not feasible at 298 K.
Temperature above which reaction will be feasible is
questions.question_raw:
The reaction, MgO(s) + C(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Mg(s) + CO(g),
for which {tex}\Delta_{r} H^{\circ}{/tex} = +491.1 kJ mol-1 and {tex}\Delta_{r} S^{\circ}{/tex}  = 198.0 JK-1 mol-1, is not feasible at 298 K.
Temperature above which reaction will be feasible is
questions.explanation:
According to Gibbs-Helmholtz equation,
{tex}\triangle{/tex}rG° = {tex}\triangle{/tex}rH° - T{tex}\triangle{/tex}rS°
For a reaction to be feasible (spontaneous)
{tex}\triangle{/tex}rGo < 0
{tex}\triangle{/tex}rHo - T{tex}\triangle{/tex}rSo < 0
Given, {tex}\triangle{/tex}rHo = + 491.1 KJ mol-1
{tex}\triangle{/tex}rSo = 198 JK-1 mol-1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 491.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 -T {tex}\times{/tex} 198 < 0
T > {tex}\frac{491.1 \times 10^{3}}{198}{/tex} = 2480.3 K
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Above 2480.3 K reaction will become spontaneous.

questions.question:
The standard reaction Gibbs energy for a chemical reaction at an absolute temperature T is given by,
$\Delta_r$G° = A - BT
where A and B are non-zero constants.
Which of the following is true about this reaction?
questions.question_raw:
The standard reaction Gibbs energy for a chemical reaction at an absolute temperature T is given by,
{tex}\Delta_r{/tex}G° = A - BT
where A and B are non-zero constants.
Which of the following is true about this reaction?
questions.explanation:
According to Gibb’s Helmholtz equation,
{tex}\triangle{/tex}rGo = {tex}\triangle{/tex}rHo - T{tex}\triangle{/tex}rGo
Given, {tex}\triangle{/tex}rGo = A - BT
On comparing the above two equations, we get,
A = {tex}\triangle{/tex}Ho and {tex}\triangle{/tex}So = B
We know that, if {tex}\triangle{/tex}H° is negative, reaction is exothermic and when it is positive, the reaction is endothermic.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} If A > 0, i.e. positive, reaction is endothermic.

questions.question:
For the chemical reaction, X $\rightleftharpoons$ Y, the standard reaction Gibbs energy depends on temperature T (in K) as
$\Delta_r$Go (in kJ mol-1) = 120 - $\frac{3}{8}$T
The major component of the reaction mixture at T is
questions.question_raw:
For the chemical reaction,  X {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Y,  the standard reaction Gibbs energy depends on temperature T (in K) as
{tex}\Delta_r{/tex}Go (in kJ mol-1) = 120 - {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex}T
The major component of the reaction mixture at T is
questions.explanation:
For a given value of T,

  1. If {tex}\triangle{/tex}rG° becomes < 0, the forward direction will be spontaneous and then the major and minor components will be Y and X respectively.
  2. If {tex}\triangle{/tex}rG° becomes > 0, the forward direction will be non-spontaneous and then the major and minor components will be X and Y respectively.
    {tex}\triangle{/tex}rG° = 120 - {tex}\frac{3}{8} \times{/tex} 315 = 1.875
    i.e {tex}\triangle{/tex}rG° > 0, major component = X

questions.question: Two blocks of the same metal having same mass and at temperature T1 and T2 respectively, are brought in contact with each other and allowed to attain thermal equilibrium at constant pressure. The change in entropy, $\Delta$S, for this process is
questions.question_raw: Two blocks of the same metal having same mass and at temperature T1 and T2 respectively, are brought in contact with each other and allowed to attain thermal equilibrium at constant pressure. The change in entropy, {tex}\Delta{/tex}S, for this process is
questions.explanation:
At the thermal equilibrium,
final temperature Tf = {tex}\frac{T_{1}+T_{2}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} for the 1st block, {tex}\triangle{/tex}SI = Cp In {tex}\frac{T_{f}}{T_{1}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} for the 2nd block, {tex}\triangle{/tex}SII = Cp In {tex}\frac{T_{f}}{T_{2}}{/tex}
When brought in contact with each other,
{tex}\triangle{/tex}S = {tex}\triangle{/tex}SI + {tex}\triangle{/tex}SII = Cp In {tex}\frac{T_{f}}{T_{1}}{/tex} + Cp In {tex}\frac{T_{f}}{T_{2}}{/tex}
= Cp In {tex}\left(\frac{T_{f}}{T_{1}} \times \frac{T_{f}}{T_{2}}\right){/tex} = Cp In {tex}\left[\frac{T_{f}^{2}}{T_{1} T_{2}}\right]{/tex}
= Cp In{tex}\left[\frac{\left(\frac{T_{1}+T_{2}}{2}\right)^{2}}{T_{1} T_{2}}\right]{/tex} = Cp In{tex}\left[\frac{\left(T_{1}+T_{2}\right)^{2}}{4 T_{1} T_{2}}\right]{/tex}

questions.question: The process with negative entropy change is
questions.question_raw: The process with negative entropy change is

options.text: dissociation of CaSO4(s) to CaO(s) and SO3(g)
synthesis of ammonia from N2 and H2

questions.explanation:
The explanation of all the options are as follows:
N2(g) + 3H2(g ) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2NH3 (g),
{tex}\triangle{/tex}ng = 2 - (1 + 3) = -2
So, {tex}\triangle{/tex}S is also negative (entropy decreases)

questions.question: An ideal gas undergoes isothermal compression from 5 m3 to 1 m3 against a constant external pressure of 4 Nm-2. Heat released in this process is used to increase the temperature of 1 mole of Al. If molar heat capacity of Al is 24 J mol-1 K-1, the temperature of Al increases by
questions.question_raw: An ideal gas undergoes isothermal compression from 5 m3 to 1 m3 against a constant external pressure of 4 Nm-2. Heat released in this process is used to increase the temperature of 1 mole of Al. If molar heat capacity of Al is 24 J mol-1 K-1, the temperature of Al increases by
questions.explanation:
It is an irreversible isothermal compression of an ideal gas.

  1. dE = dq + p(Vf - Vi)
    where, dE = Internal energy change
    dq = amount of heat released
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0 = dq + p(Vf - Vi) [{tex}\because{/tex} dE = 0 for an isothermal process]
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dq = - 4 (1 - 5) = 16J
  2. dq = n {tex}\times{/tex} C {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}T (for Al)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 16 J = 1 mol {tex}\times{/tex} 24 J mol-1 K-1 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}T
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}T = {tex}\frac{16}{24}{/tex}K = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}K

questions.question: A process has $\Delta$H = 200 J mol-1 and $\Delta$S = 40 JK-1 mol-1. Out of the values given below, choose the minimum temperature above which the process will be spontaneous
questions.question_raw: A process has {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 200 J mol-1 and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S = 40 JK-1 mol-1. Out of the values given below, choose the minimum temperature above which the process will be spontaneous
questions.explanation:
{tex}\triangle{/tex}G = {tex}\triangle{/tex}H - T{tex}\triangle{/tex}S
The process will be spontaneous, when
{tex}\triangle{/tex}G = -ve, i.e. |T{tex}\triangle{/tex}S| > |{tex}\triangle{/tex}H|
Given: {tex}\triangle{/tex}H = 200 J mol-1 and {tex}\triangle{/tex}S = 40 JK-1 mol-1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} T > {tex}\frac{|\Delta H|}{|\Delta S|}=\frac{200}{40}{/tex} = 5 K
So, the minimum temperature for the spontaneity of the process is 5 K.

questions.question: The entropy change associated with the conversion of 1 kg of ice at 273 K to water vapours at 383 K is (Specific heat of water liquid and water vapour are 4.2 kJ K-1 kg-1 and 2.0 k JK-1 kg-1; heat of liquid fusion and vapourisation of water are 334 k J kg-1 and 2491 k Jkg-1 respectively). (log 273 = 2.436, log 373 = 2.572, log 383 = 2.583)
questions.question_raw: The entropy change associated with the conversion of 1 kg of ice at 273 K to water vapours at 383 K is (Specific heat of water liquid and water vapour are 4.2 kJ K-1 kg-1 and 2.0 k JK-1 kg-1; heat of liquid fusion and vapourisation of water are 334 k J kg-1 and 2491 k Jkg-1 respectively). (log 273 = 2.436, log 373 = 2.572, log 383 = 2.583)
questions.explanation:
The conversion of 1 kg of ice at 273 K into water vapours at 383 K takes place as follows:

{tex}\triangle{/tex}S1 = {tex}\frac{\Delta H_{\mathrm{Fusion}}}{\Delta T_{\mathrm{Fusion}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{334 \mathrm{kJkg}^{-1}}{273 \mathrm{K}}{/tex} = 1.22kJ kg-1K-1
{tex}\triangle{/tex}S2 = C In {tex}\frac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}{/tex} = 4.2 kJ K-1 kg-1 In {tex}\left(\frac{373 \mathrm{K}}{273 \mathrm{K}}\right){/tex}
= 4.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2.303 (log 373 - log 273)kJ K-1 kg-1
= 4.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2303 (2.572 - 2.436) = 1.31kJ K-1 kg-1
{tex}\triangle{/tex}S3 = {tex}\frac{\Delta H_{\mathrm{vap}}}{\Delta T_{\mathrm{vap}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2491 \mathrm{kJkg}^{-1}}{373 \mathrm{K}}{/tex}
= 6.67kJ kg-1K-1
{tex}\triangle{/tex}S4 = C In {tex}\frac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}{/tex} = 2 kJ K-1 kg-1 In {tex}\left(\frac{383 \mathrm{K}}{373 \mathrm{K}}\right){/tex}
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2.303 (log 383 - log 373) kJ K-1kg-1
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2.303 (2.583 - 2.572) kJ K-1kg-1
= 0.05 kJ K-1kg-1
{tex}\triangle{/tex}STotal = {tex}\triangle{/tex}S1 + {tex}\triangle{/tex}S2 + {tex}\triangle{/tex}S3 + {tex}\triangle{/tex}S4
= 1.22+ 1.31+ 6.67+ 0.05
= 9.26 kJ kg-1K-1

questions.question: Consider the reversible isothermal expansion of an ideal gas in a closed system at two different temperatures T1 and T2 (T1 < T2). The correct graphical depiction of the dependence of work done (W) on the final volume (V) is
questions.question_raw: Consider the reversible isothermal expansion of an ideal gas in a closed system at two different temperatures T1 and T2 (T1 < T2). The correct graphical depiction of the dependence of work done (W) on the final volume (V) is
questions.explanation:
For isothermal reversible expansion,
|W| = nRT In {tex}\frac{V_{f}}{V_{i}}{/tex} = nRt In {tex}\frac{V}{V_{i}}{/tex}
where, V = final volume, Vi = initial final.
or |W| = nRT In V = nRT In Vi
On comparing with equation of straight line, y = mx + c, we get
slope = m = + nRT
intercept = - nRT ln Vi
Thus, plot of |W| with In V will give straight line in which slope of 2(T2) is greater than slope of 1(T1) which is given in all options.
Now, if Vt < 1 then y-intercept (- nRT Vi) becomes positive and if it is positive for one case then it is positive for other case also. Thus, it is not possible that one y-intercept goes above and the other y-intercept goes below. Thus, option (b) and (d) are incorrect.
If we extent plot given in option (a) it seems to be merging which is not possible because if they are merging they give the same +ve y-intercept. But they cannot give the same y-intercept because the value of T is different.
Now, if we extend the line of T1 and T2 given in option (c) it seems to be touching the origin. If they touch the origin then y-intercept becomes zero which is not possible. Thus, it is not the exactly correct answer but among the given options it is the most appropriate one.

questions.question: The combustion of benzene (l) gives CO2(g) and H2O(l). Given that heat of combustion of benzene at constant volume is -3263.9 kJ mol-1 at 25° C; heat of combustion (in kJ mol-1) of benzene at constant pressure will be (R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1)
questions.question_raw: The combustion of benzene (l) gives CO2(g) and H2O(l). Given that heat of combustion of benzene at constant volume is -3263.9 kJ mol-1 at 25° C; heat of combustion (in kJ mol-1) of benzene at constant pressure will be (R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1)
questions.explanation:
From the equation,
{tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{6}(l)+\frac{15}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \longrightarrow{/tex} 6CO2(g)+ 3H2O(l)
Change in the number of gaseous moles i.e.
{tex}\triangle{/tex}ng = 6 - {tex}\frac{15}{2}=-\frac{3}{2}{/tex} or -1.5
Now we have {tex}\triangle{/tex}ng and other values given in the question are
{tex}\triangle{/tex}U = - 3263.9 kJ/mol
T = 25° C = 273 + 25 = 298 K
R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1
{tex}\triangle{/tex}Hp = {tex}\triangle{/tex}U + {tex}\triangle{/tex}ngRT
So, {tex}\triangle{/tex}Hp = (-3263.9) + (-1.5) {tex}\times{/tex} 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 {tex}\times{/tex}298
= -3267.6 kJ mol-1

questions.question: $\Delta$U is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\Delta{/tex}U is equal to
questions.explanation:
According to the first law of thermodynamics,
{tex}\triangle{/tex}U = q + W = q - p{tex}\triangle{/tex}V
In isochoric process ({tex}\triangle{/tex}V = 0) {tex}\triangle{/tex}U = q
In isobaric process ({tex}\triangle{/tex}p = 0), {tex}\triangle{/tex}U = q
In adiabatic process (q = 0), {tex}\triangle{/tex}U = W
In isothermal process ({tex}\triangle{/tex}T = 0) and {tex}\triangle{/tex}U = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}U is equal to adiabatic work.

questions.question:
The standard state Gibbs free energies of formation of C(graphite) and C(diamond) at T = 298 K are
$\Delta_{f} G^{\circ}$[C(graphite)] = 0 kJ mol-1
$\Delta_{f} G^{\circ}$[C(diamond)] = 2.9 kJ mol-1 
The standard state means that the pressure should be 1 bar, and substance should be pure at a given temperature. The conversion of graphite [C(graphite)] to diamond [C(diamond)] reduces its volume by 2 $\times$ 10-6 m3 mol-1. If C(graphite) is converted to C(diamond) isothermally at T = 298K, the pressure at which C(graphite) is in equilibrium with C(diamond), is
[Useful information : 1 J = 1 kg m2 s-2, 1 Pa = 1 kg m-1 s-2; 1 bar = 105 Pa]
questions.question_raw:
The standard state Gibbs free energies of formation of C(graphite) and C(diamond) at T = 298 K are
{tex}\Delta_{f} G^{\circ}{/tex}[C(graphite)] = 0 kJ mol-1
{tex}\Delta_{f} G^{\circ}{/tex}[C(diamond)] = 2.9 kJ mol-1 
The standard state means that the pressure should be 1 bar, and substance should be pure at a given temperature. The conversion of graphite [C(graphite)] to diamond [C(diamond)] reduces its volume by 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 m3 mol-1. If C(graphite) is converted to C(diamond) isothermally at T = 298K, the pressure at which C(graphite) is in equilibrium with C(diamond), is
[Useful information : 1 J = 1 kg m2 s-2, 1 Pa = 1 kg m-1 s-2; 1 bar = 105 Pa]
questions.explanation:
G = H - TS = U + pV -TS
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dG = dU + pdV + Vdp - TdS - SdT = Vdp - SdT [{tex}\because{/tex} dU + pdV = dq = TdS]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dG = Vdp if isothermal process (dT = 0) 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}G = V{tex}\triangle{/tex}p
Now taking the initial state as the standard state
{tex}G_{g r}-G_{g r}^{\circ}=V_{g r} \Delta p{/tex} .....(i)
{tex}G_{d}-G_{d}^{\circ}=V_{d} \Delta p{/tex} .....(ii)
Now (ii)-(i) gives,
{tex}\left(V_{d}-V_{g r}\right) \Delta p=G_{d}-G_{g r}+\left(G_{g r}^{\circ}-G_{d}^{\circ}\right.{/tex}
At equilibrium, Gd = Ggr
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(V_{g r}-V_{d}\right) \Delta p=G_{d}^{\circ}-G_{g r}^{\circ}=29 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{J}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}p = {tex}\frac{29 \times 10^{3}}{2 \times 10^{-6}}{/tex} Pa = {tex}\frac{29}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex}108 Pa = {tex}\frac{29000}{2}{/tex}bar
p = p0 + {tex}\frac{29000}{2}{/tex} = 1 + {tex}\frac{29000}{2}{/tex} = 14501 bar

questions.question:
One mole of an ideal gas at 300 K in thermal contact with surroundings expands isothermally from 1.0 L to 2.0 L against a constant pressure of 3.0 atm.
In this process, the change in entropy of surroundings ($\Delta$Ssurr) in JK-1 is (1 L atm = 101.3 J)
questions.question_raw:
One mole of an ideal gas at 300 K in thermal contact with surroundings expands isothermally from 1.0 L to 2.0 L against a constant pressure of 3.0 atm.
In this process, the change in entropy of surroundings ({tex}\Delta{/tex}Ssurr) in JK-1 is (1 L atm = 101.3 J)
questions.explanation:
By first law,
{tex}\triangle{/tex}E = Q + W
For isothermal expansion, {tex}\triangle{/tex}E = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Q = -W
- Qirrev = Wirrev = p{tex}\triangle{/tex}V = 3(2 - 1) = 3 L atm
Also, {tex}\triangle{/tex}Ssurr = {tex}\frac{Q_{\text {irrev }}}{T}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(-3 \times 101.3) \mathrm{J}}{300 \mathrm{K}}{/tex} = -{tex}\frac{303.9}{300}{/tex} = -1.013JK-1

questions.question:
The following reaction is performed at 298K
2NO (g) + O2(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ 2NO2(g)
The standard free energy of formation of NO(g) is 86.6 kJ/mol at 298 K. What is the standard free energy of formation of NO2(g) at 298 K? (Kp = 1.6 $\times$ 1012)
