The following reaction is performed at 298K
2NO (g) + O2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO2(g)
The standard free energy of formation of NO(g) is 86.6 kJ/mol at 298 K. What is the standard free energy of formation of NO2(g) at 298 K? (Kp = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1012)
questions.explanation:
For the given reaction
2NO(g) + O2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO2(g)
Given,
{tex}\Delta G_{f}^{\circ}{/tex} (NO) = 86.6 kJ/mol
{tex}\Delta G_{f}^{\circ}{/tex} (NO2) = ?
Kp = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1012
Now, we have,
{tex}\Delta G_{f}^{\circ}=2 \Delta G_{f\left(\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right)}^{\circ}-\left[2 \Delta G_{f(\mathrm{NO})}^{\circ}+\Delta G_{f\left(\mathrm{O}_{2}\right)}^{\circ}\right]{/tex}
= -RT In Kp = 2{tex}\triangle{/tex}{tex}G_{f\left(\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right)}^{\circ}{/tex}- [2 {tex}\times{/tex} 86,600 + 0]
{tex}\Delta G_{f\left(\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right)}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [2 {tex}\times{/tex} 86600 - R {tex}\times{/tex} 298 ln (1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1012)] 
{tex}\Delta G_{f\left(\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right)}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0 5 [2 {tex}\times{/tex} 86,600- R {tex}\times{/tex} (298) In (1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1012)]

questions.question:
For the process, H2O(l) $\rightarrow$ H2O(g)
at T = 100°C and 1 atmosphere pressure, the correct choice is
questions.question_raw:
For the process, H2O(l) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2O(g)
at T = 100°C and 1 atmosphere pressure, the correct choice is
questions.explanation:
At 100°C and 1-atmosphere pressure,
H2O(l) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H2O(g) is at equilibrium.
For equilibrium,
{tex}\triangle{/tex}Stotal = 0 and {tex}\triangle{/tex}Ssystem + {tex}\triangle{/tex}Ssurrounding = 0
As we know during the conversion of liquid to gas entropy of system increases, in a similar manner entropy of surroundings decreases.
{tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}Ssystem + {tex}\triangle{/tex}Ssurrounding < 0

questions.question: A piston filled with 0.04 mole of an ideal gas expands reversibly from 50.0 mL to 375 mL at a constant temperature of 37.0°C. As it does so, it absorbs 208 J of heat. The values of q and W for the process will be (R = 8.314 J /mol K, In 7.5 = 2.01)
questions.question_raw: A piston filled with 0.04 mole of an ideal gas expands reversibly from 50.0 mL to 375 mL at a constant temperature of 37.0°C. As it does so, it absorbs 208 J of heat. The values of q and W for the process will be (R = 8.314 J /mol K, In 7.5 = 2.01)
questions.explanation:
The process is isothermal expansion, hence
q = - W
{tex}\triangle{/tex}E = 0
W = -2.303 nRT log {tex}\frac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}{/tex}
= -2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.04 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 310 {tex}\times{/tex} log{tex}\frac{335}{50}{/tex}
= -208 J
q = + 208 J
W = -208 J (expansion work)

For the process H2O (l) (1 bar, 373 K) $\to$ H2O (g) (1 bar, 373 K),
the correct set of thermodynamic parameters is

For the process H2O (l) (1 bar, 373 K) {tex}\to{/tex} H2O (g) (1 bar, 373 K),

questions.explanation: At transition point (373 K, 1.0 bar), liquid remains in equilibrium with vapour phase, therefore {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = 0. As vaporisation occurs, the degree of randomness increases, hence {tex}\Delta{/tex}S > 0.

questions.question:
The value of log10 K for a reaction A $\rightleftharpoons$ B is
(Given : $\Delta_rH_{298 K}^o$ = -54.07 kJ mol-1, $\Delta_{r} S_{298 \mathrm{K}}^{\circ}$ = 10 JK-1 mol-1 and R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1; 2.303 $\times$ 8.314 $\times$ 298 = 5705)
questions.question_raw:
The value of log10 K for  a reaction A {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} B is
(Given : {tex}\Delta_rH_{298 K}^o{/tex} = -54.07 kJ mol-1, {tex}\Delta_{r} S_{298 \mathrm{K}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 10 JK-1 mol-1 and R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1; 2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 298 = 5705)
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=\Delta H^{\circ}-T \Delta S^{\circ}=-54.07 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{J}-298 \times 10 \mathrm{J}{/tex}
= - 57.05 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 J
Also, {tex}\triangle{/tex}Go = - 2.303 RT log K
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log K = {tex}\frac{-\Delta G^{\circ}}{2.303 R T}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{57.05 \times 10^{3}}{5705}{/tex} = 10

questions.question_raw:

questions.question: A monoatomic ideal gas undergoes a process in which the ratio of p to V at any instant is constant and equals to 1. What is the molar heat capacity of the gas?
questions.question_raw: A monoatomic ideal gas undergoes a process in which the ratio of p to V at any instant is constant and equals to 1. What is the molar heat capacity of the gas?
questions.explanation:
Given,
{tex}\frac{p}{V}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = V .....(i)
Also from first law : dq = CVdT + pdV
For one mole of an ideal gas : pV = RT
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pdV + Vdp = RdT ....(ii)
From (i) pdV = Vdp
Substituting in Eq. (ii) gives
2pdV = RdT {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pdV = {tex}\frac{R}{2}{/tex}dT
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dq = CvdT + {tex}\frac{R}{2}{/tex}dT
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} {tex}\frac{d q}{d T}=C_{V}+\frac{R}{2}=\frac{3}{2} R+\frac{R}{2}{/tex} = 2R

questions.question: One mole of monoatomic ideal gas expands adiabatically at initial temperature T against a constant external pressure of 1 atm from 1 L to 2 L. Find out the final temperature (R = 0.0821 L atm K-1 mol-1)
questions.question_raw: One mole of monoatomic ideal gas expands adiabatically at initial temperature T against a constant external pressure of 1 atm from 1 L to 2 L. Find out the final temperature (R = 0.0821 L atm K-1 mol-1)
questions.explanation:
For an irreversible, adiabatic process;
0 = CV (T2 - T1) + Pe (V2 - V1)
Substituting the values
CV(T - T2) = 1(2 - 1)atm L
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} T - T2 = {tex}\frac{1}{C_{V}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{3 R}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} T2 = T - {tex}\frac{2}{3 \times 0.082}{/tex}

questions.question: 2 moles of an ideal gas expanded isothermally and reversibly from 1 L to 10 L at 300 K. What is the enthalpy change?
questions.question_raw: 2 moles of an ideal gas expanded isothermally and reversibly from 1 L to 10 L at 300 K. What is the enthalpy change?
questions.explanation:
In the case of a reversible thermodynamic process,
{tex}\triangle{/tex}H = nCp{tex}\triangle{/tex}T
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The process is isothermal, {tex}\triangle{/tex}T = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}H = 0

questions.question: Spontaneous adsorption of a gas on solid surface is an exothermic process because
questions.question_raw: Spontaneous adsorption of a gas on solid surface is an exothermic process because
questions.explanation:
For a spontaneous process {tex}\triangle{/tex}G < 0
Also; {tex}\triangle{/tex}G = {tex}\triangle{/tex}H - T{tex}\triangle{/tex}S
For adsorption of gas on solid surface, {tex}\triangle{/tex}S < 0. Therefore, in order to be {tex}\triangle{/tex}G < 0, {tex}\triangle{/tex}H must be negative.

questions.question: One mole of a non-ideal gas undergoes a change of state (2.0 atm, 3.0 L, 95 K) $\longrightarrow$ (4.0 atm, 5.0 L, 245 K) with a change in internal energy, $\Delta$E = 30.0 L-atm The change in enthalpy ($\Delta$H) of the process in L-atm is
questions.question_raw: One mole of a non-ideal gas undergoes a change of state (2.0 atm, 3.0 L, 95 K) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} (4.0 atm, 5.0 L, 245 K) with a change in internal energy, {tex}\Delta{/tex}E = 30.0 L-atm The change in enthalpy ({tex}\Delta{/tex}H) of the process in L-atm is

options.text: not defined, because pressure is not constant

questions.explanation:
{tex}\triangle H = \triangle U {/tex} + P2V2 - P1V1  Given, {tex}\triangle U = 30.0 L {/tex} atm.
P1 = 2.0 atm, V1 = 3.0 L, T1 = 95 K
P2 = 40. atm, V2 = 5.0 L, T2 = 245 K
{tex}\triangle H = \triangle U + {/tex} P2V2 - P1V1
{tex}30 + (4 \times 5) - (2 \times 3) = 30 + 20 - 6 = 44 L{/tex} atm.

questions.question:
The difference between heats of reaction at constant pressure and constant volume for the reaction
2C6H6(l) + 15O2 $\longrightarrow$ 12CO2(g) + 6H2O(l)
at 25o C in kJ is
questions.question_raw:
The difference between heats of reaction at constant pressure and constant volume for the reaction
2C6H6(l) + 15O2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 12CO2(g) + 6H2O(l)
at 25o C in kJ is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\triangle{/tex}H = {tex}\triangle{/tex}E+ {tex}\triangle{/tex}ngRT {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}H - {tex}\triangle{/tex}E = {tex}\triangle{/tex}ngRT = - 3RT
= -3 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 298 = -7433 J = -7.43 kJ

questions.question: Enthalpy of sublimation of iodine is 24 cal g-1 at 200°C. If specific heat of l2(s) and l2(vap.) are 0.055 and 0.031 cal g-1 K-1 respectively, then enthalpy of sublimation of iodine at 250°C in cal g-1 is
questions.question_raw: Enthalpy of sublimation of iodine is 24 cal g-1 at 200°C. If specific heat of l2(s) and l2(vap.) are 0.055 and 0.031 cal g-1 K-1 respectively, then enthalpy of sublimation of iodine at 250°C in cal g-1 is
questions.explanation:
Given reaction:
I2(5) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} I2(g)
Specific heat of I2(s) = 0.055 cal g-1 K-1.
Specific heat of I2(vap) = 0.031 cal g-1K-1.
Enthalpy (H1) of sublimation of iodine = 24 cal g-1
If q is the amount of heat involved in a system then at constant pressure q = qp and
{tex}\triangle{/tex}H = Cp{tex}\triangle{/tex}T
H2 - H1 = Cp(T2 - T1)
H2 = H1 + {tex}\triangle{/tex}Cp(T2 - T1)
H2 = 24 + (0.031 - 0.055) (250 - 200)
H2 = 24 + (-0.024) (50) = 24 - 1.2 = 22.8 cal/g
Thus, the enthalpy of sublimation of iodine at 250°C is 22.8 cal/g.

questions.question:
Given:
 C(graphite) + O2(g) $\longrightarrow$ CO2(g); $\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} H^{\ominus}$ = x kJ mol-1
 C(graphite) + $\frac{1}{2}$O2(g) $\longrightarrow$ CO2(g); $\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} H^{\ominus}$ = y kJ mol-1
 CO(g) + $\frac{1}{2}$O2(g) $\longrightarrow$ CO2(g); $\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} H^{\ominus}$ = z kJ mol-1 
Based on the above thermochemical equations, find out which one of the following algebraic relationships is correct?
questions.question_raw:
Given:

  1. C(graphite) + O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CO2(g); {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} H^{\ominus}{/tex} = x kJ mol-1
  2. C(graphite) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CO2(g); {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} H^{\ominus}{/tex} = y kJ mol-1
  3. CO(g) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CO2(g); {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} H^{\ominus}{/tex} = z kJ mol-1 

Based on the above thermochemical equations, find out which one of the following algebraic relationships is correct?
questions.explanation:
Second equation given in this question is wrong. Hence, No answer incorrect.
If a corrected second equation is given,
i.e. C (graphite) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CO(g)
and if we take the above reaction in consideration then x = y + z will be the answer as:

  1. C(graphite) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CO(g), {tex}\triangle{/tex}rH° = ykJ/mol
  2. CO(g) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CO2(g), {tex}\triangle{/tex}rHo = zKJ/mol
    Summing up both the equation you will get equation:
    C(graphite) + O2 (g ) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CO2(g), {tex}\triangle{/tex}rH° = x kJ/mol
    Hence, x, y and z are related as:
    x = y + z

questions.question:
Given, C(graphite) + O2(g) $\longrightarrow$ CO2(g); $\Delta_{r} H^{\circ}$ = -393.5 kJ mol-1
H2(g) + $\frac{1}{2}$O2(g) $\longrightarrow$ H2O(l); $\Delta_{r} H^{\circ}$ = -285.8 kJ mol-1
CO2(g) + 2H2O(l) $\longrightarrow$ CH4(g) + 2O2(g); $\Delta_{r} H^{\circ}$ = +890.3 kJ mol-1 
Based on the above thermochemical equations, the value of $\Delta_{r} H^{\circ}$ at 298 K for the reaction,
C(graphite) + 2H2(g) $\longrightarrow$ CH4(g) will be
questions.question_raw:
Given, C(graphite) + O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CO2(g); {tex}\Delta_{r} H^{\circ}{/tex} = -393.5 kJ mol-1
H2(g) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H2O(l); {tex}\Delta_{r} H^{\circ}{/tex} = -285.8 kJ mol-1
CO2(g) + 2H2O(l) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CH4(g) + 2O2(g); {tex}\Delta_{r} H^{\circ}{/tex} = +890.3 kJ mol-1 
Based on the above thermochemical equations, the value of {tex}\Delta_{r} H^{\circ}{/tex} at 298 K for the reaction,
C(graphite) + 2H2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CH4(g) will be
questions.explanation:
Based on given {tex}\triangle{/tex}rHo
{tex}\triangle{/tex}fHo = {tex}H_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -93.5 kJ mol-1 ......(i) 
{tex}\triangle{/tex}fHo = {tex}H_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -285.8kJ mol-1 ......(ii)
{tex}\triangle{/tex}fHo = {tex}H_{\mathrm{o}_{2}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.00 (elements) ......(iii)
Required thermal reaction is for {tex}\triangle{/tex}fH° of CH4
Thus, from III
890.3 = {tex}\left[\Delta_{f} H^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{4}\right)+2 \Delta_{f} H^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{O}_{2}\right)\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\Delta_{f} H^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right)+2 \Delta_{f} H^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)\right]{/tex}

= {tex}\left.\Delta_{f} H^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{4}\right)+0\right]-[-393.5-2 \times 285.5]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta_{f} H^{\circ}{/tex} (CH4) = - 74.8 kJ/mol

questions.question: The heats of combustion of carbon and carbon monoxide are -393.5 and - 283.5 kJ mol-1, respectively. The heat of formation (in kJ) of carbon monoxide per mole is
questions.question_raw: The heats of combustion of carbon and carbon monoxide are -393.5 and - 283.5 kJ mol-1, respectively. The heat of formation (in kJ) of carbon monoxide per mole is

CO + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}O2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CO2(g); {tex}\triangle{/tex}H = -283.5 kJ mol-1 ....(ii)
On subtracting Eq. (ii) from Eq. (i), we get
C ( s ) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CO(g);
{tex}\triangle{/tex}H = = (-393.5 + 283.5)kJ mol-1
= -110 kJ mol-1(approx)

questions.question:
For the complete combustion of ethanol,
C2H5OH(l)+ 3O2 (g) $\longrightarrow$ 2CO2(g ) + 3H2O (l), 
the amount of heat produced as measured in bomb calorimeter, is 1364.47 kJ mol-1 at 25°C. Assuming ideality the enthalpy of combustion, $\Delta_C$H, for the reaction will be (R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1)
questions.question_raw:
For the complete combustion of ethanol,
C2H5OH(l)+ 3O2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2CO2(g ) + 3H2O (l), 
the amount of heat produced as measured in bomb calorimeter, is 1364.47 kJ mol-1 at 25°C. Assuming ideality the enthalpy of combustion, {tex}\Delta_C{/tex}H, for the reaction will be (R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1)
questions.explanation:
C2H5OH (l) + 3O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2CO2(g) + 3H2O(l)
{tex}\triangle{/tex}U = -1364.47 kJ/mol
{tex}\triangle{/tex}H = {tex}\triangle{/tex}U + {tex}\triangle{/tex}ngRT
{tex}\triangle{/tex}ng = -1
{tex}\triangle{/tex}H = -1364.47 + {tex}\frac{-1 \times 8.314 \times 298}{1000}{/tex}
[Here, value of R in unit of J must be converted into kJ]
= -1364.47 - 2.4776 = -1366.9476 kJ/mol
or = -1366.95 kJ/mol

questions.question: The standard enthalpies of formation of CO2(g), H2O(l) and glucose(s) at 25°C are -400 kJ/mol, -300 kJ/mol and -1300 kJ/mol, respectively. The standard enthalpy of combustion per gram of glucose at 25°C is
questions.question_raw: The standard enthalpies of formation of CO2(g), H2O(l) and glucose(s) at 25°C are -400 kJ/mol, -300 kJ/mol and -1300 kJ/mol, respectively. The standard enthalpy of combustion per gram of glucose at 25°C is
questions.explanation:
Also, standard heat of formation ({tex}\triangle{/tex}fH°) can be taken as the standard of that substance.
{tex}H_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\triangle{/tex}fHo(CO2) = -400 kJ mol-1
{tex}H_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\triangle{/tex}fHo(C2O) = -300 kJ mol-1
{tex}H_{\mathrm{glucose}}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\triangle{/tex}fHo(glucose) = - 1300 kJ mol-1
{tex}H_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\triangle{/tex}fHo(O2) = 0.00
C6H12O6(s) + 6O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 6CO2(g) + 6H2O (l)
{tex}\triangle{/tex}cHo (glucose) = 6[{tex}\triangle{/tex}fHo(CO2) + {tex}\triangle{/tex}fHo(H2O)] - [{tex}\triangle{/tex}fHo (C6H12O6) + 6{tex}\triangle{/tex}fHo(O2)]
= 6[-400 - 300] - [-1300 + 6{tex}\times{/tex}0]
= -2900 kJ mol-1
Molar mass of C6H12O6 = 180 g mol-1
Thus, standard heat of combustion of glucose per gram
= {tex}\frac{-2900}{180}{/tex} = -16.11 kJ g-1
To solve such problem, students are advised to keep much importance in unit conversion. As here, value of R (8.314 J K-1 mol-1) in JK-1 mol-1 must be converted into kJ by dividing the unit by 1000.

questions.question:
Using the data provided, calculate the multiple bond energy (kJ mol-1) of a C $\equiv$ C bond C2H2. That energy is (take the bond energy of a C - H bond as 350 kJ mol-1)
2C(s) + H2(g) $\longrightarrow$ C2H2(g); $\Delta$H = 225 kJ mol-1
2C(s) $\longrightarrow$ 2C(g); $\Delta$H = 1410 kJ mol-1
H2(g) $\longrightarrow$ 2H(g); $\Delta$H = 330 kJ mol-1
questions.question_raw:
Using the data provided, calculate the multiple bond energy (kJ mol-1) of a C {tex}\equiv{/tex} C bond C2H2. That energy is (take the bond energy of a C - H bond as 350 kJ mol-1)
2C(s) + H2(g)  {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C2H2(g); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 225 kJ mol-1
2C(s) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2C(g); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 1410 kJ mol-1
H2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2H(g); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 330 kJ mol-1
questions.explanation:
For calculation of C {tex}\equiv {/tex} C bond energy, we must first calculate dissociation energy of C2H2 as
C2H2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2C(g) + 2H(g) ......(i)
Using the given bond energies and enthalpies:
C2H2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2C(g) + 2H(g); {tex}\triangle{/tex}H = -225 kJ ....(ii)
2C(s) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2C(g); {tex}\triangle{/tex}H = 1410 kJ .....(iii)
H2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2H(g); {tex}\triangle{/tex}H = 330 kJ ....(iv)
Adding Eqs. (ii), (iii) and (iv) gives Eq. (i).
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C2H2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2C(g) + 2H(g); {tex}\triangle{/tex}H = 1515 kJ
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1515 kJ = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (C — H) BE+ (C {tex}\equiv{/tex} C )BE
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 350 + (C {tex}\equiv{/tex} C ) BE
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (C {tex}\equiv{/tex} C )BE = 1515- 700 = 815 kJ/mol

questions.question: 2 L of an ideal gas at a pressure of 10 atm expands isothermally into a vacuum until its total volume is 10 L. How much work is done in the expansion?
questions.question_raw: 2 L of an ideal gas at a pressure of 10 atm expands isothermally into a vacuum until its total volume is 10 L. How much work is done in the expansion?
questions.explanation:
In the case of expansion, work is done by the system.
Now, as we know that,
W= −Pext.​ΔV
As the gas is expanding is a vacuum that has no pressure, i.e.,
Pext.​=0
∴W=0
Hence no work is done.
From the first law of thermodynamics,
ΔU=q+W
As the system is working at a constant temperature, i.e., isothermally.
∴ΔU=0
Hence q=−W=0
Hence no heat is absorbed in the expansion.

questions.question: Assume each reaction is carried out in an open container. For which reaction$\Delta H = \Delta E$?
questions.question_raw: Assume each reaction is carried out in an open container. For which reaction{tex}\Delta H = \Delta E{/tex}?

The given option i.e. {tex}{H_2}\left( g \right) + B{r_2}\left( g \right) \to 2HBr\left( g \right){/tex} is correct.
{tex}\Delta H = \Delta E + \Delta n_gRT{/tex}
For {tex}{H_2}\left( g \right) + B{r_2}\left( g \right) \to 2HBr\left( g \right){/tex}
{tex}\Delta n_g = 2-(1+1)=0. i.e. \Delta H= \Delta E{/tex}

questions.question: Which of the following statement regarding entropy is correct?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following statement regarding entropy is correct?

options.text: At absolute zero temperature, entropy of a perfect crystalline substance is taken to be zero
At $0^\circ C,$ the entropy of all crystalline substances is taken to be zero
At absolute zero temperature, the entropy of all crystalline substances is taken to be zero
At $0^\circ C,$ the entropy of a perfect crystalline substance is taken to be zero

questions.explanation:
According to the third law of thermodynamics,
The entropy of a system approaches a constant value as the temperature approaches absolute zero. With the exception of non-crystalline solids (glasses), the entropy of a system at absolute zero is typically close to zero.
The correct statement regarding entropy is represented by the given statement that is "At absolute zero temperature, the entropy of a perfect crystalline substance is taken to be zero."

Among the given options Volume, Mass and Enthalpy are extensive properties and Temperature is an intensive property.

questions.question: If enthalpies of formation for ${C_2}{H_4}\left( g \right),{\text{ }}C{O_2}\left( g \right)$ and ${H_2}O\left( l \right){\text{ }}at{\text{ }}5^\circ C$ and 1 atm pressure are 52, -394 and -286 kJ/mol respectively, then enthalpy of combustion of ${C_2}{H_4}\left( g \right),$ will be
questions.question_raw: If enthalpies of formation for {tex}{C_2}{H_4}\left( g \right),{\text{ }}C{O_2}\left( g \right){/tex} and {tex}{H_2}O\left( l \right){\text{ }}at{\text{ }}5^\circ C{/tex} and 1 atm pressure are 52, -394 and -286 kJ/mol respectively, then enthalpy of combustion of {tex}{C_2}{H_4}\left( g \right),{/tex} will be
questions.explanation:
The chemical equation for the above reaction is:
{tex}C_2H_{4(g)} +3O_2 \rightarrow 2CO_{2(g)} +2H_2O_{(l)} \ \Delta H = ?{/tex}
{tex}\Delta H = (\sum \Delta Hf_{products}) - (\sum \Delta Hf_{reactants}){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow [-788 -572] - 52{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow -1412 \ kJmol^{-1}{/tex}

questions.question: In an endothermic reaction, the value of change in enthalpy $\left( {\Delta H} \right)$ is
questions.question_raw: In an endothermic reaction, the value of change in enthalpy {tex}\left( {\Delta H} \right){/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Endothermic reactions are the chemical reactions that proceed with the absorption of heat energy.
Consider the reaction A+B+q→C+D.
Here, q is the heat energy absorbed during this exothermic reaction.
The enthalpy change for this reaction is ΔHrxn​=Hproducts​−Hreactants​=+q
The positive sign indicates that heat is absorbed in the reaction.
Hence, Hreactants​+q=Hproducts​
Thus, in an endothermic reaction, the total enthalpy of reactant is smaller than that of products.
Hence, for an endothermic reaction, H is positive.

questions.question:
The enthalpy change for the following reaction $NaOH\left( {aq} \right) + HCl\left( {aq} \right) \to NaCl\left( {aq} \right) + {H_2}{\text{O}}\left( l \right)$ is -57 kJ. Predict the value of the enthalpy change in the following reaction
$Ba{(OH)_2} + {H_2}S{O_4}\left( {aq} \right) \to BaS{O_4}\left( s \right) + 2{H_2}O\left( l \right)$
questions.question_raw:
The enthalpy change for the following reaction {tex}NaOH\left( {aq} \right) + HCl\left( {aq} \right) \to NaCl\left( {aq} \right) + {H_2}{\text{O}}\left( l \right){/tex} is -57 kJ. Predict the value of the enthalpy change in the following reaction
{tex}Ba{(OH)_2} + {H_2}S{O_4}\left( {aq} \right) \to BaS{O_4}\left( s \right) + 2{H_2}O\left( l \right){/tex}
questions.explanation:
In the reaction, NaOH(aq)+ HCl(aq) ⟶ NaCl(aq)+H2​O(l), one equivalent of hydrogen ions is neutralized with one equivalents of hydroxide ions. The enthalpy change is -57 kJ.
In the reaction Ba(OH)2​+H2​SO4​(aq) ⟶ BaSO4​(s)+2H2​O(l), two equivalents of hydrogen ions are neutralized with 2 equivalents of hydroxide ions. The enthalpy of neutralization is 2 × (−57kJ)=−114 kJ

questions.question: The combustion enthalpies of carbon, hydrogen and methane are $ - 395.5{\text{ }}kJ{\text{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}},{\text{ }} - 284.8{\text{ }}kJmo{l^{ - 1}}$ and $ - 890.4{\text{ }}kJ{\text{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}$ respectively at $25^\circ C.$ The value of standard formation enthalpies of methane at that temperature is
questions.question_raw: The combustion enthalpies of carbon, hydrogen and methane are {tex} - 395.5{\text{ }}kJ{\text{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}},{\text{ }} - 284.8{\text{ }}kJmo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} and {tex} - 890.4{\text{ }}kJ{\text{ }}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} respectively at {tex}25^\circ C.{/tex} The value of standard formation enthalpies of methane at that temperature is
questions.explanation:
The combustion reaction of carbon is
{tex}C_{(s)} + O_{2(s)} \rightarrow CO_{2(g)}, \Delta H_1 = -395.5 \ kJ/mol{/tex}
The combustion reaction of hydrogen is
{tex}H_{2(g)} + \frac{1}{2}O_{2(s)} \rightarrow H_2O_{(g)}, \Delta H_2 = -284.8 \ kJ/mol{/tex}
The combustion of methane;
{tex}CH_{4(g)} + 2O_{2(g)} \rightarrow CO_{2(g)} + 2H_2O{(g)}, \Delta H_3 = -890.4 \ kJ/mol{/tex}
The reaction for the formation of methane is
{tex}C_{(s)} + 2H_{2(g)} \rightarrow CH_{4(g)} ; \Delta H = ?{/tex}
From the above equations, we get
{tex}\Delta H = \Delta H_1 + 2\Delta H _2 - \Delta H_3{/tex}
{tex}= -395.5 - 2\times 284.8 +890.4{/tex}
= -74.7 kJ/mole

questions.question: Which of the following is always feasible?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is always feasible?

options.text: $\Delta H$(+ve), $T\Delta S$(-ve) and $\Delta H > T\Delta S$
$\Delta H$ (-ve), $T\Delta S$(+ve) and $\Delta H < T\Delta S$
$\Delta H$ (-ve), $T\Delta S$(-ve) and $\Delta H < T\Delta S$

questions.explanation:
The feasibility of reaction is determined by free energy change (ΔG) value. 
ΔG is given by the Gibbs Helmholtz equation:
ΔG =ΔH - TΔS 
The reaction is feasible if ΔG value is negative. Therefore, if  ΔH is negative and TΔS is positive then the ΔG will always be negative.

questions.question:
Given that,
$C + {O_2} \to C{O_2};{\text{ }}\Delta H^\circ = - a{\text{ }}kJ$
$2CO + {O_2} \to 2C{O_2};{\text{ }}\Delta H^\circ = - b{\text{ }}kJ$

questions.question_raw:
Given that,
{tex}C + {O_2} \to C{O_2};{\text{ }}\Delta H^\circ  =  - a{\text{ }}kJ{/tex}
{tex}2CO + {O_2} \to 2C{O_2};{\text{ }}\Delta H^\circ  =  - b{\text{ }}kJ{/tex}
The heat of formation of CO is

{tex}CO{(g)} + \frac{1}{2}O_{2(g)} \rightarrow CO_{2(g)}; \Delta H = -\frac{b}{2} kJ \ ...(ii){/tex}
By subtracting equations (ii) from (i), we get,
{tex}C{(s)} + \frac{1}{2}O_{2(g)} \rightarrow CO_{(g)}; {/tex}
{tex}\Delta H = -a- (-\frac {b}{2}) = \frac{b-2a}{2}k J{/tex}

questions.question: The heat of neutralization of a strong base and a strong acid is 57 kJ. The heat released when 0.5 mole of $HN{O_3}$ solution is added to 0.20 moles of NaOH solution, is

The heat of neutralization (ΔH)-57 kJ/mol, moles of HNO3 = 0.5 mole and moles of NaOH= 0.2 mole. When the HNO3 solution is added to the NaOH solution, then 0.2 moles of HNO3 solution will combine with 0.2 moles of NaOH solution.
Heat released = ΔH x 0.2= 57 x 0.2= 11.4 kJ

questions.question: One gram sample of $N{H_4}N{O_3}$ is decomposed in a bomb calorimeter. The temperature of the calorimeter increases by 6.12 K. The heat capacity of the system is 1.23 kJ/g/deg. What is the molar heat of decomposition for $N{H_4}N{O_3}$?
questions.question_raw: One gram sample of {tex}N{H_4}N{O_3}{/tex} is decomposed in a bomb calorimeter. The temperature of the calorimeter increases by 6.12 K. The heat capacity of the system is 1.23 kJ/g/deg. What is the molar heat of decomposition for {tex}N{H_4}N{O_3}{/tex}?
questions.explanation:
The expression for the heat evolved is q=mSΔT
When 1g of ammonium nitrate decomposes, the heat evolved is q =1×1.23×6.12
When 1 mole of ammonium nitrate decomposes, the heat evolved is q = 1×1.23× 6.12× 80 = 602.21kJ/mol

questions.question: Which one of the following has $\Delta S^\circ $ greater than zero?
questions.question_raw: Which one of the following has {tex}\Delta S^\circ {/tex} greater than zero?
questions.explanation:
As we know that, in the transition from solid-state to the liquid state, entropy increases. Hence the value of ΔS will be greater than zero for the reaction-
{tex}NaN{O_3}(s) \rightleftharpoons N{a^ + }(aq) + NO_3^ - (aq){/tex}

questions.question: How much energy is released when 6 moles of octane is burnt in the air? Given $\Delta H_f^ \circ $ for $C{O_2}(g),{\text{ }}{H_2}O(g)\,and{\text{ }}{C_8}{H_{18}}(l)$ respectively are -490, -240 and +160 J/mol.
questions.question_raw: How much energy is released when 6 moles of octane is burnt in the air? Given {tex}\Delta H_f^ \circ {/tex} for {tex}C{O_2}(g),{\text{ }}{H_2}O(g)\,and{\text{ }}{C_8}{H_{18}}(l){/tex} respectively are -490, -240 and +160 J/mol.
questions.explanation:
{tex}C+O_2→CO2,ΔH^∘_f=−490kJ/mol ...(I){/tex}
{tex}H_2+\frac{1}{2}O_2→H_2O,ΔH^∘_f=−240kJ/mol ...(II){/tex}
{tex}8C+18H→C_8H_{18},ΔH^∘_f =+160kJ/mol ...(III){/tex}
Applying
{tex}(I)×8+(II)×(9)(III) {/tex}
{tex}C_8H_18+\frac{25}{2}O_2→8CO_2+9H_2O{/tex}
{tex}ΔH^∘=−3920−2160−160=6240kJ/mol{/tex}
{tex}ΔH^∘ for \ 6 \ moles \ of \ octane =6240×6{/tex}
{tex}=37440kJ/mol=−37.4kJ{/tex}

questions.question:
For the equilibrium
${H_2}O(l) \rightleftharpoons {H_2}O(g)$ at 1 atm and 298 K
questions.question_raw:
For the equilibrium
{tex}{H_2}O(l) \rightleftharpoons {H_2}O(g){/tex} at 1 atm and 298 K

options.text: Standard free energy change is less than zero $\left( {\Delta G^\circ < 0} \right)$
Standard free energy change is greater than zero $\left( {\Delta G^\circ > 0} \right)$
Standard free energy change is equal to zero $\left( {\Delta G^\circ = 0} \right)$
Free energy change is less than zero $\left( {\Delta G^\circ < 0} \right)$

questions.explanation:
H2O(I) ⇌  H2O(g)
This is a spontaneous process at standard conditions thus, the standard free energy change must be less than zero, i.e.(ΔG° < 0)

questions.question: $\Delta H_f^ \circ $ (298 K) of methanol is given by the chemical equation
questions.question_raw: {tex}\Delta H_f^ \circ {/tex} (298 K) of methanol is given by the chemical equation
questions.explanation: The heat of formation is defined as the heat exchange when one mole of a compound is formed from its constituent elements at STP. In the case of allotropes, the most stable allotrope is taken into account. Among graphite and diamond graphite is stable. Hence, the heat of formation of CH3​OH is represented by the equation. {tex}C(graphite) + \frac{1}{2}{O_2}(g) + 2{H_2}(g) \to C{H_3}OH(l){/tex}

questions.question: For the reaction of one mole of zinc dust with one mole of sulphuric acid in a bomb calorimeter, $\Delta U$ and w corresponds to
questions.question_raw: For the reaction of one mole of zinc dust with one mole of sulphuric acid in a bomb calorimeter, {tex}\Delta U{/tex} and w corresponds to
questions.explanation:
Zn + H2SO4 = ZnSO4 + H2
In a bomb calorimeter, there is no expansion in volume, so, work done will be zero.
This reaction is exothermic. So, some heat will be evolved which will result in the lowering of internal energy.{tex}\Delta U < 0,\;w = 0{/tex}

questions.question: For a spontaneous process the correct statement is:
questions.question_raw: For a spontaneous process the correct statement is:

options.text: Free energy of the system always increases
Entropy of the system always increases
Total entropy change is always positive
Total entropy change is always negative

questions.explanation: The spontaneity of the process appears to be associated with a highly ordered system going to a less-ordered, uniform state. the ΔS of the surroundings increases enough because of the exothermicity of the reaction that it overcompensates for the negative ΔS of the system, and since the overall ΔS=ΔSsurrounding​−ΔSsystem​ the overall change in entropy is still positive.

questions.question: For a phase change
questions.question_raw: For a phase change
questions.explanation:
dG=VdP-SdT
During phase change of a pure substance, both temperature and pressure remain constant, that is phase change occurs at saturation pressure corresponding to the saturation temperature.
Hence, dP and dT both zero during phase change for a pure substance.
Hence, {tex}\Delta G = 0{/tex} for a pure substance during phase change.

questions.question: The enthalpy change $\left( {\Delta H} \right)$ for the reaction, ${N_2}\left( g \right) + 3{H_2}\left( g \right) \to 2N{H_3}\left( g \right)$ is -92.38 kJ at 298 K. The internal energy change $\Delta U$ at 298 K is:
questions.question_raw: The enthalpy change {tex}\left( {\Delta H} \right){/tex} for the reaction, {tex}{N_2}\left( g \right) + 3{H_2}\left( g \right) \to 2N{H_3}\left( g \right){/tex} is -92.38 kJ at 298 K. The internal energy change {tex}\Delta U{/tex} at 298 K is:
questions.explanation:
Formation of 1 mole of ammonia
{tex}\frac{1}{2} N_{2(g)} + \frac{3}{2} H_{2(g)} \rightarrow NH_3{/tex}
{tex}\Delta n = (no. \ of \ moles \ of \ gaseous \ product) - (no. \ of \ moles \ of \ gaseous \ reactant){/tex}
{tex}1-(\frac {1}{2}+\frac{3}{2}){/tex}
= -1
{tex}\Delta H = \Delta U +P\Delta V{/tex}
{tex}P\Delta V = ​​\Delta nRT{/tex}
={tex} -(-1) \times 8.314 \times 298{/tex}
{tex}\Delta H = -92.38 kJ{/tex}
{tex}\therefore -92.38kJ = \Delta U +2.477{/tex}
{tex}\Delta U = -92.38 - 2.477{/tex}
= - 94.857kJ

  1. pressure, volume, temperature

questions.explanation: PV = nRT (For an ideal gas)

questions.question: At 1 atm pressure, $\Delta$S = 75 JK-1 mol-1; $\Delta$H = 30 kJ mol-1. The temperature of the reaction at equilibrium is:
questions.question_raw: At 1 atm pressure, {tex}\Delta{/tex}S = 75 JK-1 mol-1; {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 30 kJ mol-1. The temperature of the reaction at equilibrium is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
at equilibrium, AG = 0
{tex}\therefore T=\frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S}=\frac{30 \ \times \ 10^{3}}{75}{/tex} = 400 K

questions.question: An ideal gas expands in volume from 10-3 m3 J to 10-2 m3 at 300 K against a constant pressure of 105 Nm-2. The work done is:
questions.question_raw: An ideal gas expands in volume from 10-3 m3 J to 10-2 m3 at 300 K against a constant pressure of 105 Nm-2. The work done is:
questions.explanation:
Work done by gas = - Pext {tex}\times{/tex} change in volume
= - 105(10-2 - 10-3)
= -105 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.009 = -900J

questions.question: When one mole of a monoatomic ideal gas at TK undergoes reversible adiabatic change under the constant external pressure of 1 atm changes volume from 1 litre to 2 litre. The final temperature in kelvin would be:
questions.question_raw: When one mole of a monoatomic ideal gas at TK undergoes reversible adiabatic change under the constant external pressure of 1 atm changes volume from 1 litre to 2 litre. The final temperature in kelvin would be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}T V^{\gamma-1}{/tex} = K
{tex}T_{1} V_{1}^{\gamma-1}=T_{2} V_{2}^{\gamma-1}{/tex}
{tex}T_{1} V_{1}^{2 / 3}=T_{2} V_{2}^{2 / 3}{/tex} ({tex}\gamma=\frac{5}{3}{/tex} for monoatomic)
{tex}\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}=\left[\frac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}\right]^{2 / 3}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2 / 3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ T_{2}=\frac{T}{(2)^{2 / 3}}{/tex}

questions.question: For a spontaneous reaction $\Delta$G, equilibrium constant (K ) and $E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}$ will be respectively:
questions.question_raw: For a spontaneous reaction {tex}\Delta{/tex}G, equilibrium constant (K ) and {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} will be respectively:
questions.explanation:
For a spontaneous change {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -ve
Also, K > I and {tex}E_{\text {eell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = +ve

questions.question:
An ideal gas is allowed to expand both reversible and irreversibly in an isolated system. If Ti is the initial temperature and Tf is the final temperature, which of the following statements is correct?
 (Tf)irrev > (Tf)rev
 Tf - > Ti for reversible process but Tf = Ti for irreversible process
 (Tf)rev = (Tf)irrev
 Tf = Ti for both reversible and irreversible processes
questions.question_raw:
An ideal gas is allowed to expand both reversible and irreversibly in an isolated system. If Ti is the initial temperature and Tf is the final temperature, which of the following statements is correct?

  1. (Tf)irrev > (Tf)rev
  2. Tf - > Ti for reversible process but Tf = Ti for irreversible process
  3. (Tf)rev = (Tf)irrev
  4. Tf = Ti for both reversible and irreversible processes
questions.explanation:
Work done in the reversible process is maximum. Thus, in the reversible process
{tex}T_{f_{\mathrm{rev}}}<<T_{f_{\mathrm{irr}}}{/tex}

questions.question: The direct conversion of A to B is difficult and thus it is converted by path A $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ D $\rightarrow$ B. Given $\Delta S_{A \rightarrow C}$ = 50 e.u.; $\Delta S_{C \rightarrow D}$ = 30 e.u.; $\Delta S_{B \rightarrow D}$ = 20 e.u. Where e.u. is the entropy unit, then $\Delta S_{A \rightarrow B}$ is:
questions.question_raw: The direct conversion of A to B is difficult and thus it is converted by path A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} D {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B. Given {tex}\Delta S_{A \rightarrow C}{/tex} = 50 e.u.; {tex}\Delta S_{C \rightarrow D}{/tex} = 30 e.u.; {tex}\Delta S_{B \rightarrow D}{/tex} = 20 e.u. Where e.u. is the entropy unit, then {tex}\Delta S_{A \rightarrow B}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta S_{A \rightarrow B}=\Delta S_{A \rightarrow C}+\Delta S_{C \rightarrow D}+\Delta S_{D \rightarrow B}{/tex}
= 50 + (+30) + (-20) = +60 e.u

questions.question: Given that $\Delta H_{r 298 \mathrm{~K}}$ = -54.07 kJ mol-1 and $\Delta S^\circ _{r 298 \mathrm{~K}}$ = 10 J mol-1 and R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1. The value of log10 K for a reaction, A $\rightleftharpoons$ B is:
questions.question_raw: Given that {tex}\Delta H_{r 298 \mathrm{~K}}{/tex} = -54.07 kJ mol-1 and {tex}\Delta S^\circ _{r 298 \mathrm{~K}}{/tex} = 10 J mol-1 and R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1. The value of log10 K for a reaction, A {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} B is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=\Delta H^{\circ}-T \Delta S^{\circ}{/tex}
= -54.07 - 298 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3
= -57.05 kJ
Also, {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = 2.303 RT log10 K
 log10 K  = {tex}\frac{-57.05 \ \times \ 10^{3}}{2.303 \ \times \ 8.314 \ \times \ 298}{/tex} = 10

questions.question:
For the process:
H2O(l) [1 bar, 373 K] $\rightarrow$ H2O(g) [1 bar, 373 K] the correct set of thermodynamic parameters are:
questions.question_raw:
For the process:
H2O(l) [1 bar, 373 K] {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2O(g) [1 bar, 373 K] the correct set of thermodynamic parameters are:
questions.explanation: At eq. {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = 0 and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S = +ve = SV - SL

questions.question: In the conversion of limestone to lime : CaCO3(s) $\rightarrow$ CaO(s) + CO2(g). The values of $\Delta H^{\circ}$ and $\Delta S^{\circ}$ are 179.1 kJ mol-1 and 160.2 JK-1 mol-1 respectively at 298 K and 1 bar. Assuming $\Delta H^{\circ}$ and $\Delta S^{\circ}$ remains constant with temperature, at which temperature conversion of limestone to lime will be spontaneous:
questions.question_raw: In the conversion of limestone to lime : CaCO3(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CaO(s) + CO2(g). The values of {tex}\Delta H^{\circ}{/tex} and {tex}\Delta S^{\circ}{/tex} are 179.1 kJ mol-1 and 160.2 JK-1 mol-1 respectively at 298 K and 1 bar. Assuming {tex}\Delta H^{\circ}{/tex} and {tex}\Delta S^{\circ}{/tex} remains constant with temperature, at which temperature conversion of limestone to lime will be spontaneous:
questions.explanation:
For spontaneous reaction {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = -ve
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=\Delta H^{\circ}-T \Delta S^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \Delta H^{\circ}<T \Delta S^{\circ}{/tex}
or T > {tex}\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{\Delta S^{\circ}}>\frac{179.10 \ \times \ 10^{3}}{160.2}{/tex} > 1118 K

questions.question: Standard entropy X2, Y2 and XY3 are 60, 40 and 50 JK-1 mol-1, respectively. For the reaction, $\frac{1}{2}$X2 + $\frac 32$Y2 $\rightarrow$ XY3, $\Delta$H = -30 kJ, to be at equilibrium, the temperature will be:
questions.question_raw: Standard entropy X2, Y2 and XY3 are 60, 40 and 50 JK-1 mol-1, respectively. For the reaction, {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}X2 + {tex}\frac 32{/tex}Y2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} XY3, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -30 kJ, to be at equilibrium, the temperature will be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta S=\Delta S_{x y_{3}}^{\circ}-\left[\frac{1}{2} \times \Delta S_{x_{2}}^{\circ}+\frac{3}{2} \times \Delta S_{y_{2}}^{\circ}\right]{/tex}
= 50 - {tex}\left[\frac{1}{2} \times 60+\frac{3}{2} \times 40\right]{/tex}
= -40 J = -40 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 kJ
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
0 = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
or {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
{tex}\therefore \ T=\frac{-30}{-40 \ \times \ 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 750 K

questions.question: For a particular reversible reaction at temperatures T, $\Delta$H and $\Delta$S were found to be both +ve. If Te is the temperature at equilibrium, the reaction would be spontaneous when:
questions.question_raw: For a particular reversible reaction at temperatures T, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S were found to be both +ve. If Te is the temperature at equilibrium, the reaction would be spontaneous when:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = 0. At equilibrium,
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
{tex}\therefore \ \Delta{/tex}H = T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S For a reaction to be spontaneous {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -ve and thus T > Te

questions.question: The incorrect expression among the following is:
questions.question_raw: The incorrect expression among the following is:

options.text: In K = $\frac{\Delta H^{\circ} \ - \ T \Delta S^{\circ}}{R T}$

questions.explanation:
{tex}\because \quad \Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = -RT In K
Also {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=\Delta H^{\circ}-T \Delta S^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} -RT In K = {tex}\Delta H^{\circ}-T \Delta S^{\circ}{/tex}
or In K = {tex}\frac{T \Delta S^{\circ}-\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T}{/tex}

questions.question: A piston filled with 0.04 mole of an ideal gas expands reversibly from 50.0 mL to 375 mL at a constant temperature of 37.0°C. As it does so, it absorbs 208 J of heat. The values of q and w for the process will be: (R = 8.314 J/mol K) (In 7.5 = 2.01)
questions.question_raw: A piston filled with 0.04 mole of an ideal gas expands reversibly from 50.0 mL to 375 mL at a constant temperature of 37.0°C. As it does so, it absorbs 208 J of heat. The values of q and w for the process will be: (R = 8.314 J/mol K) (In 7.5 = 2.01)
questions.explanation:
The given process is isothermal, hence {tex}\Delta{/tex}U =0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} q = -w
{tex}\because{/tex} q = 208 J
{tex}\therefore{/tex} w = -208 J (i.e. work done by the system)

questions.question: For the process H2O (l) $\rightarrow$ H2O(g) at T = 100° C and 1 atmosphere pressure, the correct choice is:
questions.question_raw: For the process H2O (l) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2O(g) at T = 100° C and 1 atmosphere pressure, the correct choice is:
questions.explanation:
At 100°C and 1 atmosphere pressure, water shows equilibrium in two phases as
H2O (l) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H2O(g )
and thus for equilibrium {tex}\Delta{/tex}Stotal = 0 and
{tex}\Delta{/tex}SSystem + {tex}\Delta{/tex}SSurrounding = 0
For change of liquid to gases state, {tex}\Delta{/tex}Ssys is + ve and therefore {tex}\Delta{/tex}Ssurr will be - ve
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}Ssystem > 0 and {tex}\Delta{/tex}Ssurrounding < 0

questions.question: One mole of an ideal gas at 300 K in thermal contact with surroundings expands isothermally from 1.0 L to 2.0 L against a constant pressure of 3.0 atm. In this process, the change in entropy of surroundings ($\Delta$Ssurr) in JK-1 is : (1 L atm = 101.3 J)
questions.question_raw: One mole of an ideal gas at 300 K in thermal contact with surroundings expands isothermally from 1.0 L to 2.0 L against a constant pressure of 3.0 atm. In this process, the change in entropy of surroundings ({tex}\Delta{/tex}Ssurr) in JK-1 is : (1 L atm = 101.3 J)
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta S_{\mathrm{surr}}=\frac{q_{\mathrm{surr}}}{T}=-\frac{q_{\mathrm{sys}}}{T}=\frac{W_{\mathrm{sys}}}{T}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} Isothermal expansion {tex}\Rightarrow \Delta{/tex}U = 0)
{tex}\Delta S_{\text {surr }}=\frac{-P_{\text {ext }}\left(V_{f} \ - \ V_{i}\right)}{T}=\frac{-3(2 \ - \ 1)}{300} \mathrm{~L}{/tex} atm K-1
= -0.01 L atm K-1
= -0.01 {tex}\times{/tex} 101.3 JK-1
= -1.013

questions.question: $\Delta$U is equal to:
questions.question_raw: {tex}\Delta{/tex}U is equal to:
questions.explanation:
For adiabatic process, q = 0
From 1st law of thermodynamics, q = {tex}\Delta{/tex}U - W
{tex}\Delta{/tex}U = W

questions.question:
The standard state Gibbs free energies of formation of C (graphite) and C (diamond) at T = 298 K are
$\Delta$fG° [C (graphite)] = 0 kJ mol-1
$\Delta$fG° [C (diamond)] = 2.9 kJ mol-1
The standard state means that the pressure should be 1 bar, and the substance should be pure at a given temperature. The conversion of graphite [C(graphite)] to diamond [C(diamond)] reduces its volume by 2 $\times$ 10-6m3 mol-1. If C(graphite) is converted to C(diamond)
isothermally at T = 298 K, the pressure at which C(graphite) is in equilibrium with C(diamond), is:
[Useful information: 1J = 1 kg m2s-2; 1 Pa = 1 kg m-1s-2; 1 bar = 105 Pa]
questions.question_raw:
The standard state Gibbs free energies of formation of C (graphite) and C (diamond) at T = 298 K are
{tex}\Delta{/tex}fG° [C (graphite)] = 0 kJ mol-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}fG° [C (diamond)] = 2.9 kJ mol-1
The standard state means that the pressure should be 1 bar, and the substance should be pure at a given temperature. The conversion of graphite [C(graphite)] to diamond [C(diamond)] reduces its volume by 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6m3 mol-1. If C(graphite) is converted to C(diamond)
isothermally at T = 298 K, the pressure at which C(graphite) is in equilibrium with C(diamond), is:
[Useful information: 1J = 1 kg m2s-2; 1 Pa = 1 kg m-1s-2; 1 bar = 105 Pa]
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=\Delta V \cdot \Delta P{/tex}
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 {tex}\Delta{/tex}P
or {tex}\Delta P=\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{\Delta P}=\frac{29 \ \times \ 10^{3} \ \times \ 10^{6}}{2} \mathrm{Pa}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}P = 1.45 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 Pa = {tex}\frac{1.45\ \times \ 10^{\mathrm{s}}}{10^{5}}{/tex} bar
= 1.45 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 bar
PF -1 = 14500 bar
or PF = 14501 bar

questions.question: The enthalpy of vaporization of a liquid is 30 kJ mol-1 and entropy of vaporization is 75 J mol-1K-1. The boiling point of the liquid at 1 atm is:
questions.question_raw: The enthalpy of vaporization of a liquid is 30 kJ mol-1 and entropy of vaporization is 75 J mol-1K-1. The boiling point of the liquid at 1 atm is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta S=\frac{\Delta H_{\mathrm{vap}}}{T}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ T=\frac{\Delta H_{\mathrm{vap}}}{\Delta S}=\frac{30 \ \times \ 10^{3}}{75}{/tex} = 400 K

questions.question: A monoatomic ideal gas undergoes a process in which the ratio of P to V at any instant is constant and equals to 1 atm litre-1. What is the molar heat capacity of gas?
questions.question_raw: A monoatomic ideal gas undergoes a process in which the ratio of P to V at any instant is constant and equals to 1 atm litre-1. What is the molar heat capacity of gas?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\because{/tex} PV = RT
or (V {tex}\times{/tex} 1) V = RT [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{P}{V}{/tex} = 1 atm L-1]
or V2 = RT
or {tex}2 V\left(\frac{\delta V}{\delta T}\right)_{P}=\frac{R}{1}{/tex}
or {tex}\left(\frac{\delta V}{\delta T}\right)_{P}=\frac{R}{2 V}{/tex}
q = {tex}\Delta{/tex}U + W
{tex}n \cdot C_{M} \cdot d T=n \cdot C_{V} \cdot d T{/tex} + PdV
or CM = CV + {tex}\frac{P}{n}\left(\frac{\delta V}{\delta T}\right)_{P}{/tex}
For 1 mole CM = CV + P {tex}\times\left(\frac{\delta V}{\delta T}\right)_{P}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} CM = CV + {tex}\frac{P \ \times \ R}{2 V}=\frac{3}{2} R+\frac{R}{2}=\frac{4 R}{2} \ \left(\frac{P}{V}=1\right){/tex}

questions.question: The entropy change for vaporization of liquid water to steam at 100°C is ________ J K1 mol1. Given that heat of vaporization is 40.8 kJ mol-1.
questions.question_raw: The entropy change for vaporization of liquid water to steam at 100°C is ________ J K1 mol1. Given that heat of vaporization is 40.8 kJ mol-1.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta S=\frac{\Delta H_{v}}{T}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta H{/tex} = 40.8 kJ
T = 373 K
{tex}\therefore \ \Delta S=\frac{40.8 \ \times \ 10^{3}}{373}{/tex}= 109.38 JK-1 mol-1

questions.question: During an isothermal expansion of an ideal gas its:
questions.question_raw: During an isothermal expansion of an ideal gas its:
questions.explanation:
As we know, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}E + {tex}\Delta{/tex}(nRT)
In isothermal expansion, {tex}\Delta{/tex}T = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}E + 0 
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}E
Thus, in isothermal expansion, enthalpy remains unaffected.

questions.question:
A closed flask contains water in all its three states, solid, liquid and vapour at 0°C. In this situation the average KE of the water molecule will be:
 maximum in vapour state
 maximum in solid state
 greater in the liquid than in vapour state
 same in all the three states
questions.question_raw:
A closed flask contains water in all its three states, solid, liquid and vapour at 0°C. In this situation the average KE of the water molecule will be:

  1. maximum in vapour state
  2. maximum in solid state
  3. greater in the liquid than in vapour state
  4. same in all the three states
questions.explanation: KE = {tex}(\frac 32){/tex}RT

questions.question: The internal energy of one mole of a gas is:
questions.question_raw: The internal energy of one mole of a gas is:
questions.explanation: Internal energy of 1 mole of gas = {tex}\frac 32{/tex}RT

questions.question: Following reaction occurring in an automobile 2C8H18(g) + 25O2(g) $\rightarrow$16CO2(g) + 18H2O(g). The sign of $\Delta$H, $\Delta$S and $\Delta$G would be:
questions.question_raw: Following reaction occurring in an automobile 2C8H18(g) + 25O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex}16CO2(g) + 18H2O(g). The sign of {tex}\Delta{/tex}H, {tex}\Delta{/tex}S and {tex}\Delta{/tex}G would be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = +ve No. of gaseous molecules increases
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -ve Combustion occurs
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = -ve This is spontaneous reaction.

questions.question:
For a reaction to occur spontaneously:
 $\Delta$H must be negative
 $\Delta$S must be negative
 ($\Delta$H - T$\Delta$S) must be negative
 ($\Delta$H + T$\Delta$S) must be negative
questions.question_raw:
For a reaction to occur spontaneously:

  1. {tex}\Delta{/tex}H must be negative
  2. {tex}\Delta{/tex}S must be negative
  3. ({tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S) must be negative
  4. ({tex}\Delta{/tex}H + T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S) must be negative
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
For spontaneous reaction {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -ve

questions.question:
Identify the correct statement regarding entropy.
 At absolute zero of temperature, the entropy of a perfectly crystalline substance is +ve.
 At absolute zero temperature entropy of the perfectly crystalline substance is taken to be zero.
 At 0°C the entropy of a perfectly crystalline substance is taken to be zero.
 At absolute zero temperature, the entropy of all crystalline substances is taken to be zero.
questions.question_raw:
Identify the correct statement regarding entropy.

  1. At absolute zero of temperature, the entropy of a perfectly crystalline substance is +ve.
  2. At absolute zero temperature entropy of the perfectly crystalline substance is taken to be zero.
  3. At 0°C the entropy of a perfectly crystalline substance is taken to be zero.
  4. At absolute zero temperature, the entropy of all crystalline substances is taken to be zero.
questions.explanation: The entropy of perfectly crystalline substance has been arbitrarily taken as zero at zero Kelvin.

questions.question: Latent heat of vaporization of a liquid at 500 K and 1 atm pressure is 10.0 K cal/mol. What will be the change in internal energy ($\Delta$U) of 3 mole of liquid at same temperature?
questions.question_raw: Latent heat of vaporization of a liquid at 500 K and 1 atm pressure is 10.0 K cal/mol. What will be the change in internal energy ({tex}\Delta{/tex}U) of 3 mole of liquid at same temperature?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}U + {tex}\Delta{/tex}n RT; given {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 30 kJ mol-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}n = 3 because in L {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} vapour; for 3 mole (liquid - vapour) equilibrium
{tex}\Delta{/tex}n = 3 - 0 = 3
30 = {tex}\Delta{/tex}U + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 {tex}\times{/tex} 500
{tex}\therefore \ \Delta{/tex}U = 27.0 kcal

questions.question: At 27°C latent heat of 1 fusion of a compound is 2930 J/mol. Entropy change during fusion is:
questions.question_raw: At 27°C latent heat of 1 fusion of a compound is 2930 J/mol. Entropy change during fusion is:
questions.explanation: {tex}\Delta S=\frac{\Delta H_{f}}{T}=\frac{2930}{300}{/tex} = 9.77 J mol-1 K-1

questions.question: If the enthalpy change for the transition of liquid water to steam is 30 kJ mol-1 at 27° C, the entropy change for the process would be:
questions.question_raw: If the enthalpy change for the transition of liquid water to steam is 30 kJ mol-1 at 27° C, the entropy change for the process would be:
questions.explanation: {tex}\Delta S=\frac{\Delta H}{T}=\frac{30 \ \times \ 10^{3}}{300}{/tex} = 100 J mol-1 K-1

questions.question:
2Zn + O2 $\rightarrow$ 2ZnO; $\Delta$G° = -616 J
2Zn + S2 $\rightarrow$ 2ZnS; $\Delta$G° = -293 J
S2 + 2O2 $\rightarrow$ 2SO2; $\Delta$G° = -408 J
$\Delta$G° for the reaction: 2ZnS + 3O2 $\rightarrow$ 2ZnO + 2SO2 is:
questions.question_raw:
2Zn + O2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2ZnO; {tex}\Delta{/tex}G° = -616 J
2Zn + S2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2ZnS; {tex}\Delta{/tex}G° = -293 J
S2 + 2O2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2SO2; {tex}\Delta{/tex}G° = -408 J
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G° for the reaction: 2ZnS + 3O2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2ZnO + 2SO2 is:
questions.explanation:
2Zn + O2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2ZnO; {tex}\Delta G_{1}^{\circ}{/tex} = -616 J ...(i)
2Zn + S2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2ZnS; {tex}\Delta G_{2}^{\circ}{/tex} = -293 J ...(ii)
S2 + 2O2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2SO2; {tex}\Delta G_{3}^{\circ}{/tex} = -408 J ...(iii)
By (i) + (iii) - (ii)
2ZnS + 3O2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2ZnO + 2SO2;
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G° = (-616 - 408) - (-293)
= -731 J

questions.question:
PbO2 $\rightarrow$ PbO; $\Delta$G298 < 0
SnO2 $\rightarrow$ SnO; $\Delta$G298 > 0
Most probable oxidation state of Pb and Sn will be:
questions.question_raw:
PbO2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} PbO; {tex}\Delta{/tex}G298 < 0
SnO2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} SnO; {tex}\Delta{/tex}G298 > 0
Most probable oxidation state of Pb and Sn will be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -ve for Pb4+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Pb2+ spontaneous and Pb2+ is more stable
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = +ve for Sn4+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Sn2+ non-spontaneous and Sn4+ is more stable

At constant volume P{tex}\Delta{/tex}V = 0,

questions.question: 2 mole of ideal gas at 27°C temperature is expanded reversibly from 2 litre to 20 litre. Find entropy change. (R = 2 cal/mol K)
questions.question_raw: 2 mole of ideal gas at 27°C temperature is expanded reversibly from 2 litre to 20 litre. Find entropy change. (R = 2 cal/mol K)
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = 2.303 nR log {tex}\frac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}{/tex}
= 2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 log {tex}\frac {20}2{/tex} = 9.2

questions.question:
Which of the following pairs of a chemical reaction is certain to result in a spontaneous reaction?
 Exothermic and increasing disorder
 Exothermic and decreasing disorder
 Endothermic and increasing disorder
 Endothermic and decreasing disorder
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following pairs of a chemical reaction is certain to result in a spontaneous reaction?

  1. Exothermic and increasing disorder
  2. Exothermic and decreasing disorder
  3. Endothermic and increasing disorder
  4. Endothermic and decreasing disorder
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
If {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -ve
If {tex}\Delta{/tex}S = +ve and {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -ve; then {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = always -ve
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H - {tex}\Delta{/tex}S will be negative

questions.question: The molar heat capacity of water at constant pressure P is 75 J K-1 mol-1. When 1.0 kJ of heat is supplied to 100 g of water which is free to expand, the increase in temperature of water is:
questions.question_raw: The molar heat capacity of water at constant pressure P is 75 J K-1 mol-1. When 1.0 kJ of heat is supplied to 100 g of water which is free to expand, the increase in temperature of water is:
questions.explanation:
q = {tex}m \cdot C_{V} \Delta T{/tex}
1000 = {tex}\frac{100}{18} \times 75 \times \Delta T{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ \Delta T{/tex} = 2.4K

questions.question: What is the entropy change (in JK-1 mol-1) when one mole of ice is converted into water at 0°C? (The enthalpy change for the conversion of ice to liquid water is 6.0 kJ mol-1 at 0°C)
questions.question_raw: What is the entropy change (in JK-1 mol-1) when one mole of ice is converted into water at 0°C? (The enthalpy change for the conversion of ice to liquid water is 6.0 kJ mol-1 at 0°C)
questions.explanation: {tex}\Delta S_{f}=\frac{\Delta H_{f}}{T}=\frac{6 \ \times \ 10^{3}}{273}{/tex} = 21.98 J

questions.question: The work done during the expansion of a gas from a volume of 4 dm3 to 6 dm3 against a constant external pressure of 3 atm is (1 atm-L = 101.32 J):
questions.question_raw: The work done during the expansion of a gas from a volume of 4 dm3 to 6 dm3 against a constant external pressure of 3 atm is (1 atm-L = 101.32 J):
questions.explanation:
Work done by gas = Pext {tex}\times{/tex} change in volume
= -3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 litre-atm
= -6 {tex}\times{/tex} 101.32J
= -607.92 J = -608 J

questions.question: Standard enthalpy and standard entropy change for the oxidation of NH3 at 298 K, are -382.64 kJ mol-1 and -145.6 J mol-1 respectively. Standard free energy change for the same reaction at 298 K is:
questions.question_raw: Standard enthalpy and standard entropy change for the oxidation of NH3 at 298 K, are -382.64 kJ mol-1 and -145.6 J mol-1 respectively. Standard free energy change for the same reaction at 298 K is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=\Delta H^{\circ}-T \Delta S^{\circ}{/tex}
= -382.64 - 298 {tex}\times{/tex} (-145.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3) 
= -339.3 kJ mol-1

questions.question: Considering entropy (S) as a thermodynamics parameter, the criterion for the spontaneity of any process is:
questions.question_raw: Considering entropy (S) as a thermodynamics parameter, the criterion for the spontaneity of any process is:
questions.explanation:
For a spontaneous process,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Stotal = {tex}\Delta{/tex}Ssystem + {tex}\Delta{/tex}Ssurroundings be positive.

questions.question:
A reaction occurs spontaneously if:
 T$\Delta$S = $\Delta$H and both $\Delta$H and $\Delta$S are positive
 T$\Delta$S > $\Delta$H and both $\Delta$H and $\Delta$S are positive
 T$\Delta$S < $\Delta$H and both $\Delta$H and $\Delta$S are positive
 T$\Delta$S > $\Delta$H and $\Delta$H is positive and $\Delta$S is negative
questions.question_raw:
A reaction occurs spontaneously if:

  1. T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and both {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S are positive
  2. T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S > {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and both {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S are positive
  3. T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S < {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and both {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S are positive
  4. T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S > {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and {tex}\Delta{/tex}H is positive and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S is negative
questions.explanation:
For spontaneous reaction {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -ve
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = +ve, {tex}\Delta{/tex}S = +ve and T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S > {tex}\Delta{/tex}H

questions.question: The enthalpy and entropy change for the reaction, Br2(l) + Cl2(g) $\rightarrow$ 2BrCl(g) are 30 kJ mol-1 and 105 JK1 mol-1 respectively. The temperature at which the reaction will be in equilibrium is:
questions.question_raw: The enthalpy and entropy change for the reaction, Br2(l) + Cl2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2BrCl(g) are 30 kJ mol-1 and 105 JK1 mol-1 respectively. The temperature at which the reaction will be in equilibrium is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
For {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = 0 at equilibrium
T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H
T = {tex}\frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S}=\frac{30 \ \times \ 10^{3}}{105}{/tex} = 285.7 K

questions.question:
Identify the correct statement for change of Gibbs energy for a system ($\Delta$Gsystem) at constant temperature and pressure:
 if $\Delta$Gsystem = 0, the system is still moving in a particular direction
 if $\Delta$Gsystem = -ve, the process is not spontaneous
 if $\Delta$Gsystem = +ve, the process is spontaneous
 if $\Delta$Gsystem = 0, the system has attained equilibrium
questions.question_raw:
Identify the correct statement for change of Gibbs energy for a system ({tex}\Delta{/tex}Gsystem) at constant temperature and pressure:

  1. if {tex}\Delta{/tex}Gsystem = 0, the system is still moving in a particular direction
  2. if {tex}\Delta{/tex}Gsystem = -ve, the process is not spontaneous
  3. if {tex}\Delta{/tex}Gsystem = +ve, the process is spontaneous
  4. if {tex}\Delta{/tex}Gsystem = 0, the system has attained equilibrium
questions.explanation:
• {tex}\Delta{/tex}Gsystem = -ve, the system is spontaneous.
  • {tex}\Delta{/tex}Gsystem = 0, the system has attained equilibrium.
  • {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = +ve, the system is non-spontaneous.

questions.question:
Identify the correct statement regarding a spontaneous process.
 Lowering of energy is the only criterion for spontaneity.
 For a spontaneous process in an isolated system, the change in entropy is positive.
 Endothermic process are never spontaneous.
 Exothermic process are always spontaneous.
questions.question_raw:
Identify the correct statement regarding a spontaneous process.

  1. Lowering of energy is the only criterion for spontaneity.
  2. For a spontaneous process in an isolated system, the change in entropy is positive.
  3. Endothermic process are never spontaneous.
  4. Exothermic process are always spontaneous.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -ve, for spontaneous process since, q = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 0 for an isolated system, a positive value of {tex}\Delta{/tex}S gives - ve {tex}\Delta{/tex}G.
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = 0 - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S

questions.question:
Which of the following are not state functions?
 q + w
 q
 w
 H - TS
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following are not state functions?

  1. q + w
  2. q
  3. w
  4. H - TS
questions.explanation: U and G(H - TS) are state functions. Also, {tex}\Delta{/tex}U = q + w is state function. But q and w are path-dependent and not state functions.

questions.question: For the gas phase reaction, PCl5(g) $\leftrightharpoons$ PCl3(g) + Cl2(g) which of the following conditions are correct?
questions.question_raw: For the gas phase reaction, PCl5(g) {tex}\leftrightharpoons{/tex} PCl3(g) + Cl2(g) which of the following conditions are correct?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = +ve and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S = +ve;
The disorder increases with an increase in moles.
PCl5(g) {tex}\leftrightharpoons{/tex} PCl3(g) + Cl2; {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = +ve (dissociation)

questions.question: The values of $\Delta$Hand $\Delta$S for the reaction, C(graphite) + CO2(g) $\rightarrow$ 2CO(g) are 170 kJ and 170 JK-1 respectively. This reaction will be spontaneous at:
questions.question_raw: The values of {tex}\Delta{/tex}Hand {tex}\Delta{/tex}S for the reaction, C(graphite) + CO2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2CO(g) are 170 kJ and 170 JK-1 respectively. This reaction will be spontaneous at:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
The reaction will be spontaneous
if T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S > {tex}\Delta{/tex}H (i.e., {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -ve)
T > {tex}\frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S}=\frac{170}{170\ \times \ 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1000 K

questions.question: Three moles of an ideal gas expanded spontaneously into vacuum. The work done will be:
questions.question_raw: Three moles of an ideal gas expanded spontaneously into vacuum. The work done will be:
questions.explanation:
W = - P {tex}\times \ \Delta{/tex}V
= - 0 {tex}\times \ \Delta{/tex}V = 0

questions.question: For vaporization of water at 1 atmospheric pressure, the values of $\Delta$H and $\Delta$S are 40.63 kJ mol-1 and 108.8JK-1 mol-1, respectively. The temperature when Gibbs energy change ($\Delta$G) for this transformation will be zero, is:
questions.question_raw: For vaporization of water at 1 atmospheric pressure, the values of {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S are 40.63 kJ mol-1 and 108.8JK-1 mol-1, respectively. The temperature when Gibbs energy change ({tex}\Delta{/tex}G) for this transformation will be zero, is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = 0, {tex}\therefore \ \Delta{/tex}H = T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
T = {tex}\frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S}=\frac{40.63 \ \times \ 10^{3}}{108.8}{/tex} = 373.4 K

questions.explanation: Ideal gas do not show cooling or heating under adiabatic expansion.

questions.question: Standard enthalpy of vaporization $\Delta_{\text {vap. }} H^{\ominus}$ for water at 100°C is 40.66 kJ mol-1. The internal energy of vaporization of water at 100°C (in kJ mol-1) is:
questions.question_raw: Standard enthalpy of vaporization {tex}\Delta_{\text {vap. }} H^{\ominus}{/tex} for water at 100°C is 40.66 kJ mol-1. The internal energy of vaporization of water at 100°C (in kJ mol-1) is:
questions.explanation:
H2O(l) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2Q(v)
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}\Delta{/tex}n = 1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H° = {tex}\Delta{/tex}U° + {tex}\Delta{/tex}nRT
40.66 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 = {tex}\Delta{/tex}U°+ 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 8314 {tex}\times{/tex} 373
{tex}\therefore \ \Delta{/tex}U° = 37559 J = 37.56 kJ

questions.question: The enthalpy of fusion of water is 1.435 kcal/ mol. The molar entropy change for the melting of ice at 0°C is:
questions.question_raw: The enthalpy of fusion of water is 1.435 kcal/ mol. The molar entropy change for the melting of ice at 0°C is:
questions.explanation: {tex}\Delta S=\frac{\Delta H}{T}=\frac{1.435\ \times \ 10^{3}}{273}{/tex} = 5.260 cal/(mol-1 K-1)

questions.question: In which of the following reactions, standard reaction entropy change ($\Delta$S°) is positive and standard Gibbs’s energy change ($\Delta$G°) decreases sharply with increasing temperature?
questions.question_raw: In which of the following reactions, standard reaction entropy change ({tex}\Delta{/tex}S°) is positive and standard Gibbs’s energy change ({tex}\Delta{/tex}G°) decreases sharply with increasing temperature?
questions.explanation:
C(s) + {tex}\frac 12{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex}​​​​ CO(g); {tex}\Delta n=+\frac{1}{2}{/tex}; Also the moles of gases increase and therefore entropy change ({tex}\Delta{/tex}S) is positive. An increase in temperature will cause more change in T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S. Also, it is a combustion reaction and thus {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = - ve
Since {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
= -ve -(+ ve) = -ve

questions.question: Equal volumes of two monoatomic gases, A and B, at same temperature and pressure are mixed. The ratio of specific heats $\left(\frac {C_{P}}{C_{V}}\right)$ of the mixture will be:
questions.question_raw: Equal volumes of two monoatomic gases, A and B, at same temperature and pressure are mixed. The ratio of specific heats {tex}\left(\frac {C_{P}}{C_{V}}\right){/tex} of the mixture will be:
questions.explanation: For monoatomic gases Cp:CV is 1.67. Note that equal volumes are mixed.

questions.question: A reaction having equal energies of activation for forward and reverse reactions has:
questions.question_raw: A reaction having equal energies of activation for forward and reverse reactions has:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}E_{a_{j}}-E_{a_{b}}{/tex}
{tex}\because \ E_{a_{f}}=E_{a_{b}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 0

questions.question: For the reaction, X2O4(l) $\rightarrow$ 2XO2(g) $\Delta$U = 2.1 kcal, $\Delta$S = 20 cal K-1 at 300 K Hence, $\Delta$G is:
questions.question_raw: For the reaction, X2O4(l) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2XO2(g) {tex}\Delta{/tex}U = 2.1 kcal, {tex}\Delta{/tex}S = 20 cal K-1 at 300 K Hence, {tex}\Delta{/tex}G is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}n = 2 - 0 = 2
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}U + {tex}\Delta{/tex}n(g) RT
= 2100 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 = 3300 cal
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
= 3300 - 300 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = -2700 cal
= -2.700 kcal

questions.question: The correct thermodynamic conditions for the spontaneous reaction at all temperatures are:
questions.question_raw: The correct thermodynamic conditions for the spontaneous reaction at all temperatures are:
questions.explanation: For a spontaneous process, {tex}\Delta{/tex}G should be negative at all temperatures, from {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S. This is possible only when {tex}\Delta{/tex}H is negative and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S is positive.

questions.question: For a sample of perfect gas when its pressure is changed isothermally from pi to pf, the entropy change is given by:
questions.question_raw: For a sample of perfect gas when its pressure is changed isothermally from pi to pf, the entropy change is given by:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta S_{s y s}{/tex} = nR loge {tex}\frac{P_{1}}{P_{2}}{/tex} + nCp loge {tex}\frac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}{/tex}
For isothermal process, T1 = T2
{tex}\therefore \ \Delta S_{s y s}{/tex} = nR loge {tex}\frac{P_{i}}{P_{f}}{/tex}

questions.question: For a given reaction $\Delta$H = 35.5 kJ mol-1 and $\Delta$S = 83.6 JK-1mol-1. The reaction is spontaneous at: (Assume that $\Delta$H and $\Delta$S do not vary with temperature)
questions.question_raw: For a given reaction {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 35.5 kJ mol-1 and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S = 83.6 JK-1mol-1. The reaction is spontaneous at: (Assume that {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S do not vary with temperature)
questions.explanation:
For a spontaneous reaction, {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -ve, Thus from
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H < T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
T > {tex}\frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S}=\frac{35.5 \ \times \ 1000}{83.6}{/tex} = 425 K

questions.question: A gas is allowed to expand in a well insulated container against a constant external pressure of 2.5 atm from an initial volume of 2.50 L to a final volume of 4.50 L. The change in internal energy $\Delta$U of the gas in joule will be:
questions.question_raw: A gas is allowed to expand in a well insulated container against a constant external pressure of 2.5 atm from an initial volume of 2.50 L to a final volume of 4.50 L. The change in internal energy {tex}\Delta{/tex}U of the gas in joule will be:
questions.explanation:
q = 0 (For insulated systems)
From 1 law q = {tex}\Delta{/tex}U +(-W) Work done by the system
{tex}\Delta{/tex}U = +W
{tex}\therefore \ \Delta{/tex}U = W = -Pext {tex}\times \ \Delta{/tex}V
= -2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} (4.5 - 2.5)
= -5 L - atm
= -5 {tex}\times{/tex} 101 = -505 J

questions.question: 16 g of gas O2 in the state (P1, V1, T1) is expanded adiabatically to attain state B(P2, V2, T2). The work done by the gas in calorie is:
questions.question_raw: 16 g of gas O2 in the state (P1, V1, T1) is expanded adiabatically to attain state B(P2, V2, T2). The work done by the gas in calorie is:
questions.explanation:
Work done by gas in adiabatic expansion is = {tex}\frac{n R\left(T_{2} \ - \ T_{1}\right)}{\gamma \ - \ 1}{/tex}
{tex}n_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}=\frac{16}{32}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Wadiabatic = {tex}\frac{1 \ \times \ 2\left[T_{2} \ - \ T_{1}\right]}{2(\gamma \ - \ 1)}=\frac{\left[T_{2} \ - \ T_{1}\right]}{(\gamma \ - \ 1)}{/tex}
Also {tex}\gamma{/tex} = 1.40 for O2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Wadiabatic = {tex}\frac{\left[T_{2} \ - \ T_{1}\right]}{1.40\ - \ 1}=\frac{\left[T_{2} \ - \ T_{1}\right]}{0.4}{/tex}
T2 < T1 i.e., Wadiabatic = -ve during expansion

questions.question:
The slopes of the isothermal and adiabatic curves are related by:
 isothermal curve slope = $\gamma \ \times$ adiabatic curve slope
 isothermal curve slope = adiabatic curve slope
 isothermal curve slope = $\frac{1}{\gamma} \times$ adiabatic slope
 isothermal curve slope = $\frac{1}{2} \times$ adiabatic slope
questions.question_raw:
The slopes of the isothermal and adiabatic curves are related by:

  1. isothermal curve slope = {tex}\gamma \ \times{/tex} adiabatic curve slope
  2. isothermal curve slope = adiabatic curve slope
  3. isothermal curve slope = {tex}\frac{1}{\gamma} \times{/tex} adiabatic slope
  4. isothermal curve slope = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} adiabatic slope
questions.explanation:
For isothermal curve: PV = constant or PdV + VdP = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} slope {tex}\frac{d P}{d V}=-\frac{P}{V}{/tex}
For adiabatic curve: PV{tex}\gamma{/tex} = constant
or {tex}\gamma P V^{\gamma-1}{/tex} dV + dp {tex}\cdot \ V^{\gamma}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} slope = {tex}\frac{d P}{d V}=-\gamma \frac{P}{V}{/tex}

questions.question: A gaseous phase reaction: 2CO + O2 $\rightarrow$ 2CO2; $\Delta$H = -300 kJ is made in 10 litre vessel at 500K and 70 bar pressure. If the pressure after the reaction becomes 50 bar, calculate the change in internal energy as a result of reaction:
questions.question_raw: A gaseous phase reaction: 2CO + O2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2CO2; {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -300 kJ is made in 10 litre vessel at 500K and 70 bar pressure. If the pressure after the reaction becomes 50 bar, calculate the change in internal energy as a result of reaction:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}P = 20 bar
= 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 Nm-2
V = 10 litre = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3m3
{tex}\Delta{/tex}n = 2 - 3 = -1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}U + {tex}\Delta{/tex}n ({tex}\Delta{/tex}P x V)
-300 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 = {tex}\Delta{/tex}U + (-1) {tex}\times{/tex} 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3
-300 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 = {tex}\Delta{/tex}-20 {tex}\times{/tex} 103
{tex}\therefore \ \Delta{/tex}U = -280 {tex}\times{/tex} 103J = - 280kJ

questions.question: A system is provided 50 joule of heat and work are done on the system is 10 J. The change in internal energy during the process is:

questions.explanation:
q = {tex}\Delta{/tex}U - W; -W is work done by the system
{tex}\therefore \ \Delta{/tex}U = q + W
= 50 + 10 = 60 J

questions.question: 1 mole of gas occupying 3 litre volume is expanded against a constant external pressure of 1 atm to a volume of 15 litre. The work done by the system is:

-Wirr. = Pext. (V2 - V1)
= 1 {tex}\times{/tex} (15 - 3) = 12 litre atm
= {tex}\frac{12 \ \times \ 1.987 \ \times \ 4184}{0.0821}{/tex}= 1.215 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 J

questions.question: One mole of ice is converted into the water at 273 K. The entropies of H2O(s) and H2O(l) are 38.20 and 60.01 J mol-1 K-1 respectively. The enthalpy change for the conversion is:

{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
or {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 273 {tex}\times{/tex} (60.01 - 38.20) = 5954.13 J mol-1

questions.question: Molar heat capacity for a gas at constant temperature and pressure is:

at constant P and T, {tex}\delta{/tex}T = 0

questions.question: The work is done in an open vessel at 300 K, when 112 g iron reacts with dil. HCI is:
questions.question_raw: The work is done in an open vessel at 300 K, when 112 g iron reacts with dil. HCI is:
questions.explanation:
Fe + 2HCI {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} FeCI2 + H2
Moles of Fe = {tex}\frac {112}{56}{/tex} = 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} moles of H2 formed = 2
Work done = P(V2 - V1) = {tex}-P \cdot V_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}{/tex}
[V2 - {tex}V_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}{/tex}, and V1 = 0 (for solid + liquid state)] = W = {tex}-P \times \frac{n R T}{P}{/tex} = nRT ({tex}\because \ P_{\mathrm{H}_{2}} \ \times{/tex} V = nRT)
= -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 = -1200 cal

questions.question: The temperature coefficient of emf of a cell can be given by:
questions.question_raw: The temperature coefficient of emf of a cell can be given by:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\because \ \Delta G=\Delta H+T\left(\frac{\delta \Delta G}{\delta T}\right)_{P}{/tex}
Gibbs-Helmholtz equation
and {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S and -{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = nEF
{tex}\therefore \quad \Delta G-\Delta H=-T \Delta S=T\left(\frac{\delta G}{\delta T}\right)_{P}{/tex}
or {tex}-T \Delta S=T\left[\frac{\delta(-n E F)}{\delta T}\right]_{P}{/tex}
or {tex}\left(\frac{\delta E}{\delta T}\right)_{P}=\frac{\Delta S}{n F}{/tex}

questions.question: 16g oxygen gas expands at STP to occupy double of its original volume. The work done during the process is:
questions.question_raw: 16g oxygen gas expands at STP to occupy double of its original volume. The work done during the process is:
questions.explanation:
At STP 16g O2 or {tex}\frac 12{/tex} mole O2 will occupy 11.2 litre. Thus if volume is doubled, it means
(V2 - V1) = 22.4 - 11.2 = 11.2 litre
W = - P {tex}\times{/tex} (V2 - V1) = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 11.2 litre atm
= {tex}\frac{1 \ \times \ 11.2 \ \times \ 2}{0.0821} \text { cal }{/tex} = -272.84 kcal

questions.question: At very low temperatures, heat capacity (C) of a solid is given as: C $\propto$ KT3 where K is 3 $\times$ 10-8 J mol-1 K-1. The change in enthalpy during heating 1 mol of the solid from 0 K to 200 K is:
questions.question_raw: At very low temperatures, heat capacity (C) of a solid is given as: C {tex}\propto{/tex} KT3 where K is 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 J mol-1 K-1. The change in enthalpy during heating 1 mol of the solid from 0 K to 200 K is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta H=\int_\limits{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} n C \cdot \Delta T=\int_\limits{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} 1 \times K T^{3} \cdot d T{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{K}{4}\left[T^{4}\right]_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}}=\frac{K}{4}\left[T_{2}^{4}-T_{1}^{4}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{3 \ \times \ 10^{-8}}{4} \times{/tex} [(200)4 - (0)4]
= {tex}\frac{3 \times 10^{-8}}{4} \times \ 16 \ \times \ 10^{8}{/tex} = 12 J moI-1
