questions.question:
Which one of the following statements is false?
 Work is a state function
 Temperature is a state function
 Change in the state is completely defined when the initial and final states are specified
 Work appears at the boundary of the system
questions.question_raw:
Which one of the following statements is false?

  1. Work is a state function
  2. Temperature is a state function
  3. Change in the state is completely defined when the initial and final states are specified
  4. Work appears at the boundary of the system
questions.explanation: Work is not a state function. State functions are P, T, V, E, H, S, etc. State functions are path independent.

questions.question:
In thermodynamics, a process is called reversible, when:
 surrounding and system change into each other
 there is no boundary between system and surroundings
 the surroundings are always in equilibrium with the system
 the system changes into the surroundings spontaneously
questions.question_raw:
In thermodynamics, a process is called reversible, when:

  1. surrounding and system change into each other
  2. there is no boundary between system and surroundings
  3. the surroundings are always in equilibrium with the system
  4. the system changes into the surroundings spontaneously
questions.explanation: During the reversible process, the internal equilibrium is disturbed only infinitesimally and in the limit, it is not disturbed at all. Thus, virtually the system always remains in the equilibrium state during this process.

questions.question:
If an endothermic reaction is non-spontaneous at freezing point of water and becomes feasible at its boiling point, then:
 $\Delta$H is -ve, $\Delta$S is +ve
 $\Delta$H and $\Delta$S both are +ve
 $\Delta$H and $\Delta$S both are -ve
 $\Delta$H is +ve, $\Delta$S is -ve
questions.question_raw:
If an endothermic reaction is non-spontaneous at freezing point of water and becomes feasible at its boiling point, then:

  1. {tex}\Delta{/tex}H is -ve, {tex}\Delta{/tex}S is +ve
  2. {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S both are +ve
  3. {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S both are -ve
  4. {tex}\Delta{/tex}H is +ve, {tex}\Delta{/tex}S is -ve
questions.explanation: {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S, since {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = +ve at T = 273 K and {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = +ve and at high temperature 373 K, it becomes spontaneous ({tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -ve) even if {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = +ve. This is possible only if {tex}\Delta{/tex}S = +ve because at high temp. T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S > {tex}\Delta{/tex}H to give {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -ve

questions.question:

questions.question_raw:

  1. violates 1st law of thermodynamics
  2. violates 1st law of thermodynamics if Q1 is -ve
  3. violates 1st law of thermodynamics if Q2 is -ve
  4. does not violate 1st law of thermodynamics
questions.explanation: Carnot engine is based on 2nd law of thermodynamics and thus does not violate 1st law of thermodynamics.

options.text: not defined, because pressure is not constant

questions.explanation:
H = U + P V
{tex}\therefore{/tex} H2 - H1 = U2 - U1 + (P2V2 - P1V1)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 30 + (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3) = 44 L atm

questions.question: Three gases A (monoatomic), B2 (diatomic) and C3 (triatomic) are placed separately at same P and T initially. Each one is compressed adiabatically from volume V to $\frac V2$. Which will show higher temperature?
questions.question_raw: Three gases A (monoatomic), B2 (diatomic) and C3 (triatomic) are placed separately at same P and T initially. Each one is compressed adiabatically from volume V to {tex}\frac V2{/tex}. Which will show higher temperature?
questions.explanation:
For adiabatic compression
{tex}T V^{\gamma-1}{/tex} = constant
or {tex}\frac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}=\left[\frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}\right]^{\gamma-1}=\left[\frac{V}{\frac V2}\right]^{\gamma-1}=2^{\gamma-1}{/tex}
For monoatomic: T2 = T1 {tex}\times{/tex} (2)1.6-1 = T1 {tex}\times{/tex} 20.6
For diatomic: T2 = T1 {tex}\times{/tex} (2)1.4-1 = T1 {tex}\times{/tex} 20.4
For triatomic: T2 = T1 {tex}\times{/tex} (2)13..-1 = T1 {tex}\times{/tex} 20.33
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Tmono > Tdi > Ttri

questions.question:

 Cp - Cv for N2 and H2 is equal to R at low pressure
 H2 + $\frac 12$O2 $\rightarrow$ H2O; $\Delta$G = highly -ve but reaction is very slow

 all expansion process lead for work
questions.question_raw:
Select the incorrect statement(s):

  1. Cp - Cv for N2 and H2 is equal to R at low pressure
  2. H2 + {tex}\frac 12{/tex}O2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2O; {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = highly -ve but reaction is very slow
  3. all adiabatic process is isoentropic
  4. all expansion process lead for work
questions.explanation:
At low-pressure gases behave ideal nature.
Negative values of {tex}\Delta{/tex}G suggest thermodynamic feasibility and spontaneity whereas the rate of reaction depends upon the energy of activation.
For a process {tex}\int d S=\int \frac{d q}{T}{/tex}
Thus, for reversible adiabatic process dq = 0 and so dS = 0 but for irreversible adiabatic process dS {tex}\ne{/tex} 0
The free expansion of ideal gas is an expansion with no work.

questions.question: Let W be the work done when a bubble of volume V is formed from a given solution. How much work is required to be done to form a bubble of volume 2 V?
questions.question_raw: Let W be the work done when a bubble of volume V is formed from a given solution. How much work is required to be done to form a bubble of volume 2 V?
questions.explanation:
Work done {tex}\propto{/tex} Surface Area and Surface Area {tex}\propto{/tex} r2
{tex}\propto\left(V^{1 / 3}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left(\because V=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}\right){/tex}
{tex}\propto{/tex} V2/3
{tex}\frac{W_{2}}{W_{1}}=\left(\frac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}\right)^{2 / 3}{/tex} = (2)2/3
= 41/3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} W2 = 41/3 {tex}\times{/tex} W1

questions.question: In which of the P-V diagram, the heat may be absorbed by gas?

questions.explanation:
1. Clockwise cycle on {tex}P-V{/tex} diagram
    {tex} \Delta W_{\text {cyclic }}=+v e=\Delta Q_{\text {cyclic }}{/tex}
    Heat is absorbed
  2. anticlockwise cycle on V-P diagram
    {tex} \Delta W_{\text {cyclic }}=+v e=\Delta Q_{\text {cyclic }}{/tex}
    heat is absorbed
  3. clockwise cycle on {tex}P-V{/tex} diagram
    {tex} \Delta W_{c y c l i c}=+v e=\Delta Q_{\text {cyclic }}{/tex}
    heat is absorbed
  4. clockwise cycle on {tex}V-P{/tex} diagram
    {tex} \Delta W_{\text {cyclic }}=-v e=\Delta Q_{\text {cyclic }}{/tex}
    heat is rejected

questions.question: The change in entropy, $\Delta$S is positive for an endothermic reaction. If enthalpy change $\Delta$H occurs at the same temperature T, then the reaction is feasible:
questions.question_raw: The change in entropy, {tex}\Delta{/tex}S is positive for an endothermic reaction. If enthalpy change {tex}\Delta{/tex}H occurs at the same temperature T, then the reaction is feasible:
questions.explanation:
We know the relation, {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H − T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S, where {tex}\Delta{/tex}G is the change in Gibb's free energy, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H is the change in enthalpy and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S is the change in entropy.
For a feasible reaction, we must have {tex}\Delta{/tex}G < 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S < 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H < T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S, if {tex}\Delta{/tex}S and {tex}\Delta{/tex}H is positive for the reaction.
Hence, the reaction is feasible when {tex}\Delta{/tex}H < T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S.

questions.question: The heat required to raise the temperature of a body by 1K is called:
questions.question_raw: The heat required to raise the temperature of a body by 1K is called:
questions.explanation: The heat required to raise the temperature of a body by 1K is called thermal capacity. In other words, when q is the heat supplied to the body and the temperature raises by 1K, then the thermal capacity of body is q.

questions.question: Considering entropy (S) as a thermodynamic parameter, the criterion for the spontaneity of any process is:
questions.question_raw: Considering entropy (S) as a thermodynamic parameter, the criterion for the spontaneity of any process is:
questions.explanation: {tex}\Delta{/tex}Ssystem + {tex}\Delta{/tex}Ssurroundings be + ve

questions.question: In which of the following processes of neutralization magnitude of $\Delta$Hneutralization is less than that of $\Delta$Hionisation of water?
questions.question_raw: In which of the following processes of neutralization magnitude of {tex}\Delta{/tex}Hneutralization is less than that of {tex}\Delta{/tex}Hionisation of water?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}ion H for water is the enthalpy change for the following process H2O {tex}\to{/tex} H+ + OH-
It is the reverse of the neutralisation of a weak acid by a strong base, less energy or heat is released when compared to the neutralisation of a strong acid by a strong base.
So, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H neutralisation is less than that of {tex}\Delta{/tex}H of ionisation for CH3COOH + NaOH

questions.question: The entropy change for vaporisation of water to steam at 100oC is ________ J K-1 mol-1. Given that heat of vaporisation is 40.8 kJ mol-1.
questions.question_raw: The entropy change for vaporisation of water to steam at 100oC is ________ J K-1 mol-1. Given that heat of vaporisation is 40.8 kJ mol-1.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = {tex}\frac{\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{v}}}{\mathrm{T}}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Hv = 40.8 kJ mol-1
T = 373 K
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}S = {tex}\frac{40.8 \times 10^{3}}{373}{/tex} = 109.38 JK-1mol-1

questions.question: The enthalpies of formation of N2O and NO are 28 and 90 kJ mol-1 respectively. The enthalpy of the reaction, 2N2O(g) + O2(g) $\longrightarrow$ 4NO(g) is equal to:
questions.question_raw: The enthalpies of formation of N2O and NO are 28 and 90 kJ mol-1 respectively. The enthalpy of the reaction, 2N2O(g) + O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 4NO(g) is equal to:
questions.explanation:
First write the balanced chemical; equations for he formation of dinitrogen oxide and nitric oxide.
N2 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}O2 {tex}\to{/tex} N2O; {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 28 kJ ...(i)
{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}N2 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}O2 {tex}\to{/tex} NO; {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 90 kJ ...(ii)
Then multiply second equation with 4 and first equation with 2
2N2 + O2 {tex}\to{/tex} 2N2O; {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 28 kJ ...(iii)
2N2 + 2O2 {tex}\to{/tex} 4NO; {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 90 kJ ...(iv)
Now substract third equation from fourth equation.
2N2O + O2 {tex}\to{/tex} 4NO; {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 304 kJ
Thus, the enthalpy of the reaction 2N2O(g) + O2(g) {tex}\to{/tex} 4NO(g) is 304 kJ.

questions.question: One mole of an ideal gas at 300K is expanded isothermally from an initial volume of 1 litre to 10 litre. The $\Delta$U for this process is: (R = 2cal K-1 mol-1)
questions.question_raw: One mole of an ideal gas at 300K is expanded isothermally from an initial volume of 1 litre to 10 litre. The {tex}\Delta{/tex}U for this process is: (R = 2cal K-1 mol-1)
questions.explanation:
For ideal gas, {tex}\Delta{/tex}U = nCvdT
For isothermal process, dT = 0
Hence, Change in internal energy ({tex}\Delta{/tex}E) is zero during isothermal expansion of a gas.

questions.question: Equal volume of 1M HCl and 1M H2SO4 are neutralized by dilute NaOH solution and x and y kcal of heat are liberated respectively. Which of the following is true?
questions.question_raw: Equal volume of 1M HCl and 1M H2SO4 are neutralized by dilute NaOH solution and x and y kcal of heat are liberated respectively. Which of the following is true?
questions.explanation:
Reaction involved are:
HCl + NaOH {tex}\to{/tex} NaCl + H2O, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H1 = x kcal
H2SO4 + 2NaOH {tex}\to{/tex} Na2SO4 + 2H2O, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H1 = y kcal
Acid-base neutralization reaction for HCl is represented as:
H+ + OH- {tex}\to{/tex} H2O, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H1 = x kcal ...(i)
Acid-base neutralization reaction for H2SO4 is represented as:
2H+ + 2OH- {tex}\to{/tex} 2H2O, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H1 = y kcal ...(ii)
Since reaction (ii) is twice of reaction (i), so is the enthalpies
Thus 2x = y
or x = 0.5y

questions.question: A solution of 200 mL of 1M KOH is added to 200 mL of 1M HCl and the mixture is well shaken. This rise in temperature T1 is noted. The experiment is repeated by using 100 mL of each solution and increase in temperature T2 is again noted. Which of the following is correct?
questions.question_raw: A solution of 200 mL of 1M KOH is added to 200 mL of 1M HCl and the mixture is well shaken. This rise in temperature T1 is noted. The experiment is repeated by using 100 mL of each solution and increase in temperature T2 is again noted. Which of the following is correct?

options.text: T1 is twice as large as T2
T2 is twice as large as T1
T1 is four times as large as T2

questions.explanation:
Heat produced by 200 Meq of K OH + HCl = {tex}\frac{13.7 \times 200}{1000}{/tex} k cal
This is used to raise the temperature of 400 mL solution say by T1
Thus, = {tex}\frac{13.7 \times 200}{1000}{/tex} = 400 {tex}\times{/tex} S {tex}\times{/tex} T1
Now, heat produced by 100 Meq of KOH + HCl = {tex}\frac{13.7 \times 100}{1000}{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\frac{13.7 \times 100}{1000}{/tex} = 200 {tex}\times{/tex} S {tex}\times{/tex} T2
It is evident that T1 = T2.

questions.question: An ideal gas expands in volume from 10-3 m3 to 10-2 m3 at 300 K against a constant pressure of 105N m-2. The work done is:
questions.question_raw: An ideal gas expands in volume from 10-3 m3 to 10-2 m3 at 300 K against a constant pressure of 105N m-2. The work done is:
questions.explanation:
As we know that, work done due to change in volume against constant pressure is given as-
W = -Pext. {tex}\Delta{/tex}V
Given:
Vi = 10-3 m3
Vf = 10-2 m3
Pext. = 105 N/m2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} W = -105 (10-2 - 10-3) = -900 J

questions.question: $\Delta$fH of graphite is 0.23 kJ/mol and $\Delta$fH of diamonds is 1.896 kJ/mol. $\Delta$Htransition from graphite to diamond is:
questions.question_raw: {tex}\Delta{/tex}fH of graphite is 0.23 kJ/mol and {tex}\Delta{/tex}fH of diamonds is 1.896 kJ/mol. {tex}\Delta{/tex}Htransition from graphite to diamond is:
questions.explanation:
The enthalpy change for the transition is equal to the difference in the enthalpy change for the formation of diamond and the enthalpy change for the formation of graphite.
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Hreaction = {tex}\Delta{/tex}fH(diamond) - {tex}\Delta{/tex}fH(graphite)
= 1.896 - 0.23 = 1.666 kJ/mol

questions.question: One mole of a gas occupying 3 dm3 expands against constant external pressure of 1 atm to a volume of 13 dm3. The work done is:
questions.question_raw: One mole of a gas occupying 3 dm3 expands against constant external pressure of 1 atm to a volume of 13 dm3. The work done is:
questions.explanation:
Wexp = -P {tex}\Delta{/tex}V
= -1 {tex}\times{/tex} (13 - 3) = -10 atm dm3

questions.question_raw: For the change, Cdiamond {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cgraphite; {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -1.89 kJ, if 6g of diamond and 6g of graphite are separately burnt to yield CO2, the heat liberated in first case is:

questions.question_raw: Under the same conditions how many mL of 1M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 solutions, respectively when mixed for a total volume of 100 mL produce the highest rise in temperature?
questions.explanation: 50 : 50, because H2SO4 is biprotic acid. It liberates {tex}2{H^ \oplus }{/tex} per mole of acid.

questions.question: 2 mole of ideal gas at 27oC temperature is expanded reversibly from 2 litre to 20 litre. Find entropy change: (R = 2cal/mol K)
questions.question_raw: 2 mole of ideal gas at 27oC temperature is expanded reversibly from 2 litre to 20 litre. Find entropy change: (R = 2cal/mol K)
questions.explanation:
Given n = 2; T = 300K; V1 = 2L; V2 = 20L; R = 2cal/molK
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = {tex}\frac{{q}}{{T}}=\frac{-{W}}{{T}}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = {tex}\frac{-\left(-2.303 n R T \log \left(V_{1} / V_{2}\right)\right)}{T}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = 2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} log (20/2)
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = 9.212 Cal/K/mol

questions.question:
S + $\frac{3}{2}$O2 $\longrightarrow$ SO3 + 2x kcal; SO2 + $\frac{1}{2}$O2 $\longrightarrow$ SO3 + y kcal;
The heat of formation of SO2 is:
questions.question_raw:
S + {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}O2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} SO3 + 2x kcal; SO2 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}O2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} SO3 + y kcal;
The heat of formation of SO2 is:
questions.explanation:
2x - y
{tex}\mathrm{S}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_2{/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} SO3 ...(i) {tex}\Delta{/tex}H1 = -2x - kJ
{tex}\mathrm{SO}_2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2{/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} SO3 ...(ii) {tex}\Delta{/tex}H2 = -ykJ
S + O2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} SO2 ...(iii) {tex}\Delta{/tex}H3 = zkJ
From Hess's law, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H3 + {tex}\Delta{/tex}H2 = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H1
z - y = -2x {tex}\therefore{/tex} z = y - 2x

questions.question: When 1 mole of gas is heated at constant volume, temperature is raised from 298 to 308 K. Heat supplied to the gas is 500 J. Then which statement is correct?
questions.question_raw: When 1 mole of gas is heated at constant volume, temperature is raised from 298 to 308 K. Heat supplied to the gas is 500 J. Then which statement is correct?
questions.explanation:
From first law of thermodynamics:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}U = q + W
In the given problem, q = 500J
W = -p{tex}\Delta{/tex}V = 0, {tex}\Delta{/tex}V = 0
So, {tex}\Delta{/tex}U = q = 500J

questions.question: Enthalpy of CH4 + $\frac{1}{2}$O2 $\longrightarrow$ CH3OH is negative. IF enthalpy of combustion of CH4 and CH3OH are x and y respectively, which relation is correct?
questions.question_raw: Enthalpy of CH4 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}O2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CH3OH is negative. IF enthalpy of combustion of CH4 and CH3OH are x and y respectively, which relation is correct?
questions.explanation:
CH4(g) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CH3OH (l)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = [({tex}\Delta{/tex}H of combustion of CH3OH)] - [({tex}\Delta{/tex}H of combustion of CH4)]
= -[(-y) - (-x)]
= -[-y + x] = y - x
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = negative
y - x = negative
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x > y

questions.question: The internal energy change when a system goes from state A to B is 40 kJ/mol. If the system goes from A to B by a reversible path and returns to state A by an irreversible path. What would be the net change in internal energy?
questions.question_raw: The internal energy change when a system goes from state A to B is 40 kJ/mol. If the system goes from A to B by a reversible path and returns to state A by an irreversible path. What would be the net change in internal energy?
questions.explanation:
Internal energy is a state function. It is not path-dependent.
{tex}\Delta{/tex}U = Uf - Ui
Since the system returns to its initial state, Uf = Ui
Hence, {tex}\Delta{/tex}U = 0

questions.question: The heat of neutralization of a strong base and a strong acid is 57 kJ. The heat released, when 0.5 mole of HNO3 solution is added to 0.20 moles of NaOH solution, is:
questions.question_raw: The heat of neutralization of a strong base and a strong acid is 57 kJ. The heat released, when 0.5 mole of HNO3 solution is added to 0.20 moles of NaOH solution, is:
questions.explanation:
H+ + OH- {tex}\to{/tex} H2O + Heat
One mole of H+ and one mole of OH- give 57 kJ. When 0.5 mole of H+ (from HNO3) reacts with 0.2 mole of OH- and 0.3 mole of H+ remains unreacted.
{tex}\mathop {{H^ + }}\limits_{0.2\,mole} \, + \,\mathop {O{H^ - }}\limits_{0.2\,mole} \, \to \,{H_2}O{/tex}
Heat evolved will be = {tex}\frac{{5 7} \times {0 . 2}}{{1}}{/tex} = 11.4 kJ

questions.question: The molar heat capacity of water at constant pressure P, is 75 JK-1 mol-1. When 1.0 kJ of heat is supplied to 100 g of water which is free to expand, the increase in temperature of water is:
questions.question_raw: The molar heat capacity of water at constant pressure P, is 75 JK-1 mol-1. When 1.0 kJ of heat is supplied to 100 g of water which is free to expand, the increase in temperature of water is:
questions.explanation:
The relationship between the heat supplied, molar heat capacity at constant volume and the temperature change is q = m {tex}\cdot{/tex} Cp{tex}\Delta{/tex}T
q = 1 kJ = 1000 J
Number of moles = m = {tex}\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { molecular } \quad \text { weight }}=\frac{100}{18}{/tex}
Substitute values in the above expression
1000 = {tex}\frac{100}{18} \times{/tex} 75 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}T
Hence, the temperature change is 2.4 K.

questions.question: The enthalpy change for the following reaction, NaOH(aq) + HCl(aq) $\longrightarrow$ NaCl(aq) + H2O(l) is -57 kJ. Predict the value of the enthalphy change in the following reactions: Ba(OH)2 + H2SO4(aq) $\longrightarrow$ BaSO4(s) + 2H2O(l)
questions.question_raw: The enthalpy change for the following reaction, NaOH(aq) + HCl(aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} NaCl(aq) + H2O(l) is -57 kJ. Predict the value of the enthalphy change in the following reactions: Ba(OH)2 + H2SO4(aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} BaSO4(s) + 2H2O(l)

questions.question: At 27oC latent heat of fusion of a compound is 2930 J/mol. Entropy change during fusion is:
questions.question_raw: At 27oC latent heat of fusion of a compound is 2930 J/mol. Entropy change during fusion is:
questions.explanation:
Entropy is a thermodynamic quantity representing the unavailability of a system's thermal energy for conversion into mechanical work, often interpreted as the degree of disorder or randomness in the system.
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Ssystem = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{rev}}}{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{2930}{300}{/tex} = 9.77 J/mol K

questions.question: The heat of atomisation of PH3(g) is 228 kcal mol-1 and that P2H4(g) is 355 kcal mol-1. The energy of the P - P bond is (in kcal):
questions.question_raw: The heat of atomisation of PH3(g) is 228 kcal mol-1 and that P2H4(g) is 355 kcal mol-1. The energy of the P - P bond is (in kcal):
questions.explanation:
The balanced chemical equation for the atomization of phosphine is PH3 {tex}\to{/tex} P + 3H; {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 228
ep-H = {tex}\frac{228}{3}{/tex}
The balanced chemical equation for the atomization of P2H4(g) is H2P - PH2 {tex}\to{/tex} 2P + 4H; {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 355
ep-p + 4 {tex}\times{/tex} ep-H = 355
ep-p + 4 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{228}{3}{/tex} = 355
ep-p = 51
The energy of the P - P bond is 51 kcal.

questions.question: At 1 atm pressure, $\Delta$S = 75 J K-1 mol-1; $\Delta$H = 30 kJ mol-1. The temperature of the reaction at equilibrium is:
questions.question_raw: At 1 atm pressure, {tex}\Delta{/tex}S = 75 J K-1  mol-1; {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 30 kJ mol-1. The temperature of the reaction at equilibrium is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
At equilibrium, {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} T = {tex}\frac{\Delta \mathrm{H}}{\Delta \mathrm{S}}=\frac{30 \times 10^{3}}{75}{/tex} = 400 K

questions.question: 1 gram sample of NH4NO3 is decomposed in a bomb calorimeter. The temperature of the calorimeter increases by 6.12 K. The heat capacity of the system is 1.23 kJ/g-deg. What is the molar heat of decomposition for NH4NO3?
questions.question_raw: 1 gram sample of NH4NO3 is decomposed in a bomb calorimeter. The temperature of the calorimeter increases by 6.12 K. The heat capacity of the system is 1.23 kJ/g-deg. What is the molar heat of decomposition for NH4NO3?
questions.explanation:
Heat of decomposition, {tex}\Delta{/tex}E = ms {tex}\Delta{/tex}T
= 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.23 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.12 = 7.5276 kJ
Molar heat of decomposition for NH4NO3
= 7.5276 {tex}\times{/tex} 80 = 602.2 kJ/mol

questions.question: For a reversible process at T = 300 K, the volume of 2 mole of ideal gas is increased from 1 litre to 10 litre, the $\Delta$H for isothermal change is:
questions.question_raw: For a reversible process at T = 300 K, the volume of 2 mole of ideal gas is increased from 1 litre to 10 litre, the {tex}\Delta{/tex}H for isothermal change is:
questions.explanation:
For isothermal process, dT = 0
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = ncp {tex}\cdot{/tex} dT
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = ncp {tex}\times{/tex} 0
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 0

questions.question:
What is the entropy change (in JK-1 mol-1) when one mole of ice is converted into water at 0oC?
(The enthalpy change for the conversion of ice to liquid water is 6.0 kJ mol-1 at 0oC)
questions.question_raw:
What is the entropy change (in JK-1 mol-1) when one mole of ice is converted into water at 0oC?
(The enthalpy change for the conversion of ice to liquid water is 6.0 kJ mol-1 at 0oC)
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 6.0 kJ mol-1
= 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 J mol-1
T = 0oC = 273 K
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = {tex}\frac{\Delta H}{T}{/tex} (For a reversible process)
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = {tex}\frac{6 \times 10^{3} J m o l^{-1}}{273 K}{/tex} = 21.98 JK-1 mol-1

questions.question: For the reaction; 3O2 $\longrightarrow$ 2O3; $\Delta$H = +ve. We can say that:

In endothermic reaction reactant is more stable than product.

questions.question: Standard enthalpy and standard entropy change for the oxidation of NH3 at 298 K, are -382.64 kJ mol-1 and -145.6 J mol-1 respectively. Standard Gibbs energy change for the same reaction at 298 K is:
questions.question_raw: Standard enthalpy and standard entropy change for the oxidation of NH3 at 298 K, are -382.64 kJ mol-1 and -145.6 J mol-1 respectively. Standard Gibbs energy change for the same reaction at 298 K is:
questions.explanation: -339.3 kJ mol-1

questions.question: The lattice energy of solid NaCl is 180 kcal/mol. The dissolution of the solid in water in the form of ions is endothermic to the extent of 1 kcal/mol. If the hydration energies of Na+ and Cl- are in the ratio 6 : 5, what is the enthalpy of hydration of Na+ ion?
questions.question_raw: The lattice energy of solid NaCl is 180 kcal/mol. The dissolution of the solid in water in the form of ions is endothermic to the extent of 1 kcal/mol. If the hydration energies of Na+ and Cl- are in the ratio 6 : 5, what is the enthalpy of hydration of Na+ ion?
questions.explanation:
The enthalpy change for dissolution is the sum of the lattice enthalpy and the enthalpy change for hydration
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Hsol = {tex}\Delta{/tex}Hlattice + {tex}\Delta{/tex}Hhyd
Substitute values in the above expression,
1 = 180 + {tex}\Delta{/tex}Hh
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Hh = -179 kcal
The enthalpy of hydration for sodium chloride is the sum of the enthalpies of hydration for sodium ions and chloride ions.
{tex}\Delta{/tex}HNa+(h) + {tex}\Delta{/tex}HCl-(h) = -179 kcal
The hydration energies of sodium ion and chloride ions are in the ratio 6 : 5.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}HNa+(h) = {tex}\frac{6 \times(-179)}{11}{/tex} = -97.64 kcal

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is wrong?
 Water in a beaker be made to boil by placing it in a bath of boiling water.
 Water can be made to boil without heating.
 Two pieces of ice stick to each other if they are pressed against each other and released due to regelation.
 Ice can be made to sublimate.
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following statements is wrong?

  1. Water in a beaker be made to boil by placing it in a bath of boiling water.
  2. Water can be made to boil without heating.
  3. Two pieces of ice stick to each other if they are pressed against each other and released due to regelation.
  4. Ice can be made to sublimate.
questions.explanation: The water in beaker will be heated to 100oC but will not boil as for boiling it requires latent heat of evaporation. Water can be made to boil by lowering the external pressure to the extent that its b.pt. becomes equal to room temperature. Regelation involves melting of ice under pressure and resolidification on releasing pressure. Ice can be sublimed by reducing external pressure to its vapour pressure.

questions.question: The enthalpy changes of formation of the gaseous oxides of nitrogen (N2O and NO) are positive because of:
questions.question_raw: The enthalpy changes of formation of the gaseous oxides of nitrogen (N2O and NO) are positive because of:

options.text: the tendency of oxygen to form O2-
the high electron affinity of oxygen atoms
the high electron affinity of nitrogen atoms
the high bond energy of the nitrogen molecule

questions.explanation: Due to high bond energy of N {tex}\equiv{/tex} N, more heat is absorbed to break up N3 molecule.

questions.question: The work done by one mole of ideal gas during an adiabatic process is not given by:
questions.question_raw: The work done by one mole of ideal gas during an adiabatic process is not given by:
questions.explanation:
w = {tex}\frac{{n R}}{\gamma-1} \times{/tex} [Tf - Ti]
{tex}\frac{{n} {R}}{(\gamma-1)} \times \frac{{P}_{\mathrm{f}} {V}_{\mathrm{f}}-{P}_{\mathrm{i}} {V}_{{i}}}{{n} {R}}=\frac{{P}_{\mathrm{f}} \cdot {V}_{{f}}-{P}_{{i}} \cdot {V}_{{i}}}{\gamma-1}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} PV = nRT)
{tex}\frac{{P}_{\mathrm{f}} \cdot {V}_{\mathrm{f}}}{\gamma-{1}}\left[{1}-\left[\frac{{P}_{{i}} \cdot {V}_{{i}}}{{P}_{\mathrm{f}} \cdot {V}_{\mathrm{f}}}\right]\right]{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{P}_{\mathrm{f}} \cdot {V}_{\mathrm{f}}}{\gamma-{1}}\left[1-\frac{{P}_{{i}}}{{P}_{\mathrm{f}}} \times\left[\frac{{P}_{\mathrm{f}}}{{P}_{\mathrm{i}}}\right]^{\frac{1}{\gamma}}\right]{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} PV{tex}\gamma{/tex} = constant]
{tex}\frac{{P}_{\mathrm{f}} \cdot {V}_{\mathrm{f}}}{\gamma-1}\left[1-\frac{{P}_{\mathrm{i}}^{1-\frac{1}{7}}}{{P}_{\mathrm{f}}^{1-\frac{1}{7}}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{P}_{\mathrm{f}} \cdot {V}_{\mathrm{f}}}{\gamma-1}\left[1-\left[\frac{{P}_{\mathrm{i}}}{{P}_{\mathrm{f}}}\right]^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]{/tex}

questions.question: If the pressure on density of a diatomic gas $\left(\gamma=\frac{7}{5}\right)$ in adiabatic process changes from (P, e) tp (P', e'), where P = 32P', then $\frac{e}{e^{\prime}}$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If the pressure on density of a diatomic gas {tex}\left(\gamma=\frac{7}{5}\right){/tex} in adiabatic process changes from (P, e) tp (P', e'), where P = 32P', then {tex}\frac{e}{e^{\prime}}{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
For an adiabatic process,
PV{tex}\gamma{/tex} = constant
P {tex}\rho^{-\gamma}{/tex} = constant
{tex}\frac{{P}_{2}}{{P}_{1}}=\left(\frac{\rho_{1}}{\rho_{2}}\right)^{-\gamma}=\left(\frac{1}{32}\right)^{-7 / 5}{/tex} = (32)7/5 = 128

questions.question: Specific heat of a substance at m.pt. (or b.pt. or for isothermal process) and specific heat of a substance during adiabatic change respectively are:
questions.question_raw: Specific heat of a substance at m.pt. (or b.pt. or for isothermal process) and specific heat of a substance during adiabatic change respectively are:
questions.explanation:
The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of unit mass of a substance by one degree celsius is known as specific heat. It is given by the ratio {tex}\left(\frac{\partial \mathrm{H}}{\partial \mathrm{T}}\right){/tex}.
When at constant pressure, one phase of a substance is in equilibrium with other phase (during melting or boiling), the heat {tex}\partial{/tex}H is supplied to the system, and the temperature change {tex}\partial{/tex}T is zero as the heat supplied is utilized for the phase transition. Hence, the specific heat is infinite. This is also true for the isothermal process. During adiabatic process, no heat is supplied to system. The term {tex}\partial{/tex}H is zero. Hence the specific heat is zero.

questions.question: The standard heat of combustion of solid boron is equal to:
questions.question_raw: The standard heat of combustion of solid boron is equal to:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\Delta{/tex}fHo (B2O3)
The balanced chemical equation for the formation of B2​O3​ from B and oxygen is as shown below.
2B + {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}O2 {tex}\to{/tex} B2O3; {tex}\Delta{/tex}fHo of B
The balanced chemical equation for the combustion of 1 mole of B is as shown below.
(B + {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}O2 {tex}\to{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}B2O3); {tex}\Delta{/tex}cHo of B
Hence, {tex}\Delta{/tex}comb Ho of boron = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}fHo of B2O3
Thus, standard heat of combustion of solid boron is equal to one half the standard heat of formation of B2​O3​.

{tex}\Delta{/tex}Cp ​ represents the change in the heat capacity at constant pressure. It is equal to the difference in the heat capacities of the products and the reactants.
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Cp ​= Cp ​ ofproducts - Cp of reactants.
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Cp​ for change N2(g) + 3H2(g) = 2NH3(g) is given by the following expression.
{tex}\Delta{/tex}CP = 2{tex}{C_{pN{H_3}}}{/tex} - (CPN2 + 3{tex}{C_{p{H_2}}}{/tex})

questions.question:
The temperature increase is not noticed in which of the following processes on providing heat to system?
 Fusion at melting point
 Phase transition at transition temperature
 Boiling at boiling point
 A reaction in chemical equilibrium
questions.question_raw:
The temperature increase is not noticed in which of the following processes on providing heat to system?

  1. Fusion at melting point
  2. Phase transition at transition temperature
  3. Boiling at boiling point
  4. A reaction in chemical equilibrium
questions.explanation:
At melting point, the heat given is used for increasing intermolecular force of attraction. Hence, temperature doesn't increase.
During phase transition, the composition of phase changes by adding heat in the system.
Boiling at boiling point makes the vapour superheated.
By adding heat in chemical equilibrium, the enthalpy of reaction changes so the temperature of the system varies.

questions.question: For an adiabatic expansion of a perfect gas $\frac{\Delta P}{P}$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: For an adiabatic expansion of a perfect gas {tex}\frac{\Delta P}{P}{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
For adiabatic process: PV{tex}\gamma{/tex} = constant
Taking derivative
dPV{tex}\gamma{/tex} + {tex}\gamma {P} {V}^{\gamma-1}{/tex} dV = 0
dPV{tex}\gamma{/tex} = {tex}-\gamma {P} {V}^{\gamma-1}{/tex} dV
{tex}\frac{{d} {P}}{{P}}=-\gamma \frac{{V}^{\gamma-{1}}}{{V}^{\gamma}}{/tex} dV
{tex}\frac{\Delta {P}}{{P}}=-\gamma \frac{\Delta {V}}{{V}}{/tex}

questions.question: An ideal gas heat engine operates in Carnot cycle between 227oC and 127oC. It absorbs 6 $\times$ 104 cal of heat at high temperature. Mass of heat converted to work is:
questions.question_raw: An ideal gas heat engine operates in Carnot cycle between 227oC and 127oC. It absorbs 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 cal of heat at high temperature. Mass of heat converted to work is:
questions.explanation:
Here, T1 = 227oC = 227 + 273 = 500 K
T2 = 127oC = 127 + 273 = 400 K
Q1 = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 cal
As {tex}\frac{Q_{2}}{Q_{1}}=\frac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Q2 = {tex}\frac{T_{2}}{T_{1}} \times{/tex} Q1 = {tex}\frac{400}{500} \times{/tex} 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 104
= 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 cal
W = Q1 - Q2 = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 - 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 104
= 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 cal

3{tex}\gamma{/tex} - 3 = {tex}\gamma{/tex}
2{tex}\gamma{/tex} = 3, {tex}\gamma{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: For a gas having molar mass M, specific heat at constant pressure can be given as:

CP - CV = R
{tex}\therefore{/tex} CP - CV = {tex}\frac{{R}}{{M}}{/tex}
Also, {tex}\frac{{C}_{{p}}}{{C}_{{v}}}=\gamma{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} CP - {tex}\frac{{C}_{{p}}}{\gamma}=\frac{{R}}{{M}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} CP = {tex}\frac{{R} \cdot \gamma}{{M}(\gamma-1)}{/tex}

First calculate the milliequivalents of H2​SO4​
Meq. of H2SO4 = 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 = 10
Then, calculate the milliequivalets of KOH
Meq. of KOH = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 = 10
Now, 1000 milliequivalents of KOH on neutralization liberate 573000 J of heat.
Hence, the heat liberated by the neutralization of 10 milliequivalents of KOH is
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Heat liberated = {tex}\frac{57.3 \times 10^{3} \times 10}{1000}{/tex} = 573 J

questions.question: One mole of an ideal gas is compressed isothermally to half of its initial volume and then heated to twice of its temperature under isochoric condition, the change in entropy is:
questions.question_raw: One mole of an ideal gas is compressed isothermally to half of its initial volume and then heated to twice of its temperature under isochoric condition, the change in entropy is:
questions.explanation:
The expression of entropy is given as
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = 2.303 Cvlog {tex}\frac{{T}_{2}}{{T}_{1}}{/tex} + 2.303 R log {tex}\frac{{V}_{2}}{{V}_{1}}{/tex}
We have T2 = 2T1 and V1 = 2V2
So we get,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = 2.303 Cvlog {tex}\frac{2}{1}{/tex} + 2.303 R log {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Or we can write it as
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = (Cv - R) In 2

questions.question: The specific heat of a gas at constant pressure and constant volume are 0.125 and 0.075 cal/g respectively. Molar mass of gas is:
questions.question_raw: The specific heat of a gas at constant pressure and constant volume are 0.125 and 0.075 cal/g respectively. Molar mass of gas is:
questions.explanation:
The formula relating the molar mass of gas and the specfic heat constant is:
CP - CV = {tex}\frac{R}{M}{/tex}
Here, R is the gas constant whose value is around 8 in SI unit and 800 in the given unit and M is the molar mass of the gas.
Let us substitute the values in order to find the answer
{tex}\because{/tex} CP - CV = {tex}\frac{R}{M}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.125 - 0.075 = {tex}\frac{800}{M}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.05 = {tex}\frac{800}{M}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} M = 800 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.05
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} M = 40

The gas is allowed to expanded adiabatically to a temperature T2 by releasing the piston suddenly. If L1 and L2 are lengths of the gas columns before and after expansion respectively, then T1/T2 is given by:
questions.question_raw: A monoatomic ideal gas at temperature T1, is enclosed in a cylinder with a frictionless piston. The gas is allowed to expanded adiabatically to a temperature T2 by releasing the piston suddenly. If L1 and L2 are lengths of the gas columns before and after expansion respectively, then T1/T2 is given by:
questions.explanation:
During adiabatic expansion, we know
{tex}T V^{\gamma-1}{/tex} = constant or {tex}T_{1} V_{1}^{\gamma-1}=T_{2} V_{2}^{\gamma-1}{/tex}
For a monoatomic gas {tex}\gamma=\frac{5}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}=\left(\frac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}\right)^{\gamma-1}=\left(\frac{A L_{2}}{A L_{1}}\right)^{(5 / 3)-1}{/tex}
(A = Area of cross-section of piston)
= {tex}\left(\frac{L_{2}}{L_{1}}\right)^{2 / 3}{/tex}

questions.question: A given process on an ideal gas has dw = 0 and dQ = -ve, then for the gas:
questions.question_raw: A given process on an ideal gas has dw = 0 and dQ = -ve, then for the gas:
questions.explanation:
Given: dW = 0 and dQ < 0
From first law of thermodynamics,
dQ = dU + dW
We have, dQ = dU ({tex}\because{/tex} dW = 0)
But (dQ < 0)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} dU < 0
NCv {tex}\Delta{/tex}T < 0
or {tex}\Delta{/tex}T < 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The temperature will decrease.

questions.question: How many times a diatomic gas should be expanded adiabatically to reduce its root mean square speed to half?
questions.question_raw: How many times a diatomic gas should be expanded adiabatically to reduce its root mean square speed to half?
questions.explanation:
As, mrms = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{3 {R} {T}}{-{M}}}=\frac{\left({v}_{\mathrm{rms}}\right)_{1}}{\left({v}_{\mathrm{rms}}\right)_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{{T}_{{1}}}{{T}_{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}{T}_{1} {V}_{1}^{\gamma-1}={T}_{2} {V}_{2}^{\gamma-1}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\left({v}_{{r m s}}\right)_{1}}{\left({v}_{\mathrm{rms}}\right)_{2}}=\left(\frac{{V}_{2}}{{V}_{1}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\frac{{V}_{2}}{{V}_{1}}\right]^{\frac{7}{{5}}-1}{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\frac{{V}_{2}}{{V}_{1}}\right]^{\frac{2}{6} \times \frac{1}{2}}=\left(\frac{{V}_{2}}{{V}_{1}}\right)^{\frac{1}{6}}=\left(\frac{{V}_{2}}{{V}_{1}}\right)^{5}{/tex}
= (2)5 = 32

questions.question: 1 eq. of strong acid (HC) and 1 eq. of strong base (DOH) on complete neutralisation releases 13.5 kcal. When 1 eq. of H2 A is completely neutralised with strong base (DOH), heat released is 13 kcal. When 1 eq. of B(OH)2 is completely neutralised with strong acid HC, heat released is 10 kcal. The heat released during complete neutralisation of 1 mole H2 A and 1 mole B(OH)2 is:
questions.question_raw: 1 eq. of strong acid (HC) and 1 eq. of strong base (DOH) on complete neutralisation releases 13.5 kcal. When 1 eq. of H2 A is completely neutralised with strong base (DOH), heat released is 13 kcal. When 1 eq. of B(OH)2 is completely neutralised with strong acid HC, heat released is 10 kcal. The heat released during complete neutralisation of 1 mole H2 A and 1 mole B(OH)2 is:
questions.explanation:
MH + NOH {tex}\to{/tex} MN + H2O
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H1 = 13.5k cal
H2A + 2NOH {tex}\to{/tex} N2A + 2H2O
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H2 = 13k cal {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 26 k cal
B(OH)2 + 2MH {tex}\to{/tex} BM2 + 2H2O
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H3 = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 20 k cal
H2A + B(OH)2 {tex}\to{/tex} AB + 2H2O
2H2O + 2MN {tex}\to{/tex} 2MH + 2NOH
-{tex}\Delta{/tex}1 = -13.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2
H2A + 2NOH {tex}\to{/tex} N2A + 2NOH
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H2 = 26 k cal
B(OH)2 + 2MH {tex}\to{/tex} BM2 + 2H2O
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H3 = 20 k cal
-{tex}\Delta{/tex}H1 + {tex}\Delta{/tex}H2 + {tex}\Delta{/tex}H3
26 + 20 - 27 = 19 k cal

{tex}\frac{T_{1} T_{2}\left(P_{1} V_{1}+P_{2} V_{2}\right)}{P_{1} V_{1} T_{2}+P_{2} V_{2} T_{1}}{/tex}

questions.question: 70 calories of heat is required to raise the temperature of 2 mole of ideal gas at constant pressure from 30oC to 35oC. The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of same gas through 30oC to 35oC at constant volume is:
questions.question_raw: 70 calories of heat is required to raise the temperature of 2 mole of ideal gas at constant pressure from 30oC to 35oC. The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of same gas through 30oC to 35oC at constant volume is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Q = n{tex}\cdot{/tex}Cp{tex}\cdot{/tex}{tex}\Delta{/tex}T ... as gas is heated at constant pressure from given data, n = 2 moles & {tex}\Delta{/tex}Q = 70 cal
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 70 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} Cp {tex}\times{/tex} (35 - 30)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Cp = 7 cal/g mol/oC
As Cp - Cv = R
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Cv = Cp - R = 7 - 2 = 5 cal/g mol/oC
{tex}\therefore{/tex} as {tex}\Delta{/tex}Q = n Cv {tex}\Delta{/tex}T ... To heat gas at constant volume
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}Q = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} (35 - 30) = 50 cal

Heat required Q1 = (1.1 + 0.02) {tex}\times{/tex} 103 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} (80 - 15)
= 72800 cal
Heat given it 'm' (in kg) steam is condensed
Q2 = (m {tex}\times{/tex} 540 {tex}\times{/tex} 103) + m {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 {tex}\times{/tex} (100 - 80)
Equating Q1 = Q2 we get
m = 0.130 kg

questions.question: In which of the following cases entropy decreases?
questions.question_raw: In which of the following cases entropy decreases?
questions.explanation:
Sometimes it is hard to follow the heat, but it is easy to decide if the disorder increases or decreases. The entropy increases whenever heat flows from a hot object to a cold object.
It increases when the ice melts, water is heated, water boils, water evaporates. Solids change to liquid, expansion of gas and dissolution of crystal are examples of increasing disorders or increasing entropy. In Polymerization the number of molecules present is decreasing, so the randomness or entropy also decreases.

questions.question: In which case, a spontaneous reaction is possible at any temperature?
questions.question_raw: In which case, a spontaneous reaction is possible at any temperature?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H - ve, {tex}\Delta{/tex}S + ve
For a spontaneous process {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -ve
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G < 0
Also, {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -ve
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = +ve
Thus, the given condition reveals {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -ve

questions.question: In a reaction, $\Delta$H and $\Delta$S both are positive. In which of the following cases, the reaction would not be spontaneous?
questions.question_raw: In a reaction, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S both are positive. In which of the following cases, the reaction would not be spontaneous?
questions.explanation:
In a reaction {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S both are positive. In the following cases, the reaction would not be spontaneous
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H > T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S:
The reaction is non spontaneous
Due to this {tex}\Delta{/tex}G > 0
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
The reaction is at equilibrium
Due to this {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = 0
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = {tex}\frac{\Delta H}{T}{/tex}
The reaction is at equilibrium
Due to this {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = 0
But the reaction will be spontaneous when {tex}\Delta{/tex}H < T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S.
Due to this {tex}\Delta{/tex}G < 0

Thus, Enthalpy of sublimation = Enthalpy of fusion + Enthalpy of vaporisation.

questions.question: Which is an extensive property of the system?
questions.question_raw: Which is an extensive property of the system?
questions.explanation:
The properties which are mass independent are intensive properties and those which are mass dependent are extensive properties.
Volume depends upon the amount of matter present in the system. Hence, it is an extensive property.
Temperature, refractive index and viscosity are independent of the amount of matter present in the system. Hence, these are intensive properties.

questions.question: An exothermic reaction is one in which the reacting substances:
questions.question_raw: An exothermic reaction is one in which the reacting substances:

options.text: have more energy than the products
are at a higher temperature than the products
have less energy than the products
have as much energy as the products

questions.explanation:
An exothermic reaction is a reaction in which overall energy is released.
Since, net energy of a reaction is given by,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = (energy of products) - (energy of reactants)
Since energy released means {tex}\Delta{/tex}H < 0
Therefore, the reacting substance has more energy compared to that of products formed.

Thus, a well stopped thermos flask containing ice an example for isolated system.

questions.explanation:
The entropy of a system is a measure of randomness or disorder of the molecules.
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{rev}}}{\mathrm{T}}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S > 0, Increase in randomness, heat is absorbed
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S < 0, Decrease in randomness, heat is evolved
Therefore, if {tex}\Delta{/tex}S > 0 means that the system becomes more disordered.

questions.question: An ideal gas is taken through the cycle A $\to$ B $\to$ C $\to$ A as shown in the figure. If the net heat supplied to gas in the cycle is 5 J, then work done by the gas in the process C $\to$ A is:
questions.question_raw: An ideal gas is taken through the cycle A {tex}\to{/tex} B {tex}\to{/tex} C {tex}\to{/tex} A as shown in the figure. If the net heat supplied to gas in the cycle is 5 J, then work done by the gas in the process C {tex}\to{/tex} A is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}WAB = p{tex}\Delta{/tex}V = (10) (2 - 1) = 10 J
{tex}\Delta{/tex}WBC = 0 (as V = constant)
From first law of thermodynamics
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Q = {tex}\Delta{/tex}W + {tex}\Delta{/tex}U
{tex}\Delta{/tex}U = 0 (process ABCA is cyclic)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}Q = {tex}\Delta{/tex}WAB + {tex}\Delta{/tex}WBC + {tex}\Delta{/tex}WCA
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}WCA = {tex}\Delta{/tex}Q - {tex}\Delta{/tex}WAB - {tex}\Delta{/tex}WBC
= 5 - 10 - 0 = -5J

questions.question: The net work done through a series of changes reported in figure at the end of cycle for an ideal gas is equal to:
questions.question_raw: The net work done through a series of changes reported in figure at the end of cycle for an ideal gas is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Net work done = Area covered by the cycle (clockwise)
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [(4P - P) {tex}\times{/tex} (3V - V)]
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 6 PV
= +3 PV

questions.question: The net work done through a series of changes reported in the figure for the ideal gas is:
questions.question_raw: The net work done through a series of changes reported in the figure for the ideal gas is:
questions.explanation:
Net work done {tex}\Delta{/tex}W = area covered by P − V curve = area of {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABE + area of square BCEF + area of {tex}\Delta{/tex}CDF
= -[{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (3 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 - 1 {tex}\times{/tex}105{tex}\times{/tex} (3 - 1)] + 3 {tex}\times{/tex}105 {tex}\times{/tex}(5 - 3) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [(1 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 - 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 105) {tex}\times{/tex} (6 - 5)]
= - (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 + 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 - 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 105)
= -7{tex}\times{/tex}105 J

questions.question: The slopes of isothermal and adiabatic curves are related as:

Isothermal curve slope = Adiabatic curve slope
Isothermal curve slope = $\gamma$ $\times$ Adiabatic curve slope
Adiabatic curve slope = $\frac{1}{2} \times$ Isothermal curve slope

questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{\text { Slope of adiabatic curve }}{\text { Slope of isothermal curve }}=\frac{(\mathrm{d} \mathrm{P} / \mathrm{dV})_{\mathrm{adi}}}{(\mathrm{dP} / \mathrm{dV})_{\mathrm{iso}}}=+\gamma{/tex}
So, slope to adiabatic curve is {tex}\gamma{/tex} times of isothermal curve, as clear also from figure.

questions.question: The true statement amongst the following is:
questions.question_raw: The true statement amongst the following is:

options.text: Both $\Delta$S and S are functions of temperature.
S is not a function of temperature but $\Delta$S is a function of temperature.
S is a function of temperature but $\Delta$S is not a function of temperature.
Both S and $\Delta$S are not functions of temperature.

questions.explanation:
A system at a higher temperature has greater entropy (randomness). S and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S are related with T as:
ST = {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{\mathrm{T}} \frac{\mathrm{n} \mathrm{C} \cdot \mathrm{dT}}{\mathrm{T}} \Delta \mathrm{S}=\int \frac{d q}{\mathrm{~T}}{/tex}
Thus both S and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S are function of temperature.

questions.question:
If 100 mole of H2O2 decomposes at 1 bar and 300 K, the work done (kJ) by one mole of O2(g) as it expands against 1 bar pressure is:
2H2O2(l) $ \rightleftharpoons $ H2O(l) + O2(g)
(R = 83 JK-1 mol-1)
questions.question_raw:
If 100 mole of H2O2 decomposes at 1 bar and 300 K, the work done (kJ) by one mole of O2(g) as it expands against 1 bar pressure is:
2H2O2(l) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H2O(l) + O2(g)
(R = 83 JK-1 mol-1)
questions.explanation:
2H2O2(l) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} q + 2H2O(l) + O2(g)
w = -Pext({tex}\Delta{/tex}V) = {tex}-n_{\mathrm{O}_{2}} R T{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} 100 mole H2O2 on decomposition give 50 mole O2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} w = -(50) (8.3) (300) = -124500 J = -124.5 kJ

questions.question: A reaction at 1 bar is non-spontaneous at low temperature but becomes spontaneous at high temperature. Identify the correct statement about the reaction among the following:
questions.question_raw: A reaction at 1 bar is non-spontaneous at low temperature but becomes spontaneous at high temperature. Identify the correct statement about the reaction among the following:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta G=\Delta H-T \Delta S{/tex}
For a spontaneous reaction {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -ve
which is possible when both {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S are positive at high temperature and hence the reaction becomes spontaneous.

questions.question: Which of the following statements/relationships is not correct in thermodynamic changes?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following statements/relationships is not correct in thermodynamic changes?

options.text: For a system of constant volume heat involved directly changes to internal energy
w = -nRT ln$\frac{\mathrm{V}_{2}}{\mathrm{~V}_{1}}$ (isothermal reversible expansion of an ideal gas)
w = nRT ln$\frac{\mathrm{V}_{2}}{\mathrm{~V}_{1}}$ (isothermal reversible expansion of an ideal gas)

questions.explanation:
For isothermal reversible expansion.
w = -nRT ln{tex}\frac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}{/tex}

questions.question: Lattice enthalpy and enthalpy of solution of NaCl are 788 kJ mol-1 and 4 kJ mol-1, respectively. The hydration enthalpy of NaCl is:
questions.question_raw: Lattice enthalpy and enthalpy of solution of NaCl are 788 kJ mol-1 and 4 kJ mol-1, respectively. The hydration enthalpy of NaCl is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta_{\text {sol }} {H}^{\circ}=\Delta_{\text {lattice }} {H}^{\circ}+\Delta_{{Hyd} .} {H}^{\circ}{/tex}
4 = 788 + {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{Hyd}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta_{{Hyd} .} {H}^{\circ}{/tex} = -784 kJ mol-1

questions.question:
For one mole of an ideal gas, which of these statements must be true?
 U and H each depends only on temperature
 Compressibility factor z is not equal to 1
 CP,m - CV,m = R
 dU = CVdT for any process
questions.question_raw:
For one mole of an ideal gas, which of these statements must be true?

  1. U and H each depends only on temperature
  2. Compressibility factor z is not equal to 1
  3. CP,m - CV,m = R
  4. dU = CVdT for any process
questions.explanation:
1. For ideal gas U and H are function of temperature.
  2. Compressibility factor for an ideal gas is 1.
  3. CP - CV = R
  4. {tex}\Delta{/tex}U = CVdT for all processes

questions.question: If enthalpy of atomization for Br2(l) is x kJ/mol and bond enthalpy for Br2 is y kJ/mol, the relation between them:
questions.question_raw: If enthalpy of atomization for Br2(l) is x kJ/mol and bond enthalpy for Br2 is y kJ/mol, the relation between them:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Hatomisattion = {tex}\Delta{/tex}Hvap + Bond energy
Hence x > y

questions.question: The difference between $\Delta$H and $\Delta$U($\Delta$H - $\Delta$U), when the combustion of one mole of heptane (I) is carried out at a temperature T, is equal to:
questions.question_raw: The difference between {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and {tex}\Delta{/tex}U({tex}\Delta{/tex}H - {tex}\Delta{/tex}U), when the combustion of one mole of heptane (I) is carried out at a temperature T, is equal to:
questions.explanation:
C7H16(l) + 11O2(g) {tex}\xrightarrow{\Delta }{/tex} 7CO2(g) + 8H2O(l)
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H - {tex}\Delta{/tex}U = {tex}\Delta{/tex}ngRT
{tex}\Delta{/tex}ng = no. of moles of product in gaseous state - no. of moles of reactant in gaseous state.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}ng = -4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - {tex}\Delta{/tex}U = -4RT

questions.question: For a reaction, A(g) $\rightarrow$ A(l); $\Delta$H= -3RT. The correct statement for the reaction is:
questions.question_raw: For a reaction, A(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} A(l); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H= -3RT. The correct statement for the reaction is:
questions.explanation:
A(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} A(l)
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}U + {tex}\Delta{/tex}ngRT
Given, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -3RT
Here
{tex}\Delta{/tex}ng = np - nr = 0 - 1 = -1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}U + {tex}\Delta{/tex}RT
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -3RT = {tex}\Delta{/tex}U - RT
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -3RT + RT = {tex}\Delta{/tex}U
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -2RT = {tex}\Delta{/tex}U
= {tex}|\Delta H|>|\Delta U|{/tex}

questions.question: The heat of atomization of methane and ethane are 360 kJ/ mol and 620 kJ/mol, respectively. The longest wavelength of light capable of breaking the C-C bond is (Avogadro number = 6.02 $\times$ 1023, h = 6.62 $\times$ 10-34 Js):
questions.question_raw: The heat of atomization of methane and ethane are 360 kJ/ mol and 620 kJ/mol, respectively. The longest wavelength of light capable of breaking the C-C bond is (Avogadro number = 6.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023, h = 6.62 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 Js):
questions.explanation:
In CH4, 4 {tex}\times{/tex} BE(C - H) = 360 kJ/mol
{tex}\therefore{/tex} BE(C - H) = 90 kJ/mol
In C2H6, BE(C - C) + 6 {tex}\times{/tex} BE(C - H) = 620 kJ/mol
{tex}\therefore{/tex} BE(C - C) = 80kJ/mol
{tex}\therefore{/tex} BE(C - C) = {tex}\frac{80 \times 10^{3}}{6.023 \times 10^{23}}{/tex} J/mol
Now, E = {tex}\frac {hc}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8} \times 6.023 \times 10^{23}}{80 \times 10^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1.49 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 n ({tex}\because{/tex} 1 nm = 10-9 m)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1.49 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 nm

questions.question: The standard enthalpy of formation of NH3 is -46.0 kJ/ mol. If the enthalpy of formation of H2 from its atoms is -436 kJ/mol and that of N2 is -712 kJ/mol, the average bond enthalpy of N-H bond in NH3 is:
questions.question_raw: The standard enthalpy of formation of NH3 is -46.0 kJ/ mol. If the enthalpy of formation of H2 from its atoms is -436 kJ/mol and that of N2 is -712 kJ/mol, the average bond enthalpy of N-H bond in NH3 is:
questions.explanation:
Given: {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}N2 + {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}H2 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} NH3; {tex}\Delta_f{/tex}H = -46.0 kJ/mol
H + H {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H2; {tex}\Delta_f{/tex}H = -436 kJ/mol
N + N {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} N2; {tex}\Delta_f{/tex}H = -712 kJ/mol
{tex}\Delta_f{/tex}H(NH3) = {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\Delta{/tex}HN-N + {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}{tex}\Delta{/tex}HH-H - 3{tex}\Delta{/tex}HN-H
-46 = {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex}(712) + {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex}(436) - 3{tex}\Delta{/tex}HN-H
-3{tex}\Delta{/tex}HN-H = -1056
{tex}\Delta{/tex}HN-H = 352 kJ/mol

questions.question: The enthalpy of vapourization of liquid is 30 kJ mol-1 and entropy of vapourization is 75 J mol-1 K. The boiling point of the liquid at 1 atm is:
questions.question_raw: The enthalpy of vapourization of liquid is 30 kJ mol-1 and entropy of vapourization is 75 J mol-1 K. The boiling point of the liquid at 1 atm is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta S=\frac{\Delta Q_{\text {rev }}}{T}{/tex}; 75 = {tex}\frac{30 \times 10^{3}}{T}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} T = 400 K

questions.question: For which change $\Delta H \neq \Delta E$:
questions.question_raw: For which change {tex}\Delta H \neq \Delta E{/tex}:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}E + {tex}\Delta{/tex}nRT For {tex}\Delta{/tex}H {tex}\neq{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}E, {tex}\Delta{/tex}n {tex}\neq{/tex} 0
Where {tex}\Delta{/tex}n = no. of moles of gaseous products - no. of moles of gaseous reactants

  1. {tex}\Delta{/tex}n = 2 - 2 = 0
  2. {tex}\Delta{/tex}n = 0 ({tex}\because{/tex} they are either in solid or liquid state)
  3. {tex}\Delta{/tex}n = 1 - 1 = 0 ({tex}\because{/tex} C is in solid state)
  4. {tex}\Delta{/tex}n = 2 - 4 = -2

questions.question: Which of the following lines correctly shows the temperature dependence of equilibrium constant, K, for an exothermic reaction?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following lines correctly shows the temperature dependence of equilibrium constant, K, for an exothermic reaction?
questions.explanation:
From thermodynamics relation.
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = -RT ln K
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Ho - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}So = RT ln K
{tex}-\frac{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}}{\mathrm{RT}}+\frac{\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}}{\mathrm{RT}}{/tex} = ln K
or ln K = {tex}-\frac{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}}{\mathrm{RT}}+\frac{\Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}}{\mathrm{R}}{/tex}
For exothermic reaction, {tex}\Delta{/tex}Ho = -ve
slope = {tex}-\frac{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}}{\mathrm{R}}{/tex} = +ve
So from graph, lines should be  A & B.

questions.question: A gas undergoes change from state A to state B. In this process, the heat absorbed and work done by the gas is 5 J and 8 J, respectively. Now gas is brought back to A by another process during which 3 J of heat is evolved. In this reverse process of B to A:
questions.question_raw: A gas undergoes change from state A to state B. In this process, the heat absorbed and work done by the gas is 5 J and 8 J, respectively. Now gas is brought back to A by another process during which 3 J of heat is evolved. In this reverse process of B to A:

options.text: 6 J of the work will be done by the surrounding on gas.
10 J of the work will be done by the surrounding on gas.
6 J of the work will be done by the gas.
10 J of the work will be done by the gas.

questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta U_{A B}{/tex} = q + w = +5 + (-8) = -3
q = -3, {tex}\Delta U_{BA}{/tex} = +3
{tex}\Delta U_{A B}{/tex} = q + w
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 = -3 + w {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} w = +6 (work done on the system)

questions.question: The intermolecular potential energy for the molecules A, B, C and D given below suggests that:
questions.question_raw: The intermolecular potential energy for the molecules A, B, C and D given below suggests that:

options.text: A-A has the largest bond enthalpy
A-D has the shortest bond length
D is more electronegative than other atoms

questions.explanation: A-B bond has the highest intermolecular potential energy among the given molecules. Hence, it is the strongest bond and has maximum bond enthalpy.

questions.question: The difference between $\Delta$H and $\Delta$U for the formation of C6H12O6(S) from its elements at 27oC is ________ kJ.
questions.question_raw: The difference between {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and {tex}\Delta{/tex}U for the formation of C6H12O6(S) from its elements at 27oC is ________ kJ.
questions.explanation:
Reaction for the formation of glucose is,
6C(s) + 6H2(g) + 3O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C6H12O6(s)
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}U + {tex}\Delta{/tex}nRT
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - {tex}\Delta{/tex}U = {tex}\Delta{/tex}nRT
Number of moles of gaseous reactants = 6 + 3 = 9
Number of moles of gaseous products = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}n = 0 - 9 = -9
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - {tex}\Delta{/tex}U = {tex}\Delta{/tex}nRT = (-9) {tex}\times{/tex} 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 300
= -22.44 kJ

questions.question:
For the given process:
$\begin{gathered} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{s})} \\ \left(-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1 \mathrm{~atm}\right) \end{gathered}\longrightarrow\begin{gathered} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{l})} \\ \left(+20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1 \mathrm{~atm}\right) \end{gathered}$
Cp for ice = 9 cal deg-1 mol-1,
Cp for H2O = 18 cal deg-1 mol-1.
Latent heat of fusion of ice = 1440 cal mol-1 at 0oC.
Determine the net entropy change for the process in cal deg-1 mol-1.
questions.question_raw:
For the given process:
{tex}\begin{gathered} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{s})} \\ \left(-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1 \mathrm{~atm}\right) \end{gathered}\longrightarrow\begin{gathered} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{l})} \\ \left(+20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1 \mathrm{~atm}\right) \end{gathered}{/tex}
Cp for ice = 9 cal deg-1 mol-1,
Cp for H2O = 18 cal deg-1 mol-1.
Latent heat of fusion of ice = 1440 cal mol-1 at 0oC.
Determine the net entropy change for the process in cal deg-1 mol-1.
questions.explanation:
Three steps are required for the above conversion process:

  1. {tex}\Delta{/tex}S1 due to warming ice from -20oC (T1) to 0oC (T2)
  2. {tex}\Delta{/tex}S2 due to melting ice at 0oC
  3. {tex}\Delta{/tex}S3 due to warming liquid from 0oC (T2) to 20oC (T3)

Step 1: (using third law of thermodynamics)
{tex}\begin{gathered} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{s})} \\ \left(-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1 \mathrm{~atm}\right) \end{gathered}\longrightarrow\begin{gathered} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{s})} \\ \left(0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1 \mathrm{~atm}\right) \end{gathered}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S1 = nCp ln {tex}(\frac{T_2}{T_1}){/tex}
= 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} log10 {tex}(\frac{273}{253}){/tex}
= 0.683 cal deg-1 mol-1
Step 2: (using second law of thermodynamics)
{tex}\begin{gathered} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{s})} \\ \left(0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1 \mathrm{~atm}\right) \end{gathered}\longrightarrow\begin{gathered} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{l})} \\ \left(0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1 \mathrm{~atm}\right) \end{gathered}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S2 = {tex}\frac{q_{r e v}}{T}=\frac{1440}{273}{/tex} = 5.27 cal deg-1 mol-1
Step: 3 (using the third law of thermodynamics)
{tex}\begin{gathered} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{l})} \\ \left(0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1 \mathrm{~atm}\right) \end{gathered}\longrightarrow\begin{gathered} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{l})} \\ \left(20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1 \mathrm{~atm}\right) \end{gathered}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S3 = nCp ln {tex}(\frac{T_2}{T_1}){/tex}
= 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 18 {tex}\times{/tex} 2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} log {tex}(\frac{293}{273}){/tex}
= 1.271 cal deg-1 mol-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = {tex}\Delta{/tex}S1 + {tex}\Delta{/tex}S2 + {tex}\Delta{/tex}S3
= 0.683 + 5.27 + 1.271
= 7.22 cal deg-1 mol-1

questions.question: A particular gas cylinder that is used for camping weighs 14.3 kg when it is full and 8.3 kg when it is completely empty. The compressed gas used in the cylinder is butane (C4H10). If the daily consumption of a family is 35000 kJ of energy per day, then for how many days can a family plan a camping trip with a single gas cylinder? ($\Delta$cH of butane is -2658 kJ mol-1)
questions.question_raw: A particular gas cylinder that is used for camping weighs 14.3 kg when it is full and 8.3 kg when it is completely empty. The compressed gas used in the cylinder is butane (C4H10). If the daily consumption of a family is 35000 kJ of energy per day, then for how many days can a family plan a camping trip with a single gas cylinder? ({tex}\Delta{/tex}cH of butane is -2658 kJ mol-1)
questions.explanation:
Molecular mass of C4H10 = 58 g mol-1
= 0.058 kg mol-1
Weight of the gas = Weight of full cylinder - Weight of empty cylinder
= 14.3 - 8.3 kg
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Weight of the gas = 6 kg
The energy produced by 6 kg of C4H10
= {tex}\frac{6\times(-2658)}{0.058}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{15948}{0.058}{/tex} = 274965 kJ mol-1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} No. of days = {tex}\frac{274965}{35000}{/tex} = 7.8 {tex}\approx{/tex} 8 days

questions.question: The volume of 2 L gas is reduced to half from its original volume at 298 K. The specific heat of the gas shall ________.
questions.question_raw: The volume of 2 L gas is reduced to half from its original volume at 298 K. The specific heat of the gas shall ________.
questions.explanation: Specific heat of a substance is the quantity of heat required to raise the temperature of 1 g of the substance through 1oC. It is an intensive property; hence, it will remain unchanged even when the volume of the gas is changed.

questions.question: For a sample of perfect gas when its pressure is changed isothermally from Pi to Pf, the entropy change is given by ________.
questions.question_raw: For a sample of perfect gas when its pressure is changed isothermally from Pi to Pf, the entropy change is given by ________.
questions.explanation:
The entropy change of a system in a process is equal to the amount of heat transferred to it in a reversible manner divided by the temperature at which the process occurs.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}S = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{rev}}}{\mathrm{T}}{/tex}
Now, qrev for an isothermal change is given as:
qrev = -w = nRT ln{tex}\left(\frac{P_{i}}{P_{f}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}S = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{nRT} \ln \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}}\right)}{\mathrm{T}}{/tex} = nR ln{tex}\left(\frac{P_{i}}{P_{f}}\right){/tex}

questions.question: Cp - Cv = ________.
questions.question_raw: Cp - Cv = ________.

options.text: both 2 cal mol-1 deg-1 and 8.314 J mol-1 deg-1

questions.explanation:
Cp - Cv = R = 2 cal mol-1 deg-1
= 8.314 J mol-1 deg-1

questions.question: What is the difference in mass of two metals M1 and M2, if both are heated through same temperature difference and supplied with same amount of energy? Given: For M1, specific heat capacity is 0.65 J (oC)-1 g-1, for M2 specific, heat capacity is 0.22 J (oC)-1 g-1 and temperature raised for 20 g of M1 is 35oC to 400oC.
questions.question_raw: What is the difference in mass of two metals M1 and M2, if both are heated through same temperature difference and supplied with same amount of energy? Given: For M1, specific heat capacity is 0.65 J (oC)-1 g-1, for M2 specific, heat capacity is 0.22 J (oC)-1 g-1 and temperature raised for 20 g of M1 is 35oC to 400oC.
questions.explanation:
Energy needed for: (M1) = q1 = m1 {tex}\times{/tex} c1 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}T1
= 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.65 {tex}\times{/tex} (400 - 35)
= 4745 J
Energy needed for (M2) = q2 = m2 {tex}\times{/tex} c2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}T2
4745 = m2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.22 {tex}\times{/tex} (400 - 35)
({tex}\because{/tex} q1 = q2)
m2 = {tex}\frac{4745}{80.3} {/tex} = 59.09 g
{tex}\because{/tex} Mass of M1 = 20 g
Mass of M2 = 59.09 g
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mass of M2 - Mass of M1 = 59.09 - 20
= 39.09 g

questions.question: The molar heat capacity of an ideal monoatomic gas at constant volume is 12.45 J K-1 mol-1. The value of Cp for the gas is ________ cal K-1 mol-1.
questions.question_raw: The molar heat capacity of an ideal monoatomic gas at constant volume is 12.45 J K-1 mol-1. The value of Cp for the gas is ________ cal K-1 mol-1.
questions.explanation:
Cp - Cv = R
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Cp = R + Cv
= 8.314 J K-1 mol-1 + 12.45 J K-1 mol-1
= 20.76 J K-1 mol-1
= 20.76 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.239 cal K-1 mol-1
= 4.96 cal K-1 mol-1

questions.question: Consider the following hypothetical ideal gases. A2, B3, AB, A2B, C, D2, BD2, BD. Which of these gases (1 mole) will have the highest value of Cp - Cv?
questions.question_raw: Consider the following hypothetical ideal gases. A2, B3, AB, A2B, C, D2, BD2, BD. Which of these gases (1 mole) will have the highest value of Cp - Cv?

options.text: All the gases will have same value

questions.explanation:
Cp - Cv for monoatomic gases are given by,
Cp - Cv = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}R - {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}R = R ...(1)
Cp - Cv for diatomic gases are given by,
Cp - Cv = {tex}\frac{7}{2}{/tex}R - {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}R = R ...(2)
Cp - Cv for polyatomic gases are given by,
Cp - Cv = 4R - 3R = R ...(3)
From the above equations, we get that the value of Cp - Cv will be same for all the gases.

questions.question:
Select the TRUE statement:
 A calorimetric measurement at constant pressure of an exothermic reaction results in a negative heat of reaction.
 A calorimetric measurement at constant volume of an endothermic reaction results in positive values of work done.
 By using a bomb calorimeter, the energy changes associated with reactions are measured at constant pressure.
 Calorimeters are mainly used to determine the heat capacity of an unknown solution by carrying out measurements at constant volume and pressure.
questions.question_raw:
Select the TRUE statement:

  1. A calorimetric measurement at constant pressure of an exothermic reaction results in a negative heat of reaction.
  2. A calorimetric measurement at constant volume of an endothermic reaction results in positive values of work done.
  3. By using a bomb calorimeter, the energy changes associated with reactions are measured at constant pressure.
  4. Calorimeters are mainly used to determine the heat capacity of an unknown solution by carrying out measurements at constant volume and pressure.
questions.explanation: A calorimetric measurement at constant pressure of an exothermic reaction results in a negative heat of reaction.

questions.question: Select the CORRECT statement about the Gibbs energy.
questions.question_raw: Select the CORRECT statement about the Gibbs energy.

options.text: It is the energy available to do useful work.
It does not directly determine the spontaneity of a reaction.
It does not depend on the amount of matter present in the system.

questions.explanation: It is the energy available to do useful work.

questions.question: For a reaction at 27oC, $\Delta$H = -285.8 kJ mol-1, $\Delta$G = -236.9 kJ mol-1. What is the value of entropy change at 27oC for the reaction?
questions.question_raw: For a reaction at 27oC, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -285.8 kJ mol-1, {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -236.9 kJ mol-1. What is the value of entropy change at 27oC for the reaction?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S, T = 27 + 273 = 300 K
-236.9 = (-285.8) - (300) {tex}\Delta{/tex}S
= -0.163 kJ mol-1 K-1

questions.question: Which of the following conditions illustrates a reaction that is spontaneous at all temperatures?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following conditions illustrates a reaction that is spontaneous at all temperatures?
questions.explanation:
