questions.question:
Identify the enthalpy change for each process marked P, Q and R in the reactions given below.
AB(s) $\stackrel{{P}}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+}+\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}$
$\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})}^{+}+\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})}^{-}\stackrel{{Q}}{\longrightarrow} $ AB(s)
$\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})}^{+}+\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})}^{-} \stackrel{\mathrm{R}}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+}+\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}$
questions.question_raw:
Identify the enthalpy change for each process marked P, Q and R in the reactions given below.
AB(s) {tex}\stackrel{{P}}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+}+\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})}^{+}+\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})}^{-}\stackrel{{Q}}{\longrightarrow} {/tex} AB(s)
{tex}\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})}^{+}+\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})}^{-} \stackrel{\mathrm{R}}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+}+\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}{/tex}
questions.explanation:
P = {tex}\Delta{/tex}solHo
Q = {tex}\Delta{/tex}latticeHo
R = {tex}\Delta{/tex}hydHo

questions.question: For a given reaction, $\Delta$H = 35.5 kJ mol-1 and $\Delta$S = 83.6 J K-1 mol-1. The reaction is spontaneous at ________. (Assume that $\Delta$H and $\Delta$S do not vary with temperature)
questions.question_raw: For a given reaction, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 35.5 kJ mol-1 and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S = 83.6 J K-1 mol-1. The reaction is spontaneous at ________. (Assume that {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S do not vary with temperature)
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 35.5 kJ mol-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = 83.6 J K-1 mol-1
= 0.0836 kJ K-1 mol-1
Since, both {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S are positive, the reaction will be spontaneous at higher temperatures. The temperature at which change over between spontaneous and non-spontaneous behaviour occurs is calculated as:
T = {tex}\frac{\Delta \mathrm{H}}{\Delta \mathrm{S}}=\frac{35.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{0.0836 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{/tex} = 425 K
Thus, the given reaction is spontaneous at T > 425 K.

questions.question: If x g of octane is burned in excess of oxygen in a constant volume calorimeter and the temperature of the calorimeter rises from 21.0oC to 27.8oC, calculate the heat transferred to the calorimeter during the combustion process. [Given: heat capacity of calorimeter = 8.9 kJ (oC)-1]
questions.question_raw: If x g of octane is burned in excess of oxygen in a constant volume calorimeter and the temperature of the calorimeter rises from 21.0oC to 27.8oC, calculate the heat transferred to the calorimeter during the combustion process. [Given: heat capacity of calorimeter = 8.9 kJ (oC)-1]
questions.explanation:
The heat given off in the combustion reaction is absorbed by the calorimeter.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} q = Heat capacity of calorimeter {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}T
= 8.9 KJ (oC)-1 {tex}\times{/tex} (27.8 - 21.0) oC
= 60.52 kJ

questions.question: If for a certain reaction $\Delta$rH is 30 kJ mol-1 at 450 K, the value of $\Delta$rS (in J K-1 mol-1) for which the same reaction will be spontaneous at the same temperature is ________.
questions.question_raw: If for a certain reaction {tex}\Delta{/tex}rH is 30 kJ mol-1 at 450 K, the value of {tex}\Delta{/tex}rS (in J K-1 mol-1) for which the same reaction will be spontaneous at the same temperature is ________.
questions.explanation:
The reaction is spontaneous when T{tex}\Delta{/tex}rS > {tex}\Delta{/tex}rH
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}rS > {tex}\frac{\Delta_ rH}{T}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}rS > {tex}\frac{30000}{450}{/tex} = 66.67 J
{tex}\therefore{/tex} For reaction to be spontaneous, the value of {tex}\Delta{/tex}rS must be greater than 66.67 J.
Hence, this option is the correct answer.

questions.question:
If
 H2(g) + $\frac{1}{2}$O2(g) $\longrightarrow$ H2O(l); $\Delta$H = -240 kJ
 C6H10(l) + $\frac{17}{2}$O2(g) $\longrightarrow$ 6CO2(g) + 5H2O(l); $\Delta$H = -3800 kJ
 C6H12(l) + 9O2(g) $\longrightarrow$ 6CO2(g) + 6H2O(l); $\Delta$H = -3920 kJ
Calculate the heat of hydrogenation of cyclohexene.
questions.question_raw:
If

  1. H2(g) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H2O(l); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -240 kJ
  2. C6H10(l) + {tex}\frac{17}{2}{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 6CO2(g) + 5H2O(l); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -3800 kJ
  3. C6H12(l) + 9O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 6CO2(g) + 6H2O(l); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -3920 kJ

Calculate the heat of hydrogenation of cyclohexene.
questions.explanation:
Given:

  1. H2(g) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H2O(l); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -240 kJ ...(i)
  2. C6H10(l) + {tex}\frac{17}{2}{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 6CO2(g) + 5H2O(l); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -3800 kJ ...(ii)
  3. C6H12(l) + 9O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 6CO2(g) + 6H2O(l); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -3920 kJ ...(iii)

The required equation is,
C6H10(l) + H2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C6H12(l); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = ?
On adding equation (i) and (ii) and subtracting equation (iii) we get,
C6H10(l) + H2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C6H12(l)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = (-240) + (-3800) - (-3920)
= -120 kJ

questions.question:
Using the given values, calculate $\Delta$rSo at 298 K for the following reaction.
H2O(g) + CO(g) $\rightleftharpoons$ H2(g) + CO2(g)
 $\Delta$rHo (kcal mol-1)
 $\Delta$rGo (kcal mol-1)
 H2O(g)
 -57.8
 -54.64
 CO(g)
 -26.42
 -32.79
 H2(g)
 0
 0
 CO2(g) 
 -94.05
 -94.24
questions.question_raw:
Using the given values, calculate {tex}\Delta{/tex}rSo at 298 K for the following reaction.
H2O(g) + CO(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H2(g) + CO2(g)

  {tex}\Delta{/tex}rHo (kcal mol-1) {tex}\Delta{/tex}rGo (kcal mol-1)
H2O(g) -57.8 -54.64
CO(g) -26.42 -32.79
H2(g) 0 0
CO2(g)  -94.05 -94.24
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}rHo = 0 - 94.05 + 57.8 + 26.42
= -9.83 kcal/mol
{tex}\Delta{/tex}rGo = 0 - 94.24 + 54.64 + 32.79
= -6.81 kcal/mol
{tex}\Delta{/tex}rGo = {tex}\Delta{/tex}rHo - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}rSo
-6.81 = -9.83 - 298 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}rSo
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}rSo = -10.13 cal/mol

questions.question: If the bond enthalpy of H-H bond is 104 kcal mol-1. What will be the energy required to dissociate 6.5 g of gaseous hydrogen into free gaseous atoms?
questions.question_raw: If the bond enthalpy of H-H bond is 104 kcal mol-1. What will be the energy required to dissociate 6.5 g of gaseous hydrogen into free gaseous atoms?
questions.explanation:
Bond enthalpy for 1 mole of H2 = 104 kcal
Number of moles in 6.5 g of H2 = {tex}\frac{6.5}{2}{/tex}
= 3.25 moles
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Energy required for dissociation of 3.25 moles of H2 = 104 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.25 = 338 kcal

Enthalpies Values (kJ mol-1)

Standard bond enthalpy of O = O bond E2

Calculate the enthalpy change for the following reaction:
H2(g) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H2O(l)
questions.explanation:
H2(g) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H2O(l) ...(i)
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - HHo + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\Delta{/tex}O = OHo - 2{tex}\Delta{/tex}O - HHo
= E1 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}E2 - 2E3
H2O(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H2O(l) ...(ii)
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}_{2}^{\circ}{/tex} = -{tex}\Delta{/tex}vapH = -E4
The given reaction is,
H2(g) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H2O(l); {tex}\Delta{/tex}cHo = ?
This given reaction can be obtained by adding (i) and (ii)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}cHo = E1 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}E2 - 2E3 - E4

questions.question: Which of the following statements is CORRECT for a reversible process in a state of equilibrium?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following statements is CORRECT for a reversible process in a state of equilibrium?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}Go + RT ln Q ...(i)
At equilibrium {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = 0 and Q = K,
Thus equation (i) becomes,
0 = {tex}\Delta{/tex}Go + RT ln K
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}Go = -RT ln K = -2.303 RT log10 K

questions.question: The bond dissociation energies of X2, Y2 and XY are in the ratio of 1 : 0.5 : 1. $\Delta$H for the formation of XY is -200 kJ mol-1. The bond dissociation energy of X2 will be ________.
questions.question_raw: The bond dissociation energies of X2, Y2 and XY are in the ratio of 1 : 0.5 : 1. {tex}\Delta{/tex}H for the formation of XY is -200 kJ mol-1. The bond dissociation energy of X2 will be ________.
questions.explanation:
The ratio of bond dissociation energies of X2, Y2 and XY is 1 : 0.5 : 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H (X-X) = x, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H (Y-Y) = 0.5x, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H (X-Y) = x
The chemical equation for formation of XY is {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}X2 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}Y2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} XY
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\sum{/tex}n{tex}\Delta{/tex}H (reactant bonds) - {tex}\sum{/tex}n{tex}\Delta{/tex}H (product bonds)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} -200 = [{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H(X-X) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H(Y-Y)] - {tex}\Delta{/tex}H(X-Y)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} -200 = [{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}x + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5x] - x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 800 kJ mol-1

questions.question: If the standard Gibbs free energy ($\Delta$Go) for a reaction is 8.40 kJ at 25oC, then the equilibrium constant will be ________. (R = 8.314 J K-1 mol-1)
questions.question_raw: If the standard Gibbs free energy ({tex}\Delta{/tex}Go) for a reaction is 8.40 kJ at 25oC, then the equilibrium constant will be ________. (R = 8.314 J K-1 mol-1)
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Go = -RT ln K
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Go = -2303 RT log K
8.40 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 = -2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 298 {tex}\times{/tex} log K
8400 = -5705.8 {tex}\times{/tex} log K
log K = -1.472
K = 0.033

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a state function?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is NOT a state function?
questions.explanation: Heat supplied to the system is not a state function as during a process its value depends on the path followed. The value of free energy (Gibbs energy), internal energy, and entropy depends on the state and not on the path followed to get that state, hence these are state functions.

questions.question:
At standard conditions, if the change in the enthalpy for the following reaction is -109 kJ mol-1. H2(g) + Br2(g) $\longrightarrow$ 2HBr(g)
Given that bond energy of H2 and Br2 is 435 kJ mol-1 and 192 kJ mol-1, respectively, what is the bond energy (in kJ mol-1) of HBr?
questions.question_raw:
At standard conditions, if the change in the enthalpy for the following reaction is -109 kJ mol-1. H2(g) + Br2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2HBr(g)
Given that bond energy of H2 and Br2 is 435 kJ mol-1 and 192 kJ mol-1, respectively, what is the bond energy (in kJ mol-1) of HBr?
questions.explanation:
H2(g) + Br2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2HBr(g);
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -109 kJ mol-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}rH = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H (reactants) - {tex}\Delta{/tex}H (products)
= [{tex}\Delta{/tex}H(H-H) + {tex}\Delta{/tex}H(Br-Br)] - [2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H(Br-Br)] - 109 = [(435) + (192)] - 2{tex}\Delta{/tex}H(H-Br)
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H(H-Br) = 368 kJ mol-1

questions.question:
Consider the following reactions:
 $\mathrm{H}_{\text {(aq) }}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}_{\text {(aq) }}^{-}\to$ H2O(l); $\Delta$H = -y1 kJ mol-1
 H2(g) + $\frac{1}{2}$O2(g) $\to$ H2O(l); $\Delta$H = -y2 kJ mol-1
 C3H8(g) + 5O2(g) $\to$ 3CO2(g) + 4H2O(l) $\to$H = -y3 kJ mol-1
 CO2(g) + H2(g) $\to$ CO(g) + H2O(l) $\Delta$H = -y4 kJ mol-1
Enthalpy of formation for water in kJ mol-1 is ________.
questions.question_raw:
Consider the following reactions:

  1. {tex}\mathrm{H}_{\text {(aq) }}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}_{\text {(aq) }}^{-}\to{/tex} H2O(l); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -y1 kJ mol-1
  2. H2(g) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\to{/tex} H2O(l); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -y2 kJ mol-1
  3. C3H8(g) + 5O2(g) {tex}\to{/tex} 3CO2(g) + 4H2O(l) {tex}\to{/tex}H = -y3 kJ mol-1
  4. CO2(g) + H2(g) {tex}\to{/tex} CO(g) + H2O(l) {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -y4 kJ mol-1

Enthalpy of formation for water in kJ mol-1 is ________.
questions.explanation:
Enthalpy of formation refers to the amount of heat released or absorbed when 1 mole of a substance is directly obtained from its constituent elements. Thus, equation (ii) represents the formation of water molecules from its constituent elements.
Hence, {tex}\Delta{/tex}rH of water = -y2 kJ mol-1.

questions.question: 20 calories would be equal to ________.
questions.question_raw: 20 calories would be equal to ________.
questions.explanation:
1 cal = 4.184 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 ergs
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 20 cal = 4.184 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 83.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 ergs
= 8.36 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 ergs

questions.question: A gas is allowed to expand in a well-insulated container against a constant external pressure of 2.5 atm from an initial volume of 2.50 L to a final volume of 4.50 L. The change in internal energy ($\Delta$U) of the gas in joules will be ________.
questions.question_raw: A gas is allowed to expand in a well-insulated container against a constant external pressure of 2.5 atm from an initial volume of 2.50 L to a final volume of 4.50 L. The change in internal energy ({tex}\Delta{/tex}U) of the gas in joules will be ________.
questions.explanation:
Work done in an irreversible process is given by:
w = -pex (V2 - V1) = -2.5 (4.50 - 2.50)
= -5 L.atm = -5 {tex}\times{/tex} 101.3 J
= -506.5 J
As the system is insulated, no heat enters or leaves the system, i.e., q = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}U = q + w = 0 - 506.5 J
= -506.5 J {tex}\approx{/tex} -505 J

questions.question: The values of $\Delta$H and $\Delta$S of a certain reaction are -400 kJ mol-1 and -20 kJ mol-1 K-1 respectively. The temperature below which the reaction is spontaneous is ________.
questions.question_raw: The values of {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S of a certain reaction are -400 kJ mol-1 and -20 kJ mol-1 K-1 respectively. The temperature below which the reaction is spontaneous is ________.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -400 kJ mol-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = -20 kJ mol-1 K-1
Since both {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S are negative, the reaction will be spontaneous at lower temperatures.
The temperature at which change over between spontaneous and non-spontaneous behavior occurs is calculated as: T = {tex}\frac{\Delta \mathrm{H}}{\Delta \mathrm{S}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} T = {tex}\frac{-400 kJ mol^{-1}}{-20 kJ mol^{-1} K^{-1}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} T = 20 K
Thus, the given reaction is spontaneous below 20 K.

questions.question: During the isothermal reversible expansion of 1 mole of an ideal gas from 2 dm3 to 20 dm3 at 300 K, the work done is ______ kJ.
questions.question_raw: During the isothermal reversible expansion of 1 mole of an ideal gas from 2 dm3 to 20 dm3 at 300 K, the work done is ______ kJ.
questions.explanation:
Given process is isothermal reversible expansion.
n = 1, V1 = 2 dm3, V2 = 20 dm3
V2 = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} V1
For isothermal reversible expansion of 1 mol of an ideal gas if final volume (V2) is 10 times the initial volume (V1), then log10 {tex}(\frac{V_2}{V_1}){/tex} = 1
Therefore, w = - 2.303 RT {tex}\approx{/tex} -(19.15 {tex}\times{/tex} T) Joule {tex}\approx{/tex} -(4.6 {tex}\times{/tex} T) cal
w = -19.15 {tex}\times{/tex} T = -19.15 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 J
= -5745 J
= -5.745 kJ

questions.question: Work done on 2 moles of a perfect gas at 27oC, if it is compressed reversibly and isothermally from a pressure of 1.01 $\times$ 105 N m-2 to 5.05 $\times$ 106 Nm-2 is ________. [GivenL log10 0.025 = -1.6]
questions.question_raw: Work done on 2 moles of a perfect gas at 27oC, if it is compressed reversibly and isothermally from a pressure of 1.01 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 N m-2 to 5.05 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 Nm-2 is ________. [GivenL log10 0.025 = -1.6]
questions.explanation:
-1.95 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 J

Use the formula for work done in a reversible isothermal process:
- {tex}W=-n R T \ln \frac{P_1}{P_2}{/tex}

Substitute the given values:
- {tex}W=-2 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times \ln \frac{1.01 \times 10^5}{5.05 \times 10^6}{/tex}

Simplify the fraction:
- {tex}W=-2 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times \ln \frac{1}{50}{/tex}

Rewrite {tex}\frac{1}{50}{/tex} as 0.02 :
- {tex}W=-2 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times \ln 0.02{/tex}

Convert the natural logarithm to base-10 logarithm:
- {tex}W=-2 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times 2.303 \times \log _{10} 0.02{/tex}

Substitute the given value for {tex}\log _{10} 0.02{/tex} :
- {tex}W=-2 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times 2.303 \times(-1.6){/tex}

Calculate the work done:
- {tex}W=19150.8 {~J}{/tex}
- {tex}W \approx 1.92 \times 10^4 {~J}{/tex}

questions.question_raw: Which of the following {tex}\Delta{/tex}rH values reports the CORRECT standard enthalpy of combustion?
questions.explanation: The standard enthalpy of combustion refers to the combustion of one mole of substance when all the substances involved are in their standard states.

questions.question: Which of the following set of conditions are TRUE for an adiabatic free expansion of an ideal gas?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following set of conditions are TRUE for an adiabatic free expansion of an ideal gas?
questions.explanation:
In an adiabatic process, no exchange of heat takes place between the system and the surrounding
{tex}\therefore{/tex} q = 0 ...(1)
For free expansion process,
pex = 0
w = -pex{tex}\Delta{/tex}V
{tex}\therefore{/tex} w = 0 ...(2)
From the first law of thermodynamics,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}U = q + w ...(3)
Substituting the values of equation (1) and (2) in equation (3) we get,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}U = q + w = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} There is no change in internal energy. As a result, temperature remains constant i.e., {tex}\Delta{/tex}T = 0.

questions.question: For the reaction, 2Cl(g) $\longrightarrow$ Cl2(g), the CORRECT option is ________.
questions.question_raw: For the reaction, 2Cl(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cl2(g), the CORRECT option is ________.
questions.explanation:
In 2Cl(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cl2(g), bond is formed between two Cl atoms. Bond formation is exothermic reaction. Hence, {tex}\Delta{/tex}rH < 0.
In the reaction, the number of moles of gaseous product is higher than that of reactant. Hence, {tex}\Delta{/tex}rS < 0.

questions.question:
For the following two reactions:
 CH4(g) + 2O2(g) $\longrightarrow$ CO2(g) + 2H2O(g), $\Delta$H = -890.4 kJ
 Cr2O3(s) $\longrightarrow$ 2Cr(s) + $\frac{3}{2}$O2(g); $\Delta$H = +1130 kJ
Which among the following statements is CORRECT?
questions.question_raw:
For the following two reactions:

  1. CH4(g) + 2O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CO2(g) + 2H2O(g), {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -890.4 kJ
  2. Cr2O3(s) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Cr(s) + {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}O2(g); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = +1130 kJ

Which among the following statements is CORRECT?

options.text: Reaction (i) is exothermic and (ii) is endothermic
Reaction (i) is endothermic and (ii) is exothermic

questions.explanation: A negative value of {tex}\Delta{/tex}H indicates that the reaction is exothermic, while a positive value of {tex}\Delta{/tex}H indicates that the reaction is endothermic.

questions.question:
The heat evolved in the combustion of methane is given by the following reaction:
CH4(g) + 2O2(g) $\longrightarrow$ CO2(g) + 2H2O(l); $\Delta$H = -890.3 kJ
How many grams of methane would be required to produce 222.5 kJ of heat of combustion?
questions.question_raw:
The heat evolved in the combustion of methane is given by the following reaction:
CH4(g) + 2O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CO2(g) + 2H2O(l); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -890.3 kJ
How many grams of methane would be required to produce 222.5 kJ of heat of combustion?
questions.explanation:
1 mole of CH4 = 16 g
{tex}\because{/tex} 16 g of CH4 is required to produce 890.3 kJ of heat
{tex}\therefore{/tex} CH4 required to produce 222.5 kJ of heat = {tex}\frac{222.5 \times 16}{890.3}{/tex} = 4 g

questions.question:
Which of the following statements related to thermodynamics is CORRECT?
 Surroundings is the region of space not affected by changes in the system and from which it is physically separated.
 The boundary separating the isolated system from its surroundings prevents any exchange of matter, but not of energy.
 Thermodynamics deeply studies the mechanism of energy changes of a system and describes the on going processes.
 The universe, except for the system and the surroundings interacting with it, is not affected by changes in the system itself.
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following statements related to thermodynamics is CORRECT?

  1. Surroundings is the region of space not affected by changes in the system and from which it is physically separated.
  2. The boundary separating the isolated system from its surroundings prevents any exchange of matter, but not of energy.
  3. Thermodynamics deeply studies the mechanism of energy changes of a system and describes the on going processes.
  4. The universe, except for the system and the surroundings interacting with it, is not affected by changes in the system itself.
questions.explanation: The universe, except for the system and the surroundings interacting with it, is not affected by changes in the system itself.

questions.question_raw:

Units of Intensive property Units of Extensive property
i. atm iv. L

questions.explanation: The unit mol L-1 is the unit used for expressing the concentration of a substance, which is an intensive property. Hence, the unit mol L-1 is a misfit in the given list.

questions.question: The heat of combustion of carbon to CO2 is -393.5 kJ mol-1. The heat released upon formation of 35.2 g of CO2 from carbon and oxygen gas is ________.
questions.question_raw: The heat of combustion of carbon to CO2 is -393.5 kJ mol-1. The heat released upon formation of 35.2 g of CO2 from carbon and oxygen gas is ________.
questions.explanation:
C(s) + O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CO2(g); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -393.5 kJ
 The heat released in the formation of 1 mole (i.e., 44 g) of CO2 is -393.5 kJ
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Heat released in the formation of 35.2 g of CO2 = {tex}\frac{-393.5 \times 35.2}{44}{/tex} = -314.8 kJ
{tex}\approx{/tex} -315 kJ

questions.question: A system has an internal energy equal to U1. 600 J of work is done on the system, when 250 J of heat is released. The final internal energy of the system is
questions.question_raw: A system has an internal energy equal to U1. 600 J of work is done on the system, when 250 J of heat is released. The final internal energy of the system is
questions.explanation:
w = +600 J
{tex}\Delta{/tex}U = U2 - U1 = q + w
{tex}\therefore{/tex} U2 = U1 + (-250) + 600
{tex}\therefore{/tex} U2 = U1 + 350 J

questions.question: The heats of neutralization of four monobasic acids A, B, C and D are -13.7, -9.4, -11.2 and -12.4 kcals respectively. When they are neutralized by a common monoacidic base, which of the following acid shows the least acid strength?
questions.question_raw: The heats of neutralization of four monobasic acids A, B, C and D are -13.7, -9.4, -11.2 and -12.4 kcals respectively. When they are neutralized by a common monoacidic base, which of the following acid shows the least acid strength?
questions.explanation: The smaller the magnitude of negative heat of neutralization, the weaker is the acid. Hence, A is the strongest acid and B is the weakest acid. The negative sign shows that the reaction is exothermic.

questions.question: A normal person needs energy of about 3000 kcal per day. If a person is taking 4.5 moles of glucose every day as a diet, find how much energy he is producing from this, if calorific value of glucose = 15.78 kJ g-1.
questions.question_raw: A normal person needs energy of about 3000 kcal per day. If a person is taking 4.5 moles of glucose every day as a diet, find how much energy he is producing from this, if calorific value of glucose = 15.78 kJ g-1.
questions.explanation:
Moles of glucose = 4.5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mass of glucose = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 180 = 810 g
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Heat produced = 15.78 kJ g-1 {tex}\times{/tex} 810 g
= 12,781.8 kJ
1 kcal = 4.184 kJ
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 12,781.8 kJ = 3054.92 kcal {tex}\approx{/tex} 3055 kcal

questions.question: The volume of a gas decreases from 600 cc to 200 cc when a sample of an unknown gas is compressed by an average pressure of 0.4 atm. During this process, 10 J of heat is evolved. The change in internal energy is ________.
questions.question_raw: The volume of a gas decreases from 600 cc to 200 cc when a sample of an unknown gas is compressed by an average pressure of 0.4 atm. During this process, 10 J of heat is evolved. The change in internal energy is ________.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}V = 200 - 600 = -400 cc = -0.4 litre
Pressure (P) = 0.4 atm and heat liberated (q) = -10 J
Work done (w) = -P{tex}\Delta{/tex}V = -(0.4 {tex}\times{/tex} -0.4)
= 0.16 litre-atm
But, 1 litre-atm = 101.3 J
Hence, w = 0.16 {tex}\times{/tex} 101.3 = 16.208 J
Thus, {tex}\Delta{/tex}U = q + w = -10 + 16.208 = +6.2 J

questions.question: For the gaseous reaction, N2O4 $\longrightarrow$ 2NO2
questions.question_raw: For the gaseous reaction, N2O4 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2NO2
questions.explanation:
In the given reaction,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}n = No. of moles of gaseous products - No. of moles of gaseous reactants
= 2 - 1 = +1
We can use the value of {tex}\Delta{/tex}n (obtained from a balanced chemical equation) to predict the relationship between {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and {tex}\Delta{/tex}U of the reaction.

Case I Case II Case III
{tex}\Delta{/tex}n = 0 {tex}\Delta{/tex}n = +ve {tex}\Delta{/tex}n = -ve
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H - {tex}\Delta{/tex}U {tex}\Delta{/tex}H > {tex}\Delta{/tex}U {tex}\Delta{/tex}H < {tex}\Delta{/tex}U

 {tex}\Delta{/tex}n = +ve
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H > {tex}\Delta{/tex}U

questions.question: Consider the liquid vapour equilibrium, Liquid $\rightleftharpoons$ Vapour. Which of the following reactions is CORRECT?
questions.question_raw: Consider the liquid vapour equilibrium, Liquid {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Vapour. Which of the following reactions is CORRECT?
questions.explanation:
The Clausius-Clapeyron equation can be given as, P = {tex}\text { A. } e^\frac{-\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{v}}}{\mathrm{RT}}{/tex} ...(1)
Where, {tex}\Delta{/tex}vH or {tex}\Delta{/tex}vapH = Latent heat of vaporization, R = Universal gas constant. Taking natural logarithm on both sides of equation (1)
ln (P) = ln (A) + ln {tex}( e^\frac{-\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{v}}}{\mathrm{RT}}){/tex}
ln (P) = ln (A) - {tex}\frac{\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{v}}}{\mathrm{RT}}{/tex} ...(2)
Differentiating on both sides w.r.t. temperatures (T) we get,
{tex}\frac{d}{dT}{/tex} (ln P) = {tex}\frac{d}{dT}{/tex} ln (A) - {tex}\frac{d}{dT}\frac{\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{v}}}{\mathrm{RT}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d}{dT}{/tex} (ln P) = {tex}\frac{+\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{v}}}{\mathrm{RT^2}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \frac{\mathrm{d}\left(\mathrm{T}^{-1}\right)}{\mathrm{dT}}=\frac{-1}{\mathrm{~T}^{2}}\right]{/tex}

questions.question:
Given the following data:
Enthalpy of hydration = -784 kJ mol-1 and lattice enthalpy of NaCl = 788 kJ mol-1
Calculate the enthalpy of solution of sodium chloride.
questions.question_raw:
Given the following data:
Enthalpy of hydration = -784 kJ mol-1 and lattice enthalpy of NaCl = 788 kJ mol-1
Calculate the enthalpy of solution of sodium chloride.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}solH = {tex}\Delta{/tex}latticeH + {tex}\Delta{/tex}hydH
= +788 + (-784)
= +4 kJ mol-1

questions.question: A mixture of He and O2 are taken together in a container at 298 K. Which of the following statement is CORRECT regarding the entropy of the mixture?

options.text: There is an increase in entropy.

questions.explanation: The mixing of non-reacting gases increases randomness and so increases entropy.

questions.question: A piece of copper metal weighing 5 g was heated from 298 K to 300 K. If the heat required for the process is +3.85 J, calculate the specific heat capacity of copper.
questions.question_raw: A piece of copper metal weighing 5 g was heated from 298 K to 300 K. If the heat required for the process is +3.85 J, calculate the specific heat capacity of copper.
questions.explanation:
Heat capacity of copper
= {tex}\frac{\text { Heat absorbed }}{\text { Rise in temperature }}=\frac{3.85 \mathrm{~J}}{2 \mathrm{~K}}{/tex}
Specific heat capacity of copper
= {tex}\frac{\text { Heat capacity }}{\text { Mass (ing) }}=\frac{3.85 \mathrm{~J}}{2 \mathrm{~K} \times 5 \mathrm{~g}}{/tex} = 0.385 J g-1 K-1

questions.question: Which of the following statements regarding entropy is CORRECT?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following statements regarding entropy is CORRECT?

options.text: The value of entropy of solar system is constant.
Adsorption of gases on solid surfaces leads to an increase in entropy of the system.
For an adiabatic reversible process, the entropy change of the system is zero.
The total entropy of the universe is decreasing continuously.

questions.explanation: For an adiabatic reversible process, the entropy change of the system is zero.

questions.question:
Arrange the following in order of increasing entropy.
 1.0 mol H2O (gas, 373 K, 0.1 atm)
 1.0 mol H2O (liquid, 298 K, 1 atm)
 1.0 mol H2O (gas, 373 K, 2 atm)
questions.question_raw:
Arrange the following in order of increasing entropy.

  1. 1.0 mol H2O (gas, 373 K, 0.1 atm)
  2. 1.0 mol H2O (liquid, 298 K, 1 atm)
  3. 1.0 mol H2O (gas, 373 K, 2 atm)
questions.explanation: Under identical conditions, the smaller the pressure on the gas, the greater is its entropy.

questions.question: A Carnot engine operates between 500 K and 350 K. It absorbs 140 cal of heat from the source. Calculate heat rejected to the sink.
questions.question_raw: A Carnot engine operates between 500 K and 350 K. It absorbs 140 cal of heat from the source. Calculate heat rejected to the sink.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\eta=\frac{\mathrm{T}_{2}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{l}}}{\mathrm{T}_{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{500-350}{500}{/tex}
= 0.3 or 30%
Where, {tex}\eta{/tex} = efficiency of Carnot engine
We know that,
{tex}\eta=\frac{w}{q_{2}}=\frac{q_{2}-q_{1}}{q_{2}}{/tex}
Where, q2 = heat absorbed by the system from the source
q1 = heat rejected to the sink
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{140-q_{1}}{140}{/tex} = 0.3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 140 - q1 = 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 140
{tex}\therefore{/tex} q1 = 140 - 42
{tex}\therefore{/tex} q1 = 98 cal

questions.question: One mole of an ideal monoatomic gas was taken through isochoric heating from 100 K to 1000 K. Find the value of $\Delta$Stotal, $\Delta$Ssystem and $\Delta$Ssurr when the process is carried out reversibly.
questions.question_raw: One mole of an ideal monoatomic gas was taken through isochoric heating from 100 K to 1000 K. Find the value of {tex}\Delta{/tex}Stotal, {tex}\Delta{/tex}Ssystem and {tex}\Delta{/tex}Ssurr when the process is carried out reversibly.
questions.explanation:
For an isochoric process, {tex}\Delta{/tex}Sv = Cv ln{tex}\frac{T_2}{T_1}{/tex}
For monoatomic gas, Cv = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}R
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Ssystem = Cv ln{tex}\frac{T_2}{T_1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}R ln{tex}\frac{1000}{100}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}R ln 10
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Ssurr = -Cv ln{tex}\frac{T_2}{T_1}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{3}{2}{/tex}R ln {tex}\frac{1000}{100}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{3}{2}{/tex}R ln 10
For reversible process,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Stotal = {tex}\Delta{/tex}Ssystem + ({tex}\Delta{/tex}surr) = 0

questions.question: If the entropy change for the conversion of Ice $\rightleftharpoons$ liquid water at 0oC is 25.67 J K-1 mol-1. What will be the enthalpy change for the given process at 0oC?
questions.question_raw: If the entropy change for the conversion of Ice {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} liquid water at 0oC is 25.67 J K-1 mol-1. What will be the enthalpy change for the given process at 0oC?
questions.explanation:
For the conversion, Ice {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} liquid water,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}fusS = {tex}\frac{\Delta_{\text {fus }} \mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{T}}{/tex}
25.67 = {tex}\frac{\Delta_{\text {fus }} \mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{273}}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}fusH = 7007.9 J mol-1 = 7.00 kJ mol-1

{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}U {tex}\Delta{/tex}H > {tex}\Delta{/tex}U {tex}\Delta{/tex}H < {tex}\Delta{/tex}U

{tex}\Delta{/tex}U = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H, when {tex}\Delta{/tex}N = 0
In the equation (C),
H2(g) + Cl2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2HCl(g),

Monoatomic gas Diatomic gas Triatomic gas (non-linear)

questions.question: Given the following diagram, determine the value of $\Delta$H5.
questions.question_raw: Given the following diagram, determine the value of {tex}\Delta{/tex}H5.
questions.explanation:
By using Hess’s law,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H1 = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H2 + {tex}\Delta{/tex}H3
Now, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H2 = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H4 + {tex}\Delta{/tex}H5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H1 = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H4 + {tex}\Delta{/tex}H5 + {tex}\Delta{/tex}H3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H5 = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H1 - {tex}\Delta{/tex}H4 - {tex}\Delta{/tex}H3
= -890 - (-203) - (-283)
= -404 kJ

questions.question: Consider the following diagrams that show a cylindrical automobile piston. As the gas expands against a constant external pressure of 3.2 atm, calculate the work done in each stroke of the piston.
questions.question_raw: Consider the following diagrams that show a cylindrical automobile piston. As the gas expands against a constant external pressure of 3.2 atm, calculate the work done in each stroke of the piston.

questions.question: The above graph represents the isothermal reversible and irreversible work. Which of the following interpretation is NOT CORRECT with reference to above graph?
questions.question_raw: The above graph represents the isothermal reversible and irreversible work. Which of the following interpretation is NOT CORRECT with reference to above graph?

options.text: For the same increase in volume, Wrev > Wirrev
The area YZ V1V2 represents work done due to isothermal irreversible expansion
Reversible work of compression is the maximum work
Smooth curve of XY represent isothermal reversible expansion

questions.explanation: Reversible work of expansion (Wrev) is the maximum work. For reversible expansion, external pressure (Pex) should be infinitesimally smaller than internal pressure. In other words, Pex is the maximum possible value of the pressure. Therefore, for a given change of volume, W is maximum. Thus, Wrev = Wmax. Similarly, it can be explained that the reversible work of compression is the minimum work.

w = -106841.05 J
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Magnitude of work obtained = 106841.05 J
Now, w = mgh
106841.05 = m {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 150

Given

  1. {tex}2 \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \Delta \mathrm{H}_1^\theta=-\mathrm{xkJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\mathrm{C} (graphite) +\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \Delta \mathrm{H}_2^\theta=-\mathrm{ykJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}

The{tex} \Delta \mathrm{H}^\theta{/tex} for the reaction {tex}\mathrm{C}( graphite )+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g}) {/tex} is

{tex} \mathrm{C} \text { (graphite) }+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \text { (g) } \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g}) {/tex} ...(i) {tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}{/tex}

questions.question:
Given that:
 $ \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} H^{\circ} \text { of } \mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O} \text { is } 82 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
 Bond energies of N $\equiv $ N, N = N, O = O and N = O are 946, 418, 498 and 607 kJ mol-1 respectively,
The resonance energy of N2O is:
questions.question_raw:
Given that:

  1. {tex} \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} H^{\circ} \text { of } \mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O} \text { is } 82 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}{/tex}
  2. Bond energies of N {tex}\equiv {/tex} N, N = N, O = O and N = O are 946, 418, 498 and 607 kJ mol-1 respectively,

The resonance energy of N2O is:
questions.explanation:
{tex} \mathrm{N}_2(\mathrm{~g})+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g}){/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{N} \equiv \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{g})+\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{O}=\mathrm{O}) \rightarrow \underset{\mathrm{N}}{\overline{\mathrm{N}}}=\stackrel{+}{\mathrm{N}}=\ddot{\mathrm{O}}(\mathrm{g}){/tex}
{tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} H^{\circ}{/tex} = [Energy required for breaking of bonds] - [Energy released for forming of bonds]
{tex}=\left(\Delta H_{\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{N}}+\frac{1}{2} \Delta H_{\mathrm{O}=\mathrm{O}}\right)-\left(\Delta H_{\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{N}}+\Delta H_{\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{O}}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\left(946+\frac{1}{2} \times 498\right)-(418+607){/tex} = 170 kJ mol−1
Resonance energy = observed {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} H^{\circ}{/tex} - calculated {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} H^o{/tex} 82 - 170 = -88 kJ mol-1.

questions.question:
Which of the following relations are correct?
 $\Delta U$ = q + p$\Delta V$
 $\Delta G = \triangle H - T \Delta S$
 $\Delta S = \frac {q_{rev }} {T}$
 $\Delta H = \Delta U - \Delta nRT$
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following relations are correct?

  1. {tex}\Delta U{/tex} = q + p{tex}\Delta V{/tex}
  2. {tex}\Delta G = \triangle H - T \Delta S{/tex}
  3. {tex}\Delta S = \frac {q_{rev }} {T}{/tex}
  4. {tex}\Delta H = \Delta U - \Delta nRT{/tex}
questions.explanation:
B and C only

(B) {tex}{G}={H}-{TS}{/tex}

At constant T

{tex} \Delta G=\Delta H-T \Delta S{/tex}

(A)First law is given by

{tex} \Delta U=Q+W{/tex}

If we apply constant {tex}P{/tex} and reversible work.

{tex} \Delta U=Q-P \Delta V{/tex}

(C) By definition of entropy change dqrev

{tex} {dS}={dq}_{{rev}} / {T}{/tex}

At constant T rev

{tex} \Delta {S}={q}_{{rev}} / {T}{/tex}

(D) {tex}{H}={U}+{PV}{/tex}

For ideal gas

{tex} {H}={U}+{nRT}{/tex}

At constant T

{tex} \Delta H=\Delta U+\Delta n R T{/tex}

questions.question:
At 25oC and 1 atm pressure, the enthalpies of combustion are as given below:
$\begin{array}{llll} \text { Substance } & \mathrm{H}_2 & \mathrm{C} \text { (graphite) } & \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_6(\mathrm{~g}) \end{array}$
$\frac{\Delta_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}}{\mathrm{kjmol}^{-1}} \quad-286.0 \quad-394.0 \quad \cdot \quad-1560.0$
The enthalpy of formation of ethane is
questions.question_raw:
At 25oC and 1 atm pressure, the enthalpies of combustion are as given below:
{tex}\begin{array}{llll} \text { Substance } & \mathrm{H}_2 & \mathrm{C} \text { (graphite) } & \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_6(\mathrm{~g}) \end{array}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\Delta_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}}{\mathrm{kjmol}^{-1}} \quad-286.0 \quad-394.0 \quad \cdot \quad-1560.0{/tex}
The enthalpy of formation of ethane is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_6(\mathrm{~g})+\frac{7}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) {/tex}{tex}\longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}){/tex}
Heat of combustion
{tex}=\sum \Delta_f H_{\text {(products) }}-\sum \Delta_f H_{\text {(reactants })}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{c}} H\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_6, \mathrm{~g}\right){/tex} = {tex}2 \Delta \mathrm{c} H(\mathrm{C}, \text { graphite }){/tex}+{tex}3 \Delta_{\mathrm{c}} H\left(\mathrm{H}_2, \mathrm{~g}\right)-\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} H{/tex}
{tex}\left(\mathrm{O}_2, \mathrm{~g}\right)-\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} H\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_6, \mathrm{~g}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-1560{/tex} = {tex}2(-394)+3(-286)-0-\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} H\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_6, \mathrm{~g}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} H\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_6, \mathrm{~g}\right)=-86 {/tex} {tex}{KJ} {/tex} {tex}\mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}.

questions.question:
Change in enthalpy for reaction, 2H2O2(l) $\rightarrow$ 2H2O(l) + O2(g)
If heat of formation of H2O2(l) and H2O(l) are -188 and -286 kJ/mol respectively is:
questions.question_raw:
Change in enthalpy for reaction, 2H2O2(l) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2H2O(l) + O2(g)
If heat of formation of H2O2(l) and H2O(l) are -188 and -286 kJ/mol respectively is:
questions.explanation:
2H2O2(l) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2H2O(l) + O2(g) {tex}\Delta{/tex}Hr = ?
H2(g) + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2O2(l); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -188 kJ mol ...(1)
H2(g) + {tex}\frac12{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2O(l); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -286 kJ/mol ...(2)
Subtract eqn. (1) from eqn. (2)
H2O2(l) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2H2O(l) + {tex}\frac12{/tex}O2(g)
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -286 - (-188) = -98 kJ mol-1
Multiply this equation by 2 to get the required equation
2H2O2(l) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2H2O(l) + O2(g); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 2 (-98) kJ/mol
= -196 kJ/mol

questions.question_raw: If for a certain reaction {tex}\Delta{/tex}H is 30 kJ mol-1 at 450 K, the value of {tex}\Delta{/tex}S (in JK-1 mol-1) for which the same reaction will be spontaneous at the same temperature is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
for spontaneity, {tex}\Delta{/tex}G < 0
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S <
30000 < 450 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}S
66.67 < {tex}\Delta{/tex}S
So, {tex}\Delta{/tex}S should be more than 66.67. Therefore, 70 is the correct answer here.

questions.question:
From the given reactions,
S(s) + $\frac32$O2(g) $\rightarrow$ SO3(g) + 2x kcal
SO2(g) + $\frac12$O2(g) $\rightarrow$ y kcal
The heat of formation of SO2 is:
questions.question_raw:
From the given reactions,
S(s) + {tex}\frac32{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} SO3(g) + 2x kcal
SO2(g) + {tex}\frac12{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} y kcal
The heat of formation of SO2 is:
questions.explanation:
S(s) + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} SO2(g); {tex}\Delta{/tex}Hf = ?
S(s) + {tex}\frac32{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} SO3(g); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 2x kcal
SO2(g) + {tex}\frac12{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} SO3(g); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = y kcal
S(s) + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} SO2(g); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 2x - y kcal

questions.question: For an ideal solution, the correct option is
questions.question_raw: For an ideal solution, the correct option is

options.text: $\Delta_{mix}$ S = 0 at constant T and P
$\Delta_{mix}$ V $\neq$ 0 at constant T and P
$\Delta_{mix}$ H = 0 at constant T and P
$\Delta_{mix}$ G = 0 at constant T and P

questions.explanation: For an ideal solution at constant T and P, {tex}\Delta_{mix}{/tex} H = 0, {tex}\Delta_{mix}{/tex} S > 0, {tex}\Delta_{mix}{/tex} G < 0 and {tex}\Delta_{mix}{/tex} V = 0.

questions.question:
Given that,
C(s) + O2(g) $\rightarrow$ CO2(g), $\Delta$Ho = -x kJ
2CO(g) + O2(g) $\rightarrow$ 2CO2(g), $\Delta$Ho = -y kJ
The enthalpy of formation of carbon monoxide will be:
questions.question_raw:
Given that,
C(s) + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CO2(g), {tex}\Delta{/tex}Ho = -x kJ
2CO(g) + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2CO2(g), {tex}\Delta{/tex}Ho = -y kJ
The enthalpy of formation of carbon monoxide will be:
questions.explanation:
C(s) + {tex}\frac12{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CO(g), {tex}\Delta{/tex}Hr = ?
2 {tex}\times{/tex} C(s) + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CO2(g), {tex}\Delta{/tex}Ho = -2x kJ
-2CO(g) + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2CO2(g), {tex}\Delta{/tex}Ho = -y kJ
2C(s) + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2CO(g), {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -2x - (-y) kJ
= y - 2x kJ
Divide the above resultant equation by 2 to get required equation
C(s) + {tex}\frac12{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CO(g), {tex}\Delta{/tex}Hr = {tex}\frac{y-2 x}{2} \mathrm{~kJ}{/tex}

questions.question: For the reaction, C3H8(g) + 5O2(g) $\rightarrow$ 3CO2(g) + 4H2O(l) at constant temperature $\Delta$H - $\Delta$E is:
questions.question_raw: For the reaction, C3H8(g) + 5O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 3CO2(g) + 4H2O(l) at constant temperature {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - {tex}\Delta{/tex}E is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}E + {tex}\Delta{/tex}ng RT
{tex}\Delta{/tex}ng = 3 - (1 + 5) = -3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}E + -3RT
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H - {tex}\Delta{/tex}E = -3RT

questions.question:
This question has two correct options. The correct thermodynamic conditions for the spontaneous reaction at all temperatures is:
 $\Delta$H < 0 and $\Delta$S > 0
 $\Delta$H < 0 and $\Delta$S < 0
 $\Delta$H < 0 and $\Delta$S = 0
 $\Delta$H > 0 and $\Delta$S < 0
questions.question_raw:
This question has two correct options. The correct thermodynamic conditions for the spontaneous reaction at all temperatures is:

  1. {tex}\Delta{/tex}H < 0 and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S > 0
  2. {tex}\Delta{/tex}H < 0 and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S < 0
  3. {tex}\Delta{/tex}H < 0 and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S = 0
  4. {tex}\Delta{/tex}H > 0 and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S < 0
questions.explanation:
According to Gibbs free energy,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
For the spontaneous reaction, {tex}\Delta{/tex}G must be negative. If {tex}\Delta{/tex}H < 0 and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S > 0
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -ve - T = -ve
Thus, at all temperatures {tex}\Delta{/tex}G is -ve hence, reaction is spontaneous.
If {tex}\Delta{/tex}H < 0 and S < 0
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -ve - T (-ve)
Thus, at higher value of temperature, {tex}\Delta{/tex}G is +ve hence, non-spontaneous.
If {tex}\Delta{/tex}H > 0 and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S < 0
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = +ve - T(-ve)
Thus, {tex}\Delta{/tex}G is the +ve for all temperatures and reaction is non-spontaneous.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is correct for the spontaneous absorption of a gas?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following statements is correct for the spontaneous absorption of a gas?

options.text: $\Delta$S is negative and therefore, $\Delta$H should be highly positive
$\Delta$S is positive and therefore, $\Delta$H should be negative.
$\Delta$S is negative and therefore, $\Delta$H should be highly negative.
$\Delta$S is positive and therefore, $\Delta$H should also be highly positive.

questions.explanation:
For adsorption of the gas its entropy decreases that is {tex}\Delta{/tex}S is negative. Now, the adsorption is spontaneous, too that means, change in Gibbs, energy {tex}\Delta{/tex}G should be negative. According to the relation,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
or {tex}\Delta{/tex}G + T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H
-ve + T(-ve) = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H
Thus, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H should be highly negative.

questions.question: If, $\Delta$E is the heat of reaction for C2H5OH(l) + 3O2(g) $\rightarrow$ 2CO2(g) + 3H2O(l) at constant volume, the $\Delta$H (heat of reaction at constant pressure), then the correct relation is:
questions.question_raw: If, {tex}\Delta{/tex}E is the heat of reaction for C2H5OH(l) + 3O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2CO2(g) + 3H2O(l) at constant volume, the {tex}\Delta{/tex}H (heat of reaction at constant pressure), then the correct relation is:
questions.explanation:
As we know, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}E + {tex}\Delta{/tex}ngRT
Now, {tex}\Delta{/tex}ng = Number of gaseous moles of products - number of gaseous moles of reactions
= 2 - 3 = -1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}E + (-1)RT
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}E - RT

questions.question: A reaction occurs spontaneous if:
questions.question_raw: A reaction occurs spontaneous if:

options.text: T$\Delta$S > $\Delta$H and both $\Delta$H is +ve and $\Delta$S are -ve

questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
For spontaneous reaction, {tex}\Delta{/tex}G has to be negative Among the given options, it is positive only when T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S > {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and both {tex}\Delta{/tex}H and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S are +ve.

questions.question: For the reactions, N2 + 3H2 $\rightarrow$ 2NH3, $\Delta$H = ?
questions.question_raw: For the reactions, N2 + 3H2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2NH3, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = ?
questions.explanation:
For the given reaction,
N2(g) + 3H2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2NH3(g)
{tex}\Delta{/tex}ng = 2 - (1 + 3) = -2
Now, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}E + {tex}\Delta{/tex}ng RT
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}E - 2RT

questions.question: If $\Delta$H is the change is enthalpy and $\Delta$E, the change in internal energy accompanying a gaseous reaction, then
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\Delta{/tex}H is the change is enthalpy and {tex}\Delta{/tex}E, the change in internal energy accompanying a gaseous reaction, then

options.text: $\Delta$H < $\Delta$E only if the number of moles of products is greater than the number of moles of the reactants.
$\Delta$H < $\Delta$E only if the number of moles of products is less than the number of moles of the reactants.

questions.explanation:
As we know, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}E + {tex}\Delta{/tex}ngRT
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H < {tex}\Delta{/tex}ng only when {tex}\Delta{/tex}ng is negative.
Now, {tex}\Delta{/tex}ng = Number of gaseous moles of products number of gaseous moles of reactants.
Thus, {tex}\Delta{/tex}ng is negative only when number of gaseous moles of products is less than that of moles of reactants.

questions.question: At standard conditions, if the change in the enthalpy for the following reaction is -109 kJ mol-1 H2(g) + Br2(g) $\rightarrow$ 2HBr(g) Given that bond energy of H2 and Br2 is 435 kJ mol-1 and 192 kJ mol-1, respectively, what is the bond energy (in kJ mol-1) of HBr?
questions.question_raw: At standard conditions, if the change in the enthalpy for the following reaction is -109 kJ mol-1 H2(g) + Br2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2HBr(g) Given that bond energy of H2 and Br2 is 435 kJ mol-1 and 192 kJ mol-1, respectively, what is the bond energy (in kJ mol-1) of HBr?
questions.explanation:
2{tex}\Delta{/tex}HHBr - {tex}\Delta{/tex}HH2 - {tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{Br}_2}{/tex} = -109
2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}HHBr + 435 + 192 = -109
2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -6627 - 109
2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -736
{tex}\Delta{/tex}HHBr = -368 kJ/ mole
So, bond energy of H - Br bond is 368 kJ/ mole.

questions.question: The densities of graphite and diamond at 298 K are 2.25 and 3.31 g cm-3, respectively. If the standard free energy difference ($\Delta$Go) is equal to 1895 J mol-1, the pressure at which graphite will be transformed into diamond at 298 K is:
questions.question_raw: The densities of graphite and diamond at 298 K are 2.25 and 3.31 g cm-3, respectively. If the standard free energy difference ({tex}\Delta{/tex}Go) is equal to 1895 J mol-1, the pressure at which graphite will be transformed into diamond at 298 K is:
questions.explanation:
C(graphite) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C(diamond) {tex}\Delta{/tex}Go = - P{tex}\Delta{/tex}V
Volume of graphite = {tex}\frac{\text { Mass of } 1 \text { mole }}{\text { Density }}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{12 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{2.25 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}}{/tex} = 5.33 cm3 mol-1
Volume of diamond = {tex}\frac{\text { Mass of } 1 \text { mole }}{\text { Density }}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{12 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{3.31 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}}{/tex} = 3.63 cm3 mol-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}V = Vgraphite - Vdiamond
= 1.70 cm3 mol-1
= 1.70 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 L mo-1
So, {tex}\Delta{/tex}Go = P{tex}\Delta{/tex}V
1895 J mol-1 = P(1.70 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 L mol-1)
1114.70 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 J = P
1114.70 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 J/L = P
1114.70 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 {tex}\times{/tex} 101.33 atm = P
P = 112952 atm {tex}\times{/tex} 1.013 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 Pa
= 114420.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 P
= 114420.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 P
= 11.44 {tex}\times{/tex} 109 Pa
Approximately {tex}\approx{/tex} 9.92 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 Pa

questions.question: The bond dissociation energies of X2, Y2 and XY are in the ratio of 1 : 0.5 : 1. $\Delta$H for the formation of XY is -200 kJ mol-1. The bond dissociation energy of X2 will be
questions.question_raw: The bond dissociation energies of X2, Y2 and XY are in the ratio of  1 : 0.5 : 1. {tex}\Delta{/tex}H for the formation of XY is -200 kJ mol-1. The bond dissociation energy of X2 will be
questions.explanation:
Let bond energies of X2, Y2 and XY are x kJ mol-1, 0.5x kJ mol-1 and x kJ mol-1 respectively.
{tex}\frac{1}{2} X_2+\frac{1}{2} Y_2 {/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} XY; {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -200 kJ mol-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -200 = {tex}\Sigma{/tex}(B.E)Reactant - {tex}\Sigma{/tex}(B.E)Product
= {tex}\left[\frac{1}{2} \times(x)+\frac{1}{2} \times(0.5 x)\right]{/tex} - [1 {tex}\times{/tex} (x)]
B.E. of X2 = x = 800 kJ mol-1

questions.question:
From the following bond energies
H - H bond energy: 431.37 kJ mol-1
C = C bond energy: 606.10 kJ mol-1
C - C bond energy: 336.49 kJ mol-1
C - H bond energy: 410.50 kJ mol-1
Enthalpy for the reaction,
will be:
questions.question_raw:
From the following bond energies
H - H bond energy: 431.37 kJ mol-1
C = C bond energy: 606.10 kJ mol-1
C - C bond energy: 336.49 kJ mol-1
C - H bond energy: 410.50 kJ mol-1
Enthalpy for the reaction,

will be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Hreaction = {tex}\Sigma{/tex}(Bond enthalpy)reactants - {tex}\Sigma{/tex}(Bond enthalpy)Product
= [(B.E)c=c + 4(B.E)C-H + (B.E.)H-H] - [(B.E)C-C + 6(B.E)H-H]
= [606.10 + 4(410.50) + 431.37] - [336;49 + 6(410.50)]
= 2679.47 - 2799.49
= -120.02 kJ mol-1

questions.question:
The factor of $\Delta$G values is important in metallurgy. The $\Delta$G values for the following reactions at 800oC are given:
S2(s) + 2O2(g) $\rightarrow$ 2SO2(g), $\Delta$G = -544 kJ
2Zn(s) + S2(s) $\rightarrow$ 2ZnS(s), $\Delta$G = -293 kJ
The $\Delta$G for the reaction
2ZnS(s) $\rightarrow$ 3O2(g) $\rightarrow$ 2Zn(s) + 2SO2(g) will be:
questions.question_raw:
The factor of {tex}\Delta{/tex}G values is important in metallurgy. The {tex}\Delta{/tex}G values for the following reactions at 800oC are given:
S2(s) + 2O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2SO2(g), {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -544 kJ
2Zn(s) + S2(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2ZnS(s), {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -293 kJ
The {tex}\Delta{/tex}G for the reaction
2ZnS(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 3O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Zn(s) + 2SO2(g) will be:
questions.explanation:
S2(s) + 2O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2SO2(g), {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -544 kJ
+2Zn(s) + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2ZnO(s), {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -480 kJ
S2(s) + 2Zn(s) + 3O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2SO2(g) + 2ZnO(g), {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = (-544 - 480) kJ = -1024 kJ
S2(s) + 2Zn(s) + 3O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2SO2(g) + 2ZnO(g), {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -1024 kJ
2Zn(s) + S2(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2ZnS(s), {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -293 kJ
2Zn(s) + 3O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} SO2(g) + 2ZnO(g), {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -1024 - (-293) kJ = -731 kJ

questions.question: The entropy change in the fusion of one mole of a solid melting at 27oC (latent heat of fusion is 2930 J mol-1) is:
questions.question_raw: The entropy change in the fusion of one mole of a solid melting at 27oC (latent heat of fusion is 2930 J mol-1) is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}S = {tex}\frac{\Delta \mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{2930 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{(27+273) \mathrm{K}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2930 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{300 \mathrm{~K}}{/tex} = 9.77 JK-1mol-1

questions.question: Bond dissociation enthalpy of H2, Cl2 and HCI are 434, 242 and 431 kJ mol-1 respectively. Enthalpy of formation of HCl is:
questions.question_raw: Bond dissociation enthalpy of H2, Cl2 and HCI are 434, 242 and 431 kJ mol-1 respectively. Enthalpy of formation of HCl is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_2 {/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} HCl {tex}\Delta{/tex}Hf = ?
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Hreaction = {tex}\left[\frac{1}{2}(\text { B.E. })_{\mathrm{H}_2}+\frac{1}{2}(\text { B.E. })_{\mathrm{Cl}_2}\right]-(\text { B.E. })_{\mathrm{HCl}}{/tex}
= [217 + 121] - 431
= 338 - 431 = -93 kJ mol-1

questions.question: Given the following entropy values (in JK-1 mol-1) at 298 K and 1 atm H2(g) : 130.6, Cl2(g) : 223.0, HCI(g) : 186.7. The entropy change (in JK-1 mol-1) for the reaction H2(g) + Cl2(g) $\rightarrow$ 2HCI(g), is:
questions.question_raw: Given the following entropy values (in JK-1 mol-1) at 298 K and 1 atm H2(g) : 130.6, Cl2(g) : 223.0, HCI(g) : 186.7. The entropy change (in JK-1 mol-1) for the reaction H2(g) + Cl2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2HCI(g), is:
questions.explanation:
Entropy change for a reaction can be given as follows:
{tex}\Delta S^{\circ}{ }_{\text {reaction }}=\Sigma S^{\circ}{ }_{\text {products }}-\Sigma S_{\text {reactants }}^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}=2 \times \mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{HCl}}^{\circ}=\left(\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{H}_2}^{\circ}+\mathrm{S}^{\circ} \mathrm{Cl}_2\right){/tex}
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 186.7 - (130.6 + 223.0)
= 373.4 - (353.6)
= 19.8 Jk-1 mol-1

questions.question: Given that bond energies of H-H and CI-CI are 430 kJ mol-1 and 240 kJ mol-1 respectively and $\Delta$Hf for HCl is -90 kJ mol-1. Bond enthalpy of HCI is:
questions.question_raw: Given that bond energies of H-H and CI-CI are 430 kJ mol-1 and 240 kJ mol-1 respectively and {tex}\Delta{/tex}Hf for HCl is -90 kJ mol-1. Bond enthalpy of HCI is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac12{/tex}H2 + {tex}\frac12{/tex}Cl2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} HCl
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Hf = -90 kJ mol-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Hf = [{tex}\frac12{/tex}(B.E.){tex}H_2{/tex} + {tex}\frac12{/tex}(B.E.)HCl
-90 = [{tex}\frac12{/tex}(430) + {tex}\frac12{/tex}(240)] - (B.E.)HCl
-90 = [215 + 120] - (B.E.)HCl
(B.E.)HCl = 335 + 90 = 425 kJ mol-1

questions.question: According to the third law of thermodynamics which one of the following quantities for a perfectly crystalline solid is zero at absolute zero?
questions.question_raw: According to the third law of thermodynamics which one of the following quantities for a perfectly crystalline solid is zero at absolute zero?
questions.explanation: Entropy for a perfectly crystalline solid is zero at absolute zero. This statement is also called the third law of thermodynamics.

questions.question: The correct relationship between free energy and equilibrium constant Kof a reaction is:
questions.question_raw: The correct relationship between free energy and equilibrium constant Kof a reaction is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}Go = -RT InK
Q = reaction quotient
At equilibrium Q becomes equilibrium constant, K and at equilibrium {tex}\Delta{/tex}G= 0
Thus, 0 = {tex}\Delta{/tex}Go + RT In K
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Go = -RT InK

questions.question:
Consider the following reactions
 H+(aq) + OH-(aq) $\rightarrow$ H2O(l), - x1 kJ mol-1
 H2(g) + $\frac12$O2(g) $\rightarrow$ H2O(l), - x2 kJ mol-1
 CO2(g) + H2(g) $\rightarrow$ CO(g) + H2O(l), - x3 kJ mol-1
 C2H2(g) + $\frac52$O2(g) $\rightarrow$ 2CO2(g) + H2O(l), - x4 kJ mol-1
Enthalpy of formation of H2O(l) is:
questions.question_raw:
Consider the following reactions

  1. H+(aq) + OH-(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2O(l), - x1 kJ mol-1
  2. H2(g) + {tex}\frac12{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2O(l), - x2 kJ mol-1
  3. CO2(g) + H2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CO(g) + H2O(l), - x3 kJ mol-1
  4. C2H2(g) + {tex}\frac52{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2CO2(g) + H2O(l), - x4 kJ mol-1

Enthalpy of formation of H2O(l) is:
questions.explanation:
Chemical equation for the formation of H2O(l) is
H2(g) + {tex}\frac12{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2O(l)
Because enthalpy of formation of a compound is the heat absorbed or released when one mole of this substance is formed from its constituent elements. Thus, enthalpy of formation of H2O is -x2 kJ mol-1 where negative sign shows that the reaction is exothermic.

questions.question: A chemical reaction will be spontaneous if it is accompanied by a decrease in:
questions.question_raw: A chemical reaction will be spontaneous if it is accompanied by a decrease in:
questions.explanation: Spontaneity of a reaction depends on the Gibbs free energy change of the reaction. If Gibbs free energy of the system decreases i.e {tex}\Delta{/tex}G is negative then only the reaction is spontaneous.

questions.question: The enthalpy of combustion of Hz, cyclohexene (C6H10) and cyclohexane (C6H12) are -241, -3800 and -3920 k] per mol respectively. Heat of hydrogenation of cyclohexene is:
questions.question_raw: The enthalpy of combustion of Hz, cyclohexene (C6H10) and cyclohexane (C6H12) are -241, -3800 and -3920 k] per mol respectively. Heat of hydrogenation of cyclohexene is:
questions.explanation:
H2 + {tex}\frac12{/tex}O2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2O;  {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = - 241 kJ m-1 ...(1)

{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -3800 kJ mol-1 ...(2)

{tex}\Delta{/tex} = -3920 kJ mol-1 ...(3)
Add eqn. (1) and (2) and subtract eqn. (3) from it, we get

{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = [(-241- 3800) - (-3920)] kJ mol-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -4041 + 3920 kJ mol-1
= -121 kJ mol-1

questions.question: The absolute enthalpy of neutralization of the reaction MgO(s) + 2HCl(aq) $\rightarrow$ MgCl2(aq) + H2O(l) will be:
questions.question_raw: The absolute enthalpy of neutralization of the reaction MgO(s) + 2HCl(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} MgCl2(aq) + H2O(l) will be:
questions.explanation: Enthalpy of neutralization of a strong acid and a strong base is -57.33 kJ mol-1. Here, MgO is the weak base and HCl is a strong acid thus, a small amount of energy is used in the ionization of MgO thus, the heat of neutralization decreases. Therefore, enthalpy of neutralization is less than -57.33 kJ mol-1.

questions.question: If the bond energies of H - H, Br - Br and H - Br are 433, 192 and 364 kJ mol-1 respectively, then $\Delta$Ho for the reaction H2(g) + Br2(g) $\rightarrow$ 2HBr(g) is:
questions.question_raw: If the bond energies of H - H, Br - Br and H - Br are 433, 192 and 364 kJ mol-1 respectively, then {tex}\Delta{/tex}Ho for the reaction H2(g) + Br2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2HBr(g) is:
questions.explanation:
H2(g) + Br2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2HBr(g) {tex}\Delta{/tex}Hof = ?
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Hof = {tex}\Sigma{/tex}(B.E.)reactants - {tex}\Sigma{/tex}(B.E.)Products
= (B.E.)H - H + (B.E.)Br - Br -2(B.E.)H - Br
= [433 + 192] - 2(364) kJ mol-1
= (625 -728) kJ mol-1 = -103 kJ mol-1

questions.question: For which one of the following equation $\Delta \mathbf{H}_f^{\circ}$ equal to $\Delta \mathbf{H}_f^{\circ}$ for the product?
questions.question_raw: For which one of the following equation {tex}\Delta \mathbf{H}_f^{\circ}{/tex} equal to {tex}\Delta \mathbf{H}_f^{\circ}{/tex} for the product?
questions.explanation:
Heat of formation, {tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}_f^{\circ}{/tex} of a substance is the amount of heat absorbed or released when one mole of this substance is formed directly from its constituent elements.
In option (Xe(g) + 2F2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} XeF4(g)), one mole of XeF4 is formed from its constituent elements i.e., Xe and F2 thus, the equation has equal value of {tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}_r^{\circ}{/tex} and {tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}_f^{\circ}{/tex}. In option (2CO(g) + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2CO2(g)), the constituent atoms should be carbon and oxygen only but the reactant used is CO thus, {tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}_r^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\neq{/tex} {tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}_f^{\circ}{/tex}
In option (N2(g) + 3O(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} N2O3(g)), the reactant used is O3 which is again not in its element form thus,
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}_r^{\circ} \neq \Delta \mathrm{H}_f^{\circ}{/tex}.
In option (CH4(g) + 2Cl2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CH2Cl2(l)  + 2HCl(g)), two products are formed thus
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}_r^{\circ} \neq \Delta \mathrm{H}_f^{\circ}{/tex}

questions.question: Heat of combustion $\Delta$Ho for C(s), H2(g) and CH(g) are -94, 68 and -213 kcal/mol. Then, $\Delta$Ho for C(s) + 2H2(g) $\rightarrow$ CH4(g) is:
questions.question_raw: Heat of combustion {tex}\Delta{/tex}Ho for C(s), H2(g) and CH(g) are -94, 68 and -213 kcal/mol. Then, {tex}\Delta{/tex}Ho for C(s) + 2H2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CH4(g) is:
questions.explanation:
C(s) + 2H2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CH4(g) = ?
C(s) + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CO2(g); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = - 94 kcal/mol ...(1)
H2(g) + {tex}\frac12{/tex} O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2O(g); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = - 68 kcal/mol ...(2)
CH4(g) + 2O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CO2(g) + 2H2O(l); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -213 kcal/mol ...(3)
Eqn. (1) + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} Eqn. (2) - Eqn (3)
C(s) + 2H2(O) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CH4(g)
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Ho = [-94 - 2(68)] - (-213) kcal/mol
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Ho = [-94 - 136] + 213 kcal/mol
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Ho = -230 + 213 = -17 kcal/mol

questions.question: Enthalpy of the reaction, CH4 + $\frac12$O2 $\rightarrow$ CH3OH, is negative. If enthalpy of combustion of CH4 and CH3OH are X and Y respectively, then which relation is correct?
questions.question_raw: Enthalpy of the reaction, CH4 + {tex}\frac12{/tex}O2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CH3OH, is negative. If enthalpy of combustion of CH4 and CH3OH are X and Y respectively, then which relation is correct?
questions.explanation:
CH4 + {tex}\frac12{/tex}O2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CH3OH
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Hr = -ve
CH4 + 2O2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CO2 + 2H2O; {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = x ...(1)
CH3OH + {tex}\frac32{/tex}O2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CO2 + 2H2O; {tex}\Delta{/tex}x = y ...(2)
Eqn. (1) - Eqn. (2)
CH4 + {tex}\frac12{/tex}O2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CH3OH; {tex}\Delta{/tex}Hr = x - y = -ve
Thus, x < y

questions.question: For a reaction A $\rightarrow$ B, enthalpy of reaction is -4.2 kJ mol-1 and enthalpy of activation is 9.6 kJ mol-1. The correct potential energy profile for the reaction is shown in option.
questions.question_raw: For a reaction A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B, enthalpy of reaction is -4.2 kJ mol-1 and enthalpy of activation is 9.6 kJ mol-1. The correct potential energy profile for the reaction is shown in option.
questions.explanation:
From {tex}\Delta{/tex}Hrxn = (Ea)f - (Ea)b
-4.2 = 9.6 - (Ea)b
(Ea)b = 9.6 + 4.2 = 13.8 kJ/mol.
Here (Ea)b is highly positive and the reaction is exothermic in nature.

questions.question: For irreversible expansion of an ideal gas under isothermal condition, the correct option is
questions.question_raw: For irreversible expansion of an ideal gas under isothermal condition, the correct option is
questions.explanation:
For spontaneous process, {tex}\Delta{/tex}Stotal > 0 and since irreversible is always spontaneous
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}Stotal > 0
For isothermal process, {tex}\Delta{/tex}T = 0. So, {tex}\Delta{/tex}U = 0

questions.question: A liquid when kept inside a thermally insulated closed vessel at $25^{\circ} {C}$ was mechanically stirred from outside. What will be the correct option for the following thermodynamic parameters?
questions.question_raw: A liquid when kept inside a thermally insulated closed vessel at {tex}25^{\circ} {C}{/tex} was mechanically stirred from outside. What will be the correct option for the following thermodynamic parameters?
questions.explanation:
A thermally insulated closed vessel having {tex}q=0{/tex}, as it is mechanically stirred from outside.
{tex}\therefore w \gt 0{/tex} because work is done on the vessel.
Similarly, from First law of thermodynamics
{tex}\Delta U=q+w{/tex}
As {tex}q=0{/tex}, so {tex}\Delta {U}=w{/tex}
As {tex}w \gt 0{/tex}, so {tex}\Delta U \gt 0{/tex}

questions.question: Two vessels A and B are connected via stopcock. The vessel A is filled with a gas at a certain pressure. The entire assembly is immersed in water and is allowed to come to thermal equilibrium with water. After opening the stopcock, the gas from vessel A expands into vessel B and no change in temperature is observed in the thermometer. Which of the following statement is true?
questions.question_raw: Two vessels A and B are connected via stopcock. The vessel A is filled with a gas at a certain pressure. The entire assembly is immersed in water and is allowed to come to thermal equilibrium with water. After opening the stopcock, the gas from vessel A expands into vessel B and no change in temperature is observed in the thermometer. Which of the following statement is true?

options.text: The pressure in the vessel $B$ before opening the stopcock is zero.

questions.explanation:
After opening the stopcock, the gas from vessel A expands into vessel B and no change in the temperature is observed in the thermometer that indicates there is no pressure present in the vessel B due to which the gas readily expands from vessel A to B.
As
{tex}d T=0 \therefore d U=0{/tex}
{tex}d U=0 \therefore d q=0{/tex}
From the first law of thermodynamics,
{tex}d U=d q+d w{/tex}
{tex}\text { So, } d w=0{/tex}

questions.question: Work done by a Carnot engine operating between temperatures $127^{\circ} {C}$ and $27^{\circ} {C}$ is 2 kJ. The amount of heat transferred to the engine by the reservoir is:
questions.question_raw: Work done by a Carnot engine operating between temperatures {tex}127^{\circ} {C}{/tex} and {tex}27^{\circ} {C}{/tex} is 2 kJ. The amount of heat transferred to the engine by the reservoir is:
questions.explanation:
Efficiency of a carnot engine is given by
{tex}\eta=1-\frac{{T}_{2}}{{~T}_{1}}=\frac{{W}}{{Q}_{1}}{/tex}
where {tex}{T}_{1}=127+273=400 {~K}{/tex}
{tex}{T}_{2}=27+273=300 {~K}{/tex}
Therefore,
{tex}\frac{{W}}{{Q}_{1}}=1-\frac{{T}_{2}}{{~T}_{1}}=1-\frac{300}{400}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 \times 10^{3} {~J}}{{Q}_{1}}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow Q_{1}=8 \times 10^{3} {~J}=8 {~kJ}{/tex}
